keyQuotations.quote: [Our] experience has shown that the real work in molding specifications comes after it has been directed for use... in order for the specifications for Burden Reduction to mature at a meaningful pace, ONC must provide a mandate via a certification program.
Market validation must precede standardization.

One-sided requirements – such as the requirement in the rescinded Interoperability and Prior Authorization rule that required impacted payers to build Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) – will not result in the broad adoption necessary to achieve the benefits of ePA.
Technology and standards without a guiding 'north star' may lead to getting lost at sea.

Under-resourced health systems and practices currently suffer the most in terms of prior authorization burden. They are also the least likely to be able to afford the implementation expenses of electronic prior authorization.

A certification program can also hinder when it is initiated too soon because guidance is not yet mature, when not all actors in the workflow are all validated on their conformance but rather only one actor is...

Different rules for different segments of the industry would complicate adoption and create more administrative burden for payers and providers... having different requirements for prior authorization and attachments under ONC authorities and HIPAA is likely to confuse the industry and thus stall adoption.
As a healthcare provider, it is unbelievable to watch these plans enjoy the fruits of the wild wild west with little regulation, accountability, and oversight.

Without appropriate incentives or mature and tested standards, a voluntary Certification Program will fail to have any measurable impact on the prior authorization problems plaguing the health care system.

These standards only work when both sides of the partnership are held by certification to the same standard and there is penalty for non-adherence. For now, all of the burden falls on the practice team and takes away from providing clinical care.
It is time for CMS and ONC to take action and — once and for all — put patients over paperwork.

Although the payer is technically regulated by CMS and not ONC, both sides need to be compatible to achieve integrated care delivery. There must be consistency and reciprocity across the EHR and payer sides.
Moving forward at a well-regulated speed is a strong recommendation.
If a payer doesn’t implement ePA for receiving and sending, then it becomes a problem for providers and patients.
Providers desperately need this functionality but will be deprived of these important burden-reducing features if ONC delays.

sections.executiveSummary: Public comment on payer-side reforms reveals a near-universal consensus among providers that inconsistent, opaque, and ever-changing health plan policies are the primary drivers of prior authorization (PA) burden and patient harm. While payers defend PA as an essential quality and cost-control tool, an overwhelming majority of providers, their associations, and patient advocates call for federal mandates to standardize clinical rules, increase transparency, and hold plans accountable for the delays and costs they create, arguing that technical solutions will fail without addressing these fundamental operational problems.

consensusPoints.text: Payer policies are inconsistent, opaque, and constantly changing, creating the primary source of PA burden. Moffitt Cancer Center details how requirements are inconsistent across insurance companies and even change within the same payer for different plans, and there is "not a reliable and uniform methodology to determine which services require authorization." The American Hospital Association (AHA) notes that plans vary widely on accepted submission methods, forcing providers to use idiosyncratic proprietary plan portals that reduce, rather than improve, efficiency. The Medical Group Management Association (MGMA) states that "differing requirements, many of which change suddenly without adequate notice to practices, and the inconsistencies and manual nature of the process, result in inefficient communication and delays in patient care."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Virtually every commenter from a provider, health system, or professional association background (over 50 commenters) described this as the central problem.

exceptions.text: Payer organizations like CareFirst and Providence Health Plan assert they provide clarity through provider manuals and portals, though providers see these as part of the fragmented problem.

consensusPoints.text: Technical standards for electronic prior authorization (ePA) will be ineffective without fundamental reforms to payer business practices. UCSF’s Center for Digital Health Innovation warns that "successful deployment of technical standards into PA would expose the real drivers of burdens: the volume of authorizations, frequency of denials, delays in approvals, and ever-changing requirements and rules," which are payer-side issues. The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons (AAHKS) states that "improving health IT functionality without addressing the urgent, underlying issues associated with payor prior authorization policies would further build upon a broken system." The Regulatory Relief Coalition (RRC) concludes, "unless and until payer PA criteria are standardized, e-PA efficiencies are likely to be ephemeral."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, including providers, health IT vendors, and advocacy groups, stressed this point.

exceptions.text: No commenters explicitly disagreed, though some focused more narrowly on the benefits of technical implementation without linking it to broader reform.

consensusPoints.text: Payer responses to PA requests must be timely, transparent, and clinically actionable. The American College of Physicians (ACP) insists that for ePA to be useful, "the response from the payer must contain actionable information," including precisely what documentation is needed for a reversal if a decision is negative. ASCO recommends payers provide "very specific reasons for denials" and explanations for pending requests, including the cause of delay. Dana-Farber Cancer Institute reports frequently receiving "generic denial responses" like "The service did not meet medical policy," which are unhelpful and require follow-up.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all provider-side commenters and many health IT vendors emphasized this need.

areasOfDebate.description: A deep divide exists on whether prior authorization serves primarily as a cost-control measure that harms patients or as an essential tool for ensuring care quality and safety.

positions.label: Burdensome Gatekeeping
positions.stance: PA is a cost-control tactic used by payers to delay, deny, and discourage medically necessary care, externalizing costs onto providers and harming patients. Representative voices include the Alliance of Specialty Medicine, South Carolina Hospital Association, and a New York urologist.
positions.supportLevel: The overwhelming majority of providers, specialty societies, and patient advocacy groups.
keyArguments: Payers manufacture delays and denials (ASTRO).
The purpose is to "avoid or delay payment for services" (SCHA).
The process "endangers patient safety" for cost savings (The Michael J. Fox Foundation).

positions.label: Essential Safety Tool
positions.stance: PA is a crucial mechanism to ensure care is safe, effective, and evidence-based, preventing unnecessary treatments and managing costs. Representative voices include CVS Health, AHIP, and WEDI.
positions.supportLevel: Payers and their associations.
keyArguments: It is an "essential and effective tool" for quality (CVS Health).
Payers rely on evidence-based guidelines to design programs that improve quality and affordability (WEDI citing an AHIP survey).

areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders disagree on whether payers must first standardize their processes before technology can be implemented, or if all parties must work collaboratively from the start.

positions.label: Payers Must Act First
positions.stance: Payers must standardize their requirements and adopt modern technology before providers and IT vendors can effectively implement ePA solutions. Representative voices include Allscripts (Health IT Vendor), Greenway Health (Health IT Vendor), and NASL (Provider/IT Association).
positions.supportLevel: Most health IT vendors and many provider groups.
keyArguments: Without payer alignment upfront, vendor development is a "massive" effort with likely rework (Allscripts).
New certification for health IT won't solve the problem without payer standardization first (Greenway Health).

positions.label: Collaborative Alignment
positions.stance: All stakeholders (providers, payers, vendors) must align and work in partnership to realize the benefits of ePA. Representative voices include Point-of-Care Partners (Consulting Firm), Anthem (Payer), and American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA) (Informatics Association).
positions.supportLevel: Some payers, consulting firms, and informatics associations.
keyArguments: ONC and CMS should work to "actively align requirements for providers, payers, and HIT vendors" (Point-of-Care Partners).
Payers need to "partner and collaborate with the informatics communities" (AMIA).

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers (Hospitals, Health Systems, Medical Practices)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient care delays leading to harm; extreme administrative burden and cost; physician and staff burnout; opaque and constantly shifting payer rules.
specificPoints: They provide vivid, ground-level examples of system failures, such as payers downgrading an already-approved inpatient stay to outpatient, forcing re-authorization (Trinity Health), or payers authorizing treatment for patients who don't have benefits for the service (Dana-Farber).

Proposed solutions include mandating standardization of PA criteria across all payers; requiring payers to be transparent about what requires PA and what documentation is needed; and mandating 24/7/365 processing and timely, actionable responses.

Key examples: BJC HealthCare calls for "concise and consistent PA standards for all payers." Moffitt Cancer Center details extreme variability in PA requirements even within a single payer's different plans.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The cumulative impact of PA burden on medical specialties; erosion of physician autonomy; patient safety risks from "fail-first" policies and treatment delays; lack of clinical expertise in payer review processes.
specificPoints: They aggregate the experiences of thousands of members, often citing survey data on the escalating burden (TMA) and framing the issue as a systemic threat to patient care and the medical profession.
Proposed solutions include comprehensive PA reform beyond technology; exempting guideline-based care from PA (ADCC); and requiring payers to consult specialty societies on policy development (ACOG).

The Alliance of Specialty Medicine highlights concerns about "inexperienced unknowledgeable ‘physicians’" at insurance companies making complex treatment decisions. The American College of Cardiology points to the added complexity of third-party PA vendors with their own unique portals and rules.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining PA as a tool for quality control and cost management; the high cost and effort of implementing new ePA standards; receiving incomplete or incorrect data from providers.
specificPoints: They defend PA as a necessary function to ensure evidence-based care and prevent fraud, waste, and abuse. They argue that they invest significant resources in developing appropriate policies (CVS Health) and are already working to streamline processes (UnitedHealth Group).

Proposed solutions include allowing compliance costs for new standards to be counted as Quality Improvement Activities (QIA) in Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) calculations (Anthem, AHIP); and requiring providers to submit complete and accurate data (PCMA).
PCMA states, "incomplete data from providers delays the timely processing of PA requests." Anthem and AHIP both propose the MLR accounting change to offset implementation costs.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The infeasibility and high cost of developing software for unstandardized payer requirements; the risk of being blamed for failures caused by payer-side issues; the need for payers to adopt modern technology like FHIR.
specificPoints: They are caught between providers demanding a single, streamlined workflow and payers who each have different rules and legacy systems. They articulate the technical impossibility of creating an efficient system on top of a chaotic foundation.

Proposed solutions include mandating that payers adopt the standards first to create a stable target for development (Greenway); and requiring payers to provide a single, standardized gateway for PA submissions (Epic).

Allscripts warns, "Absent standardization, development work would be massive and likely require future rework." Epic notes that payer customization made even the older X12 278 standard infeasible for providers to implement widely.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Automation May Expose, Not Solve, the Problem: UCSF CDHI astutely points out that effective ePA technology won't eliminate burden but will instead "expose the real drivers of burdens: the volume of authorizations, frequency of denials, delays in approvals, and ever-changing requirements and rules," shifting the focus squarely onto payer policies.

Payer "Gotcha" Tactics: Trinity Health provides a compelling example of payers authorizing an inpatient stay but then retroactively downgrading it to outpatient, forcing the provider to re-seek authorization for the procedures, which may then be denied. This illustrates a specific, burdensome tactic that goes beyond simple inefficiency.

Fragmentation of Care Plans: The Alliance of Dedicated Cancer Centers (ADCC) highlights a unique harm where payers authorize a care plan based on guidelines but then mandate that components (e.g., imaging, pharmacy) be performed at different, potentially lower-quality, facilities, fragmenting care and undermining the treating physician's integrated approach.

The Power of Denials: The Michael J. Fox Foundation cites HHS OIG data showing Medicare Advantage plans overturn their own denials 75% of the time, providing powerful evidence that the initial denial process is often flawed and serves as a barrier that many patients and providers cannot overcome, even if the service is ultimately deemed necessary.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Provider-Payer Divide: There is a stark, philosophical chasm. Providers view PA as an antagonistic, harmful, and financially motivated barrier. Payers view it as a prudent, necessary tool for ensuring quality and value. This fundamental disagreement underlies all other debates.

Specialty-Specific Harms: Cancer centers (Moffitt, ADCC, Dana-Farber) and other specialists (cardiology, rheumatology, psychiatry) articulate more acute harms than generalists, focusing on delays for time-sensitive treatments, fragmentation of complex care regimens, and decisions made by non-specialist reviewers.

Health IT Vendor as "Canary in the Coal Mine": Health IT vendors (Epic, Allscripts, Greenway) are uniquely positioned between payers and providers. Their universal plea for payer-side standardization first serves as a powerful indicator that the current ecosystem is too fragmented to support a scalable, efficient electronic solution.

Unintended Consequences of Automation: Several commenters, most notably the New York urologist and UCSF, warn that automating a broken process could make things worse by allowing payers to issue even more low-value PAs at a lower cost to themselves, increasing the overall burden on the system.

keyQuotations.quote: [PA is used in ways that are] shameful, if not criminal. I have patients that have been hospitalized and almost died due to the delays imposed by prior authorizations and inexperienced unknowledgeable ‘physicians’...making decisions on complex rheumatologic treatments being given to seriously ill rheumatology patients.

The systems, with layer upon layer of middlemen and ever-changing rules and processes, have become so complex... To all who suffer the consequences, it often feels like intentional denial of care is the goal.
We would argue that the purpose of this process is not to manage care, but to avoid or delay payment for services ordered by the patient’s physician.
Without payer alignment at the front end, a new certification criterion for health IT products on the back-end won’t solve the problem of streamlining the prior authorization process.
Technological standardization alone is not enough to lift the unnecessary burdens that impede, complicate and add stress to patients who simply want to access the care they need.
Unless and until payer PA criteria are standardized, e-PA efficiencies are likely to be ephemeral.

There are situations where payers authorize inpatient services which include procedures and then subsequently downgrade the authorized level of care to outpatient. As a result, the payers then require that providers seek reauthorization of the procedure... Upon seeking re-authorization, the procedure may be denied requiring costly appeal work. This is disruptive and unfair for patients and providers.
Payers authorize a course of chemotherapy but not at the facility where the oncologist practices, instead at a separate facility or retail pharmacy.

Health plans 'internalize' financial benefits of prior authorization and 'externalize' costs by imposing them on providers, creating a 'limitless incentive for PAs' that automation will only worsen without reform.

Prior authorization is an essential and effective tool to ensure that beneficiaries receive necessary, effective treatment while also preventing unnecessary and ineffective treatments, adverse medical events, and costs.
Psychiatrists attempting to address emergent issues with a patient require a faster turnaround from payers via ePA to address patients who are in acute crisis.
Ultimately, how PA is currently used frequently endangers patient safety and often defies physician recommendations for appropriate care to protect potential cost savings for health plans.
Keeping track of different prior authorization and utilization review requirements among all plan types is cumbersome and confusing.
First and foremost, the Agencies must establish concise and consistent PA standards for all payers that providers and patients and their families can easily access, understand, and navigate.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on provider burden is one of universal and profound frustration. A near-unanimous chorus of providers, professional associations, and even payers and vendors agrees that the current prior authorization (PA) system is a leading contributor to administrative waste, workforce burnout, and practice unsustainability. While electronic PA (ePA) is viewed as a potential solution, this optimism is heavily conditioned by deep skepticism. The key tension is whether ePA will genuinely reduce burden or simply automate a broken process, shifting uncompensated work into the clinical workflow and creating new costs and complexities. Dominant recommendations demand national standardization, rigorous real-world testing before mandates, and seamless integration that supports—rather than disrupts—existing clinical workflows.

consensusPoints.text: The current prior authorization process imposes an unsustainable administrative and financial burden on providers. The American Hospital Association (AHA) cited specific examples of the massive financial cost, stating, "One 17-hospital system spends $11 million annually just complying with health plan prior authorization requirements," and "A single 355-bed psychiatric facility needs 24 full-time staff members to deal with prior authorizations." The American Medical Association (AMA) provided widely-cited survey data that "Physicians and their staff spend an average of almost two business days (13 hours) each week completing prior authorizations" and that "88% of physicians describe PA burden as high or extremely high." UnityPoint Health, a large integrated provider, detailed the human resource cost: "UnityPoint Health employs more than 60 FTEs to verify and obtain prior authorizations for their Iowa providers alone."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Virtually every single commenter (over 60 commenters) across all stakeholder groups—providers, associations, vendors, and even payers—acknowledged the severe burden of current PA processes.

exceptions.text: The only partial exception was a DMEPOS supplier association (AAHomecare) that saw some benefits in reducing audits, but even they noted the burden of inconsistent payer requirements.

consensusPoints.text: Provider and staff burnout is at crisis levels, and PA is a major contributing factor. Novant Health stated, "At a time when provider and staff burnout and nursing and clinical workforce shortages are at critical levels after two years of the COVID-19 pandemic, reducing unnecessary burden must receive the utmost prioritization from policymakers." The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) noted, "In an informal survey of ACOG members, ACOG found that prior authorization for services has been noted as one of the top drivers of job dissatisfaction and administrative burden." Ascension connected the process directly to burnout: "Today, these processes contribute to clinician burnout by forcing them to spend considerable time and effort engaging in manual or semi-automated administrative processes attempting to meet each payer's unique requirements."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly provider organizations and professional associations (over 30 commenters), explicitly linked PA burdens to the ongoing workforce crisis.

consensusPoints.text: Lack of standardization across payers is a root cause of the problem. Moffitt Cancer Center described the landscape as "a morass of shifting requirements, fraught with inefficiency and redundancy, that vary from payer to payer." CAQH CORE warned, "Different rules for different segments of the industry would complicate adoption and create more administrative burden for payers and providers." Allina Health noted a fundamental challenge: "A major problem we encounter is simply identifying when a prior authorization is required, as standards and policies among payers change constantly, and the inquiry work is done manually."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most stakeholders who elaborated on the cause of the burden (over 25 commenters) identified inconsistent requirements, forms, portals, and rules across different health plans as a primary driver of inefficiency and cost.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Real-World Impact of ePA on Provider Burden

positions.label: Potential Solution
positions.stance: ePA, if implemented correctly with strong standards, will significantly reduce administrative steps, costs, and time for providers.
positions.supportLevel: Most health IT vendors, payers, and standards organizations; cautiously supported by many provider groups.
keyArguments: Automation removes manual work (phone/fax)
integrated workflows are more efficient than portals
transparency of requirements reduces rework
data from pilots (CAQH, Cohere Health) shows dramatic time savings per transaction.

positions.label: Potential New Burden
positions.stance: ePA could automate a broken system, shift uncompensated work to clinicians, and add significant implementation costs and new workflow disruptions.
positions.supportLevel: A strong contingent of provider associations, individual providers, and at least one major EHR vendor.
keyArguments: Simply digitizing a bad process doesn't solve the problem
shifting PA into the clinical workflow is a massive change that moves payment tasks onto clinicians
short-term implementation costs and training will increase burden
without standardization, it will just be a mess of different electronic systems instead of different paper ones.

positions.label: Embed in Clinical Workflow
positions.stance: To be adopted and effective, ePA must be seamlessly integrated into the EHR at the point of ordering.
positions.supportLevel: The majority of commenters who addressed this, including vendors and provider groups.
keyArguments: Providers will not adopt solutions that require logging into separate portals
a non-disruptive, real-time process is essential for efficiency
this is the only way to realize the full benefits of automation.

positions.label: Beware the Clinical Workflow
positions.stance: Forcing payment-related tasks into the clinical workflow is a dangerous and disruptive shift of burden.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller but highly articulate group, led by a major EHR vendor.
keyArguments: PA is currently handled by administrative staff, not physicians
embedding it in the EHR forces physicians to do uncompensated payment work
this fundamentally upends efficient clinical workflows and will be resisted.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers (Hospitals, Health Systems, Medical Practices)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Massive financial costs (millions of dollars annually), immense staff resource drain (dozens of dedicated FTEs), and the direct link between administrative burden and clinician burnout.
specificPoints: They provide the most granular, real-world examples of the operational chaos, from the cost of monitoring payer rule changes to the workflow friction caused by misaligned documentation and adjudication timelines.

Demand for standardization, reduction in the volume of PAs, and solutions that don't add new costs or implementation burdens. Some, like Dr. Shteynshlyuger, demand compensation for PA work and the ability to disable ePA functionality.
UnityPoint Health employing over 60 FTEs for PA in Iowa alone. A large national system spending $15 million per month on administrative costs including PA.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Professional & Trade Associations (AMA, AAFP, AHA, specialty groups)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Aggregating member burnout and dissatisfaction, quantifying the national scale of the problem through surveys (e.g., hours per week, percentage of practices hiring dedicated staff), and advocating for comprehensive reform beyond just technology.
specificPoints: They serve as the primary source of quantitative data on provider burden, repeatedly citing AMA, MGMA, and their own internal surveys. They bridge the gap between individual provider anecdotes and national policy implications.

Strong push for standardized, integrated ePA, but also emphasis on reducing the overall scope of services subject to PA and ensuring any new technology is rigorously tested and doesn't exacerbate burdens, especially for small and rural practices.

The AAFP and RRC warning that ePA implementation could increase burden in the short term. The American College of Surgeons reports practices spend the equivalent of three business days per week on PA.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Vendors & Technology Companies (Epic, Cerner, Allscripts, Olive AI)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The need for clear, stable, and mature technical standards before being required to build and certify new functionalities. They worry about the cost of development and the risk of building solutions for a fragmented market that providers won't adopt.
specificPoints: They provide a reality check on the technical and workflow implications. Allscripts offers a crucial contrarian view, warning that shifting PA into the clinical workflow is a fundamental, and potentially disastrous, change. Mettle Solutions points out that EHR vendor fees for app installation are a major, overlooked barrier to adoption.
Strong support for mandating FHIR-based standards to create a single development target. They also request adequate time for development and for providers to adopt the new technology.
Epic's concern about the 'cumulative regulatory burden' and request for an 18-month provider adoption runway.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Payers & Payer Associations (AHIP, Cigna, Anthem, CVS Health)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: While acknowledging provider burden, they are also concerned with low provider adoption of existing electronic tools (portals, etc.). They seek to ensure that ePA standards are effective and don't undermine the purpose of PA.
specificPoints: They subtly shift some responsibility to providers, noting that many still choose to use fax even when portals are available. AHIP provides data from its Fast PATH pilot showing that providers who do use ePA see significant burden reduction, framing it as an adoption issue.
Advocate for standards and incentives (e.g., in MIPS) to drive provider adoption of electronic tools.
CVS Health states, "In many of our markets, 50-100% of the providers submit prior authorizations through faxes, even when we currently provide a provider portal."

noteworthyInsights.insight: Allscripts makes the critical point that "Current PA activities are managed by support staff, not providers." Therefore, moving ePA into the EHR clinical workflow is not a simple automation; it is a fundamental redesign that shifts administrative, payment-related tasks directly onto clinicians, which could be highly disruptive and unwelcome.

OneHealthPort insightfully argues that the term "prior authorization" is too narrow. The true provider burden comes from a wider array of "pre-service requirements" that must be completed to ensure payment. Focusing only on the PA transaction itself will solve only part of the problem and deliver insufficient value to providers.

Clarity Child Guidance Center provides a compelling real-world example of workflow conflict: a payer's 24-hour adjudication clock starts upon admission notification (ADT), but the finalized clinical assessments needed for the PA are not completed until later. This creates an inherent, process-based failure point that technology alone cannot solve.

A solo practitioner, Dr. Shteynshlyuger, astutely reframes the ROI issue, stating that ePA cannot be a "jackpot" for health plans (saving them massive costs) while remaining a money-losing, uncompensated activity for providers. This highlights the need to consider the financial equity of the solution.

Mettle Solutions, a technology developer, reveals a hidden barrier to adoption: even if a third-party developer offers a valuable ePA app for free, high fees charged by major EHR vendors to install any app in their "store" can kill adoption before it even starts.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The scale of the burden is articulated differently by facility type. Large systems like AHA and UnityPoint Health quantify the problem in terms of millions of dollars and dozens of full-time employees. Small and rural practices, represented by groups like AAFP and ACP, emphasize the disproportionate impact, where the burden falls directly on physicians, exacerbating workforce shortages. Specialty centers (e.g., Moffitt, Dana-Farber) focus on the acute patient harm caused by delays in time-sensitive care like cancer treatment.

A clear pattern emerges based on role. Front-line providers and their associations express the most skepticism about technology as a panacea, focusing on workflow, cost, and the need to reduce the volume of PA. Health IT vendors and standards bodies are more optimistic about technology, focusing on the need for stable, universal standards as the primary solution. Payers acknowledge the problem but also point to low provider adoption of existing electronic tools as a key hurdle.

Multiple commenters converged on the risk that a poorly implemented ePA system could have significant negative knock-on effects. The most frequently cited were: shifting work from paid administrative staff to uncompensated clinicians, increasing rather than decreasing burnout during implementation, and creating a new fragmented system of competing ePA apps that mirrors the current fragmented system of payer portals.

keyQuotations.quote: [PA] creates more angst and frustration for specialty physicians and their patients than any other administrative task.
The concepts embedded in this RFI are essentially asking providers to perform payment-related work as part of the clinical treatment workflows.
To be adopted, prior authorization cannot be a money-losing activity for healthcare providers. It cannot be a 'jackpot' to health plans.
One 17-hospital system spends $11 million annually just complying with health plan prior authorization requirements.
Providers and clinical staff waste precious time on paperwork instead of with patients.

During a time of national emergency where workforce shortages and strained health system capacity have been persistent challenges, there is simply insufficient bandwidth to comply with such cumbersome administrative procedures.
Over the short term, implementation of e-PA has the potential to increase, rather than decrease, providers’ administrative burdens.
I spend about half my time submitting new authorization requests; the other half of my time is spent looking for status updates on previously submitted authorizations.

A major problem we encounter is simply identifying when a prior authorization is required, as standards and policies among payers change constantly, and the inquiry work is done manually each time a procedure or service is performed.

The prior authorization process is going to have to take into account that current workflows and timing may not directly translate into a fully digital (no manual intervention) process; not a complete replacement of process.
Different rules for different segments of the industry would complicate adoption and create more administrative burden for payers and providers.
The value of physician time spent on this activity often costs more than the medication.
The potential volume of computer-generated and DTR-transmitted PA questions sent to providers may lead to significant provider overload, similar to coding and quality measure documentation burdens.
Never have I spent more time on administrative issues that do nothing but delay appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic intervention.

sections.executiveSummary: There is universal consensus among all stakeholders that current prior authorization (PA) processes cause direct and significant harm to patients through dangerous care delays, treatment abandonment, and severe adverse events. While there is broad support for electronic solutions to improve efficiency, a primary tension exists between the push for patient empowerment through data transparency and grave concerns about data privacy, particularly for vulnerable populations. Dominant recommendations demand that any reform must prioritize patient safety and timely access to care, with robust privacy guardrails and accountability measures, ensuring that technology serves patients rather than creating new barriers or risks.

consensusPoints.text: Current prior authorization processes directly harm patients by delaying necessary care. Virtually every commenter, from individual patients to the largest trade associations and payers, agrees that the current PA system is a source of patient harm. A supermajority of providers and their associations cite specific negative clinical outcomes.

exceptions.text: While still acknowledging potential delays, one association (AAHomecare) noted that PA can be beneficial for certain durable medical equipment categories for all stakeholders, including patients. Payers like AHIP frame PA as a tool that "helps ensure access to safe, effective, affordable, high-quality care," while still acknowledging it "can be burdensome to patients."

consensusPoints.text: Patient-centricity must be a primary goal of any reform. A strong majority of stakeholders, including providers, payers, and patient advocates, assert that the ultimate goal of streamlining PA is to improve the patient experience and health outcomes.

areasOfDebate.description: This debate centers on the degree to which patients should be directly involved in the electronic prior authorization process, weighing the benefits of active empowerment against the risks of added burden and complexity.

positions.label: Active Empowerment
positions.stance: Patients should be given direct electronic access to initiate, monitor, and receive status updates on their PA requests.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by several payers, patient advocacy groups, and some providers.
keyArguments: Empowers patients to be engaged in their care and become better healthcare shoppers
increases transparency and holds the system accountable
can reduce provider burden by allowing patients to track progress themselves.

positions.label: Cautious Involvement
positions.stance: Patient participation should be optional and carefully managed to avoid new burdens or negative consequences.
positions.supportLevel: Most major physician associations and some technology vendors.
keyArguments: Requiring patient participation could lead to more denials if patients cannot comply with complex documentation
risks shifting administrative burden from providers to patients
could introduce conflicting information into the process, causing further delays.

areasOfDebate.description: This debate highlights the tension between providing patients with maximum transparency and access to their health data versus implementing strict privacy guardrails to protect them from potential misuse and harm.

positions.label: Maximum Transparency
positions.stance: Give patients unfettered access to their PA and health data to enable informed decision-making and shopping for care.
positions.supportLevel: Patient advocacy groups and some technology consultants.
keyArguments: Transparency is a fundamental patient right
access to information empowers patients to lower costs and avoid surprise bills
engaged patients are essential for a functioning healthcare market.

positions.label: Strict Guardrails
positions.stance: Prioritize robust privacy protections, limiting data sharing to the minimum necessary and giving patients explicit control.
positions.supportLevel: Individual patients, nearly all physician/provider associations.
keyArguments: Unfettered data sharing poses extreme risks to vulnerable individuals (e.g., domestic violence victims)
payers could "eavesdrop" on EHRs, accessing irrelevant, sensitive data
patients may withhold critical information from doctors if they fear it will be shared without their control.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Severe privacy violations, potential for physical harm due to data breaches (e.g., for domestic violence victims), and violation of civil rights.
specificPoints: These commenters provide the most visceral and specific warnings about the real-world dangers of poorly implemented data sharing, drawing a direct and damning parallel to the flawed rollout of Electronic Visit Verification (EVV) systems.
Demand "extreme legislative oversight" before implementation, strict privacy standards, clear guidelines on data sharing, and patient consent controls.
A domestic violence victim explains their use of a "safe address" for medical mail and a friend's address for the pharmacy, a system that could be compromised by integrated data systems.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Direct clinical harm to patients, including treatment abandonment, disease progression, and serious adverse events up to and including death; process opacity and inconsistency; the erosion of the patient-provider relationship.
specificPoints: This group provides the most extensive quantitative evidence of harm, frequently citing AMA surveys showing that over 90% of physicians report care delays and 34% report serious adverse events due to PA.

Mandate drastically shorter and binding decision timelines; eliminate PA for routinely approved services; standardize denial reasons; and ensure technology does not grant payers unfettered access to patient EHRs.

The South Carolina Medical Association cites a survey where "34% of respondents reported that prior authorization led to a serious adverse event such as a death, hospitalization, disability, or permanent bodily damage".
Moffitt Cancer Center states that PA delays are "clinically and emotionally damaging to patients" battling cancer.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring patient privacy and data security; avoiding patient confusion from multiple data streams (e.g., an Advanced EOB before a PA is complete); maintaining PA as a tool for safety and affordability.
specificPoints: While acknowledging patient frustration, payers frame PA as a necessary safeguard against inappropriate care. They are supportive of ePA for efficiency but cautious about implementation details that could create new liabilities or confuse members.
Add functionality for patient opt-in to notifications; align new ePA standards with other consumer-facing tools; ensure robust data security is built into any certified system.
Anthem worries about "consumer confusion" if new ePA standards are not aligned with other initiatives.
AHIP emphasizes that PA "helps ensure access to safe, effective, affordable, high-quality care".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The risk of poor implementation leading to patient harm; the potential for increased PA volume if the process becomes too efficient; ensuring patients are not an "afterthought" in the technical design.
specificPoints: This group focuses on the technical "how," highlighting that certification can be harmful if standards are immature. They offer a crucial counterintuitive warning: efficiency could backfire if payers use it to require more PAs, increasing denials and patient suffering.
Require payers to notify patients of denials directly; use patient portals to share PA communications; ensure real-world testing to prevent unintended consequences.
Allscripts warns that increased efficiency could "increase the volume of required PA items by health plans," leading to more denials and patient suffering.
Olive AI notes that "patients feel lost in the system today" due to siloed data.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: PA serving as a direct barrier to care; lack of patient transparency and empowerment; the risk of surprise medical bills due to opaque processes.
specificPoints: These groups are singularly focused on arming the patient with information and recourse. They see data access not just as a convenience but as a fundamental right and a tool to combat systemic issues.
Give patients the ability to question denials and report non-compliant insurers/providers; provide patients with full access to PA status and requirements to make informed decisions.
PatientRightsAdvocate.org states HHS must provide comprehensive information as PA "often serve as barriers to patients getting access to care".
The Michael J. Fox Foundation describes how PA forces people with Parkinson's to "try ineffective treatments, leading to... irreversible disease progression".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Allscripts makes the crucial point that a more efficient ePA system could incentivize payers to increase the volume of services requiring authorization, potentially leading to more denials and greater patient harm, fundamentally undermining the goal of reform.

The anonymous comment from a domestic violence victim provides a concrete, unforgettable scenario of how data consolidation can endanger lives, stating, "I am allowed to utilize a safe address for all things medical... I use a friend’s address for the pharmacy to protect my safety." This highlights stakes far beyond administrative convenience.

The repeated citation across numerous submissions of the AMA survey finding that 34% of physicians report a serious adverse event due to PA moves the conversation from anecdote to quantifiable crisis. This statistic is the single most impactful piece of data in the comments.

The comparison to the flawed implementation of Electronic Visit Verification (EVV), which led to "GPS tracking of service delivery" for people with disabilities and put domestic violence victims in harm's way, serves as a powerful cautionary tale about the need for specific safeguards before rolling out new technology on vulnerable populations.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Commenters with direct patient care roles (physicians, hospitals) or personal vulnerabilities (patients) focus overwhelmingly on the immediate, severe risks of care delays and privacy breaches. In contrast, stakeholders from the technology and payer sectors, while acknowledging these risks, place greater emphasis on system-level efficiency, interoperability, and the potential future benefits of a streamlined process.

A recurring pattern is the fear that a technologically focused solution could create new problems. These include payers increasing the volume of PAs (Allscripts), new technology exacerbating the digital divide and health inequities (AMA, ASCO), and shifting administrative burdens from providers onto already stressed patients (AANP, HealthHelp).

There is a clear tension running through the comments. Patient advocates demand radical transparency and data access to empower consumers. Simultaneously, provider groups and vulnerable individuals demand radical privacy controls to prevent data misuse and harm. This suggests that a one-size-fits-all approach to data sharing will fail, and patient-level consent and control will be critical.

keyQuotations.quote: [M]edically necessary care should not be denied because a physician and/or a patient cannot jump through complicated opaque hoops.
34% of respondents reported that prior authorization led to a serious adverse event such as a death, hospitalization, disability, or permanent bodily damage for a patient in their care.

It’s been ruled unconstitutional to gps track sex offenders but suddenly was okay to track the services of the disabled because there were no safe guards or specific modifications put in place to protect consumers from harm or data breaches.
The overutilization of prior authorization has become one of the most impactful negative pressures on access to medically necessary care for people with Parkinson’s.
Physicians and patients should not be forced to choose between automating PA to reduce burden and forfeiting privacy rights and expectations.
At the heart of the discussion is the patient who is affected by the process.
Patients feel lost in the system today and healthcare employees work in the dark due to outdated technology that creates a lack of shared knowledge and siloed data.
Effectively this complexity subverts medical necessity guidelines and prioritizes process over health and wellness decisions.
Technological standardization alone is not enough to lift the unnecessary burdens that impede, complicate and add stress to patients who simply want to access the care they need.
It is of utmost importance to stress that ACOG members work with very sensitive patient information, and secure, safe transmission of patient health data is critical.

[We are concerned that] increased efficiency of PA could 'increase the volume of required PA items by health plans,' potentially leading to 'more denials, leading to patient suffering or delay in care...'
Of all HIPAA transactions, e-PA are the only ones that impact patients directly, and the need for thorough testing cannot be overemphasized.
We encourage ONC to prioritize the best interests of patients as they consider new approaches to prior authorization standards.
The delays in care and systemic costs are jarring, considering the resources required in provider and payer organizations for this wasteful, inefficient process.

10.themeDescription: Scope and Functional Boundaries of ePA

sections.executiveSummary: Public input reveals overwhelming consensus on the need for a certified electronic prior authorization (ePA) system to encompass a core set of end-to-end functional capabilities. However, a major debate exists on how to structure this certification. Providers, payers, and patient advocates largely support a holistic scope that replaces the entire manual workflow, includes appeals and patient access, and provides actionable data. In stark contrast, health IT vendors warn that a single, monolithic certification criterion represents a massive and potentially infeasible expansion into administrative and financial domains beyond the traditional scope of a clinical EHR, advocating instead for a flexible, modular certification approach.

consensusPoints.text: A vast majority of commenters across all stakeholder types (providers, payers, vendors, standards bodies, and advocacy groups) agree that the seven core functional capabilities outlined in the RFI are the necessary minimum for a successful ePA system. These include: (1) identifying PA applicability, (2) querying payer requirements, (3) collecting documentation, (4) submitting the request, (5) receiving a response, (6) querying status updates, and (7) handling digital signatures. At least 25 major organizations explicitly endorsed this core set, including nearly all provider and payer associations. The American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) "recommends including all functional capabilities described in the RFI in certified health IT for successful electronic prior authorization". Payer association AHIP stated the "core capabilities considered appropriate for inclusion in certified health IT for ePA include" the full list provided by ONC. Standards developer NCPDP "agrees with the following minimum functional capabilities" and lists all seven points.

exceptions.text: While there is agreement on what the functions are, there is significant disagreement on how they should be bundled and certified, which is the central debate of this theme.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of stakeholders, particularly providers and payers, stressed that for ePA to be effective, it must support the entire PA process from start to finish, replacing manual workflows rather than just automating fragments. This view was strongly articulated by major payers, large health systems, and health IT vendors focused on automation. Anthem insists that "any adopted electronic prior authorization standard must be capable of providing an end-to-end replacement for all elements of current manual processes, not just parts of it". OCHIN, representing community health centers, stated that "Certified health information technology should support an end-to-end automated prior authorization process". Health IT vendor Allscripts advocated for "the entire workflow, from initial request to final resolution (including denials and status updates), to be part of the certified scope for all stakeholders".

areasOfDebate.description: The central debate concerns whether the ePA system should be certified as a single, comprehensive solution (monolithic) or as a set of separate, independently certifiable functions (modular).

positions.label: Holistic Certification
positions.stance: The entire ePA workflow should be certified as a single, comprehensive module or process.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily supported by provider groups and some payers.
keyArguments: Prevents financial burden on physicians who would otherwise have to purchase and integrate multiple, separate modules.
Ensures vendors build the complete solution, avoiding situations where only the easiest parts are automated and the overall workflow remains broken.
Reflects the integrated nature of the workflow and simplifies implementation for providers.

positions.label: Modular Certification
positions.stance: ePA capabilities should be broken down into separate, independently certifiable modules or criteria.
positions.supportLevel: The dominant view among health IT developers and technical consulting firms.
keyArguments: The ePA workflow spans multiple systems beyond the traditional clinical EHR (e.g., revenue cycle, practice management), making a single certification infeasible for specialized vendors.
A monolithic requirement would stifle innovation and could force developers out of the certification program if they cannot support the entire scope.
Provides flexibility for providers to choose "best-of-breed" solutions and allows developers to certify to the functions relevant to their products.

areasOfDebate.description: This debate centers on whether the certified ePA system should be limited to the core authorization transaction or expanded to include related administrative processes like appeals and patient-facing features.

positions.label: Focused on PA
positions.stance: The scope of certification should adhere to the core prior authorization transaction as outlined in the RFI.
positions.supportLevel: The implicit position of the majority of commenters who focused their responses on the seven core capabilities.
keyArguments: This approach is pragmatic, aligns with the RFI's direct questions, and tackles the most immediate burden before expanding into more complex areas.

positions.label: Expanded Scope
positions.stance: The system must include related processes beyond the initial PA request to be truly effective.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority of commenters, including patient advocates, large providers, and some payers.
keyArguments: Excluding processes like appeals, concurrent review, and retrospective review leaves major administrative burdens unaddressed.
The system should include patient-facing capabilities, allowing them to track status and receive notifications.
The workflow should incorporate peer-to-peer review processes and handle authorizations for complex care plans and associated items (e.g., DME, prescriptions).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden, eliminating care delays, and receiving complete and actionable information from payers.
specificPoints: They emphasize the real-world clinical and administrative workflow impact. For example, the Alliance of Dedicated Cancer Centers highlighted the need for a single authorization to cover an entire cancer treatment plan, not separate requests for imaging, labs, and chemotherapy.

Mandate a comprehensive, end-to-end ePA system. Require payers to provide specific, actionable reasons for denials and clear information on pending requests, not just a "pending" status. The American College of Physicians stressed that responses must "contain actionable information" to be useful.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The feasibility, cost, and complexity of developing and certifying a massively expanded set of functions.
specificPoints: They view the issue through the lens of software development, product lines, and certification logistics. Cerner noted that ePA "marks potentially the most significant expansion to the certification criteria...A boundary of sorts is crossed into the administrative side of healthcare". Epic detailed how ePA functions span modules (registration, revenue cycle) often outside the current scope of certification.

Adopt a flexible, modular certification approach. Allow developers to certify to individual capabilities rather than a single, monolithic workflow. Ensure standards are mature and granular before mandating them.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring the ePA system is robust enough to fully replace manual processes, maintaining flexibility in their own systems, and incorporating the patient perspective.
specificPoints: They bring up system-level requirements like routing requests to third-party Utilization Management Organizations (WEDI) and the need for identity resolution and security infrastructure to work at scale (Cigna).

Implement an end-to-end FHIR-based solution. Add functionality for patient notifications. Caution against overly prescriptive requirements that could "limit functionality and usability" of APIs (CareFirst).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring the system is truly comprehensive and that patients are not left out of the process.
specificPoints: This group uniquely focuses on expanding the definition of "authorization" and empowering patients. PatientRightsAdvocate.org argued that excluding concurrent or retrospective review is a "significant limitation".
New York Urology Specialists recommended that patients should be able to initiate and monitor the ePA process themselves.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Shifting the Design Paradigm: UCSF CDHI offered a powerful reframing of the task, recommending that ONC "ensure each tranche of work attempts to solve for a single end-to-end patient use case, rather than fragmented capabilities". This user-centric approach contrasts with the technology-centric debate over modular vs. monolithic certification.

The Administrative-Clinical Boundary Crossing: Cerner's insight that ePA certification "crosses a boundary of sorts into the administrative side of healthcare" crystallizes the core tension. It explains why health IT vendors, whose certification experience is rooted in clinical EHRs, view this as a fundamental and burdensome shift in scope.

The "80/20 Rule" for Complexity: The Alliance of Community Health Plans (ACHP) proposed a pragmatic approach, suggesting that "electronic prior authorization should not be used for infrequent, complex services" and that ONC should identify an "industry-wide '80/20 rule' enabling the industry to achieve appropriate value without solving complex, low-occurrence use cases". This acknowledges that trying to automate 100% of cases may be inefficient.

Data-backed Observation: Olive AI provided concrete data on the current state, estimating a "$7 billion societal burden in the ambulatory prior authorization market" and noting that a single request can take "38 to 50 minutes" for provider staff. This quantifies the problem being addressed.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type Patterns: Large, integrated delivery networks that act as both provider and payer (Kaiser Permanente, Providence) tend to favor a single, unified system, as it simplifies their internal operations. In contrast, specialty vendors and consultants who serve a diverse market of un-integrated providers see the deep fragmentation of existing systems (EHR, RCM, PMS) and thus advocate strongly for modularity.

Experience Correlations: Commenters with deep technical experience in standards development (e.g., Epic, Point-of-Care Partners, AEGIS.net) consistently proposed the most detailed, modular certification frameworks, mapping specific functions to specific IGs. In contrast, commenters focused on clinical practice (e.g., AAFP, ACOG, ASCO) focused more on the desired end-state workflow and outcomes, such as receiving actionable information and reducing clicks.

keyQuotations.quote: "[ePA] marks potentially the most significant expansion to the certification criteria set since the original 2015 Edition... A boundary of sorts is crossed into the administrative side of healthcare, and the traditional core focus of Certified HIT (CHIT) on clinical EHR is expanded."
"The RFI’s exclusive focus on prior authorization, to the exclusion of other utilization management processes, is a significant limitation that must be addressed in rulemaking."
"Ensure each tranche of work attempts to solve for a single end-to-end patient use case, rather than fragmented capabilities."
"For electronic prior authorization to be meaningfully useful to the clinician, decrease burden, and improve patient care, the response from the payer must contain actionable information."

"Breaking out the necessary prior authorization functions into multiple modules could exacerbate the already existing financial burden experienced by all physicians... Requiring physicians to purchase multiple modules... could add to these burdens, potentially threatening the financial stability of smaller practices."

"One must weigh the consequences of setting very specific capabilities against the potential burden it would create for providers and payers alike... loading up specific requirements that may ultimately limit functionality and usability."

"Many payers give status updates that simply read, “pending,” but with no indication of what further information might be needed for a final decision. An explanation of the cause of the delay... would also help to streamline the process."

"[Our centers] are not granted a single prior authorization for a care plan that follows evidence-based medicine... rather, each step or type of service (consult, imaging, laboratory testing, surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, adjunctive therapies) must be separately authorized."

"If certification criteria only cover 'part of the workflow'... there's a 'massive risk that functionality will not be fully utilized,' leading to 'significant development work with little to no return' for software developers."

"Some practices have multiple employees to perform just this function – for example, to print each authorization submission, keep them in a stack, log into each provider portal every day, and manually check the status of each."

1.1.themeDescription: Reducing PA Volume as the Primary Goal

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus among providers and their representative organizations that automating prior authorization (PA) is insufficient and must be paired with fundamental reforms to reduce the overall volume of PA requirements. The core public sentiment is that the primary goal should be changing when and why PA is used, not just how it is processed. The most dominant recommendations are the widespread adoption of "gold carding" programs to exempt high-performing providers and the elimination of PA for services that are routinely approved or follow evidence-based guidelines. While support for these volume-reduction strategies is nearly unanimous among clinical stakeholders, a key tension exists with a minority viewpoint from a technology vendor warning that such exemptions could lead to increased healthcare spending.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters explicitly state that automating the current PA system will not solve the core problems of provider burden, patient care delays, and administrative waste. A strong majority express fear that without broader reforms, automation could backfire, making it easier for payers to expand the volume of services requiring PA.

exceptions.text: A few technology vendors see automation itself (particularly with AI/ML) as a primary method to reduce manual reviews and thus overall burden, which is a slightly different framing but shares the goal of reducing unnecessary touchpoints.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters across all stakeholder types (except one) advocate for "gold carding" or similar programs that exempt providers with a history of high approval rates from PA requirements. This is the single most frequently proposed solution. The Texas "gold card" law (HB 3459) was cited as a model by multiple organizations.

exceptions.text: One technology vendor, Olive AI, explicitly argued against gold carding, suggesting it would increase healthcare spending.

consensusPoints.text: A vast majority of provider and professional organizations argue that services that are almost always approved, are low-cost, or adhere to established national guidelines should be exempt from PA entirely.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A vast majority of provider and professional organizations

areasOfDebate.topic: Utility of Prior Authorization as a Cost-Control Tool
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether prior authorization is an effective and necessary tool for managing healthcare costs or an inefficient, burdensome process that harms patient care and creates waste.

positions.label: Burdensome & Ineffective
positions.stance: PA is a blunt, wasteful tool that primarily creates delays and administrative burden for appropriate care, rather than effectively managing utilization.
positions.supportLevel: The overwhelming majority of commenters, including virtually all providers, professional organizations, and patient advocates.
keyArguments: High approval rates for many services prove the process is unnecessary
the administrative cost of the PA process often outweighs the cost of the service itself
the goal seems to be discouraging care through hassle, not ensuring quality.

positions.label: Necessary Cost Control
positions.stance: Eliminating PA programs outright would lead to increased healthcare spending without a corresponding improvement in quality or value.
positions.supportLevel: A single technology vendor.
keyArguments: PA is a necessary mechanism to manage utilization and control costs
eliminating it has been shown to increase spending.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Massive operational costs ("hundreds of full-time employees" at Allina Health); direct, measurable patient harm (UCSF reports ~10% of surgeries canceled last-minute due to PA issues); payer expansion of PA to low-cost, routine treatments (Dana-Farber).
specificPoints: They provide concrete operational data and examples of system-level impact. Northwell Health proposed a novel "authorize all, audit a sample" model based on its internal coding compliance processes.
Nearly unanimous support for gold carding, exempting guideline-based care (especially in oncology), and eliminating PA for routinely approved services.
UCSF's report that 10% of operative cases are canceled at the last minute due to PA, nearly all of which are later approved, powerfully illustrates the system's dysfunction.
Dana-Farber's experience of having an "incredibly low rate of clinical prior authorization denials" yet still facing intense PA requirements highlights the perceived irrationality of the process.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Professional & Trade Associations (Physician, Specialist, & Admin Groups)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Physician burnout; erosion of clinical decision-making; ever-increasing volume of PA requirements; negative patient outcomes from delays.
specificPoints: They aggregate the voice of thousands of members, citing survey data (TMA found 87% of physicians saw a drastic increase in PA) and representing specific clinical scenarios where PA is inappropriate (ACOG on NIPT testing).
The most vocal and consistent proponents of legislative and regulatory action to mandate gold carding (citing the Texas model) and limit PA to services with a documented history of overutilization.

The American College of Cardiology's observation that high approval rates after long delays create the perception that PA's goal is to "discourage appropriate consumption of healthcare resources rather than to ensure high-quality care".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Direct patient harm and irreversible disease progression caused by "fail first" protocols and care delays.
specificPoints: They uniquely center the human cost and irreversible consequences for patients, framing PA as one of the "most impactful negative pressures on access to medically necessary care."
Comprehensive reform to reduce the overall use of PA to ensure timely access to evidence-based care.
The Michael J. Fox Foundation's testimony that PA forces Parkinson's patients to "try ineffective treatments," leading to "irreversible disease progression".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring that new standards are implementable and drive adoption of their solutions; standardizing data elements to make automation feasible.
specificPoints: They see technology (AI/ML, CDS, FHIR standards) as the mechanism to achieve volume reduction. They propose using analytics on pooled data to identify low-risk requests (1upHealth, Apixio) or using Clinical Decision Support (CDS) to bypass PA entirely (Elsevier).

Leverage technology to automate approvals, not just submissions. WEDI, an industry-wide group, strongly endorsed a two-pronged approach: "reducing the overall volume of authorizations and automating the remaining requirements."
Apixio claims its solution reduces manual reviews by over 50% by automating approvals based on historical data.
Olive AI stands out for its caution against eliminating PA.

noteworthyInsights.insight: PA as "Structural Inequality": OCHIN, a network serving community health centers, uniquely frames the "proliferation of prior authorization requirements for well-established, evidence-based clinical practice" as a form of structural inequality, disproportionately harming vulnerable patient populations who can least afford delays.

The "TSA Pre-Check" Analogy: UCSF offers a powerful and easily understood analogy for "gold carding," describing it as a "bypass status, like TSA pre-check" for physicians practicing evidence-based medicine. This framing makes the concept highly accessible to policymakers.

A Novel "Authorize and Audit" Model: Northwell Health provides a completely different operational model: "authorize all cases, and audit a sample afterwards." This shifts the paradigm from pre-emptive restriction to post-hoc verification, a significant departure from current thinking.

Value-Based Care as a PA Replacement: The American Medical Group Association makes a compelling policy argument that for providers in value-based care models, PA is redundant because financial incentives are already aligned to prevent overutilization, making PA an unnecessary administrative hurdle.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Texas "Gold Card" Law as a National Template: The repeated citation of Texas House Bill 3459 by multiple national and state organizations (TMA, PAI, SCMA, AdvaMed) indicates it has become the de facto model for state-level and potential federal PA reform legislation.

A Unified Provider Front: Despite representing diverse specialties (cardiology, oncology, pediatrics, urology, etc.) and practice types (large hospitals, small clinics), there is a remarkably unified front among all healthcare provider and professional organizations on the core message: reduce PA volume first.

Unintended Consequences are the Top Fear: The most consistent cross-cutting theme is the fear that a well-intentioned technical solution (automation) will have the unintended consequence of worsening the root problem (PA volume) without strong regulatory guardrails.

Oncology as a Case Study: Cancer care is frequently used as the prime example of PA's dysfunction. Commenters from major cancer centers (Moffitt, Dana-Farber, MSKCC) detail how PA is required for every fragmented step of care, even when following universally accepted NCI guidelines.

keyQuotations.quote: "[A]n efficiency applied to unnecessary work only allows users to do unnecessary work faster."
"Not only must the prior authorization process be less onerous, but also less common."
"Ultimately, AMGA believes the issues surrounding prior authorization can best be solved by eliminating it when at all possible."

"High approval rates after inordinate delays drive the perception among clinicians that the goal of prior authorization is to discourage appropriate consumption of healthcare resources rather than to ensure high-quality care."
"The overutilization of prior authorization has become one of the most impactful negative pressures on access to medically necessary care for people with Parkinson’s."
"Since majority of cases are authorized without issue, authorize all cases, and audit a sample afterwards."

"A jarring statistic in UCSF Orthopedics is that approximately 10% of operative cases are canceled at the last minute due to a lack of authorization, and nearly all of these are eventually approved on appeal..."

"If the process for electronic prior authorization is seamless but doesn’t have proper regulatory guardrails, we may see unintended consequences such as payers requiring even more services be subject to prior authorization."

"These types of prior authorizations [routinely approved ones] accomplish nothing in the way of improved patient care or healthcare savings and simply place roadblocks between a patient and the patient’s needed treatment."
"While technology and automation have crucial roles to play, the actual process itself needs to be reworked and streamlined first."

"Olive believes that eliminating prior authorizations outright, through gold card programs or direct elimination, has repeatedly been shown to increase healthcare spending without any noticeable improvement in quality or value."
"For those providers delivering care in value-based model, the incentives to over-utilize select procedures, tests, or medications, do not exist."

1.2.themeDescription: Automation and Streamlining as the Primary Goal

sections.executiveSummary: There is universal consensus among all stakeholders that the current manual, fax- and phone-based prior authorization (PA) system is broken and must be replaced with a standardized, automated electronic process. A vast majority believe this electronic prior authorization (ePA) system must be integrated directly into provider EHR workflows to be effective. The key tension is between stakeholders (primarily payers and some technology vendors) who view automation as the complete solution to fix an inefficient but valid process, and provider groups who see it as a necessary but insufficient step that fails to address the excessive volume and questionable clinical basis of many PA requirements. The dominant recommendation is a powerful, cross-stakeholder call for federal mandates requiring all payers to adopt a single, national set of interoperability standards to end the current fragmented and burdensome proprietary systems.

consensusPoints.text: The current manual prior authorization process is unsustainable and must be replaced with an electronic, automated system.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (over 95%) across every stakeholder category—providers, payers, tech vendors, and associations—explicitly condemn the current reliance on faxes, phone calls, and mail as inefficient, costly, and a primary source of provider burden and patient care delays.

consensusPoints.text: Electronic prior authorization (ePA) must be integrated into provider EHR workflows.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly providers and health IT vendors, insist that for ePA to be effective, it cannot be a separate, bolt-on solution. It must be a seamless part of the clinical workflow at the point of care.

exceptions.text: A few commenters, like Allscripts, express skepticism about the feasibility of a truly real-time process during a patient visit, calling it "highly aspirational" and warning it could be "inefficient and frustrating" for providers.

consensusPoints.text: Standardization across all payers is essential for success.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters identify the current lack of standardization—with every payer having its own portal, rules, and data requirements—as a primary driver of administrative burden. There is a universal call for a single, national standard that all payers must adopt.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over which technical standard—modern FHIR APIs, existing X12 standards, or a harmonized approach—should be mandated for electronic prior authorization.

positions.label: Modern FHIR APIs
positions.stance: Implement the new HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) as the standard.
positions.supportLevel: Most technology vendors, standards bodies, and many large provider systems and payers.
keyArguments: FHIR APIs are modern, enable true workflow integration and automation, and can "transform" the process rather than just digitizing an old one. They are seen as the path to real-time decisions.

positions.label: Leverage Existing X12
positions.stance: Continue to support and build upon the existing HIPAA-mandated X12 standards (e.g., X12 278).
positions.supportLevel: A minority of commenters, primarily clearinghouses and those focused on immediate, broad payer adoption.
keyArguments: X12 is the established standard that payers already use; leveraging it would increase health plan participation and avoid disrupting existing infrastructure, making adoption faster.

positions.label: Standard Agnostic/Harmonization
positions.stance: Allow for flexibility, support multiple standards, or mandate translation between them (e.g., FHIR to X12).
positions.supportLevel: Some large payers and multi-stakeholder groups.
keyArguments: Exclusively relying on one standard (like FHIR) could create duplicative workflows and be disruptive. A "standard agnostic" approach to data content or requiring translation capabilities would ease transition and reduce burden.

areasOfDebate.topic: Sufficiency of Automation as a Solution
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether technological automation is a complete solution to the problems of prior authorization or merely an insufficient first step.

positions.label: Technology is the Fix
positions.stance: Automation and streamlining are the primary and sufficient goals for reform.
positions.supportLevel: Most payers, some technology vendors.
keyArguments: Prior authorization is a valid and necessary tool for utilization management. The problem is not the existence of PA, but its inefficient, manual execution, which technology can solve.

positions.label: Necessary, Not Sufficient
positions.stance: Automation is a critical first step, but it will not solve the fundamental problems with prior authorization.
positions.supportLevel: Nearly all provider organizations and many individual clinicians.
keyArguments: Automating a flawed system does not fix it. This approach fails to address the excessive number of services requiring PA, the use of non-evidence-based payer rules, and inappropriate denials.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing immense administrative burden, preventing patient care delays, and mitigating staff/physician burnout.
specificPoints: They consistently emphasize that technology must serve the clinical workflow, not disrupt it. They are the most vocal in warning that automation is not a panacea and that simply "digitizing a broken process" could make things worse if not implemented carefully.
Mandate a single, national ePA standard for ALL payers; require real-time or near-real-time responses; design systems that automatically pre-populate forms with EHR data.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Improving operational efficiency, reducing administrative costs (for both themselves and providers), and preserving PA as a key tool for ensuring appropriate, evidence-based care.
specificPoints: They frame PA as a positive and necessary function for patient safety and cost management. They view technology as the key to addressing the negative "side effects" (burden, delays) of the current process.
Industry-wide adoption of national ePA standards (often favoring FHIR); ensuring solutions are scalable and integrated.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The need for clear, stable, and singular technical standards to develop against; feasible implementation timelines; avoiding a patchwork of requirements that makes development impractical and costly.
