specificPoints: They offer the most detailed technical analysis and warnings. Some, like Allscripts, provide a deeply skeptical view of "real-time" PA feasibility, while others, like the Da Vinci Project, offer a transformative vision of what's possible with modern standards.
Strong advocacy for federal adoption of specific standards (usually FHIR IGs); clear certification criteria; phased implementation timelines.

noteworthyInsights.insight: UCSF's Center for Digital Health Innovation provides a powerful warning based on their experience with the shift from paper to EHRs: "Digitizing manual processes often exposes, rather than eliminates, inefficiencies." This phrase concisely captures a primary fear among providers.

New York Urology Specialists proposed a unique and ambitious solution: a national "data exchange hub/clearinghouse" run by the federal government to solve the fragmentation problem of connecting over 1 million providers to over 50,000 health plans.

Allscripts offers a sharp analysis of potential negative clinical outcomes from a poorly implemented ePA system, suggesting providers might be "motivated to submit for easily approved treatments, rather than potentially more beneficial but harder-to-authorize options" to avoid workflow friction.

The South Carolina Hospital Association offers a cynical but thought-provoking perspective that payers may have a financial disincentive to be efficient, as manual processes and friction can "creat[e] the potential for claims denials and to slow the delivery of patient claims and reimbursement."

PCMA provides compelling data showing the dramatic success of ePA for prescription drugs (64% increase in requests) versus the sluggish adoption for medical benefits (2.3% increase), highlighting both a potential roadmap and the scale of the challenge ahead.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A clear pattern emerged where payers and their associations consistently defend prior authorization as a critical tool for patient safety and cost control, viewing technology as the fix for its procedural flaws. Conversely, nearly every provider organization, while supporting the technology, frames it as a partial solution to a fundamentally overused and often harmful requirement.

A tension exists between the transformative, optimistic vision for ePA articulated by standards bodies (HL7, Da Vinci) and the more cautious, skeptical perspective from some on-the-ground implementers (Allscripts, New York Urology Specialists) who highlight the immense practical complexities of data standardization, workflow integration, and the sheer number of payer connections required.

A recurring pattern is the belief that incremental or partial standardization will fail. Commenters consistently argue that unless a standard is mandated for all payers, the burden on providers will not be meaningfully reduced, as they will still need to manage exceptions and proprietary systems.

keyQuotations.quote: Standardization and automation are urgently needed and long overdue.
This is not only needed, it’s absolutely necessary to reduce the delays in care that we are seeing from the lack of standards and manual intervention that must be done.

The availability of electronic prior authorization capabilities within certified health information technology will only reduce burden for healthcare providers to the extent that it is broadly standardized and adopted by health plans and payers.
Health IT companies serving LTPAC and other providers... cannot program for the possibly unending combinations of payers and payer requirements, which is why standardization is urgently needed.
keyQuotations.sourceType: National Association for the Support of Long Term Care (NASL)

keyQuotations.quote: Interoperability between providers and payers will eliminate manual steps like phone calls, fax transmissions, scanned documents, etc.
We believe it is important to move forward with ePA in a way that does not just digitize the current paper process, but transforms it into an optimized digital solution.
The goal must be real-time or near-real-time processing of requests to ensure decisions can be made at the point of care before patients walk out the door.
An ideal system would permit a physician to document a patient’s condition and medical decision-making and have this documentation be sufficient for prior authorization review.
Healthcare is calling for an automated, episodic, and collaborative UM model that will streamline and make the Prior Authorization process more predictable for patients, providers and payers.
Digitizing manual processes often exposes, rather than eliminates, inefficiencies (e.g., shift from paper to electronic health records).
keyQuotations.sourceType: University of California, San Francisco - Center for Digital Health Innovation (UCSF CDHI)

keyQuotations.quote: The goal to have all of this activity occur in real time while a provider is ordering patient care is admirable but highly aspirational...
Simply requiring a technical standard may not result in timely adoption that satisfies industry business needs.
AMRPA is therefore highly concerned that any efforts to 'automate' prior authorization could promote the use of these types of [inappropriate] tools.

For frontline physicians who are already facing burnout and staff shortages, investing now in a single harmonious standard would be the best option for preventing the long-term challenges and inefficiencies of having to translate between and satisfy multiple sets of standards.
Patients feel lost in the system today and healthcare employees work in the dark due to outdated technology that creates a lack of shared knowledge and siloed data.

Yes, ONC should pursue this approach and the risks of doing nothing far outweigh not taking this position. People are getting sicker, money is running out, and doctors are quitting. This entire approach, whatever is chosen, is urgent to pursue.
Providers desperately need this functionality but will be deprived of these important burden-reducing features if ONC delays.

1.3.themeDescription: Opposition to Federal Mandates and Regulation

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on this theme is overwhelmingly one of caution and opposition to a new federal mandate for electronic prior authorization (ePA). Commenters across all stakeholder groups—including providers, payers, IT developers, and patients—view a premature or overly prescriptive mandate as a significant threat. The core tension is between a desire for market-driven innovation and the practical realities of implementation, with a strong consensus that current standards are too immature and provider systems too overloaded for a new mandate. Dominant recommendations advocate for a voluntary, flexible, and phased approach, urging the government to support real-world testing and enforce existing standards rather than imposing new, untested ones.

consensusPoints.text: Mandating immature standards is counterproductive and harmful. A strong majority of commenters from nearly every stakeholder group explicitly warned against codifying ePA standards before they are mature and have undergone robust, real-world testing. They argue this would lock in flawed technology, shift focus from genuine reform to box-checking compliance, and create significant downstream problems. The American Medical Association (AMA) "cautions against requiring ePA use to 'work out the kinks' by codifying it in regulation now. This would shift industry focus to federal program compliance instead of PA reform". The American Health Information Management Association (AHIMA) states flatly, "Standards that have not completed robust real-world testing are not suitable for mandated use in health policy". Health IT developer Voluware is "strongly against the adoption of any certification process that locks in standards with unproven merits".

exceptions.text: While opposing a mandate now, some commenters like Cigna suggest a coordinated roadmap with requirements for all stakeholders could be acceptable once standards mature.

consensusPoints.text: The healthcare system lacks the capacity to absorb another major IT mandate at this time. Many commenters, particularly provider associations and IT leaders, stressed that providers and developers are already overwhelmed implementing requirements from the 21st Century Cures Act. Adding a new ePA mandate would strain resources, disrupt ongoing projects, and lead to poor implementation. The College of Healthcare Information Management Executives (CHIME) notes providers are still awaiting delivery of Cures Update certified products and encourages HHS to "refrain from mandating any changes to health IT certification until a sufficient time after the full implementation...has been completed," suggesting no sooner than the end of 2023. The National Association for the Support of Long Term Care (NASL) anticipates that "providers will be overwhelmed by the upcoming transition to a broader definition of electronic health information (EHI)" and worries it will be "difficult for providers to prioritize among multiple, changing health IT standards". The American Psychiatric Association (APA) is "concerned about the timing," stating a premature release "would place a burden on developers to appropriately test and deploy this technology" leading to "negative administrative, financial, and clinical consequences".

Prescriptive mandates stifle innovation and harm market-based solutions. A significant cohort of commenters, especially health IT developers and providers focused on innovation, argued that overly specific federal rules would prevent the development of better, more efficient solutions. They advocate for flexibility and a focus on goals, not specific technologies. RadNet advises ONC to "refrain from being overly prescriptive" and instead focus on the "intent and not any specific technology," to "capitalize on, rather than inhibit, innovations already happening in this space". athenahealth warns ONC to "exercise caution around certification requirements that unintentionally determine business models for the industry and risk stifling innovative approaches". Voluware argues certification "impede[s] the ability for market driven solutions that provide real cost savings from entering the marketplace" and "ossify the market".

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of the Federal Government in ePA Standards

positions.label: Fundamental Opposition
positions.stance: The federal government is already too involved in healthcare and should rescind regulations, not add new ones.
positions.supportLevel: A small but vocal minority, most clearly articulated by one physician advocacy group.
keyArguments: Government involvement inherently increases red tape; electronic systems create unacceptable security risks (breaches, hijacking) not present with paper or phone; the free market is superior.

positions.label: Cautious Guidance
positions.stance: The government's role should be to guide and facilitate, not mandate.
positions.supportLevel: The largest group, including most health IT vendors, innovative providers, and some trade associations.
keyArguments: Mandates should be voluntary/optional at first; the focus should be on high-level goals and data standards, not prescriptive technology; this allows for flexibility, innovation, and market competition to find the best solutions.

positions.label: Coordinated Mandate
positions.stance: Mandates are ineffective and unfair if they are one-sided.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily payers.
keyArguments: Requiring payers to build APIs is pointless if providers and EHR vendors are not also required and incentivized to use them; a successful system requires a coordinated roadmap with consistent requirements for all stakeholders.

positions.label: Enforce Existing
positions.stance: The government should focus on enforcing existing, underutilized standards rather than creating new ones.
positions.supportLevel: A minority of providers and payers.
keyArguments: Existing standards like X12 are already in place; creating new FHIR-based standards would create duplicative and burdensome workflows; the problem is lack of enforcement and adoption, not a lack of standards.

positions.label: Develop New
positions.stance: New, modern standards (like FHIR APIs) are necessary, but they must be mature before being mandated.
positions.supportLevel: The implicit position of the majority of commenters who argue for caution and testing.
keyArguments: While new standards hold promise, they are not ready for primetime; they require extensive real-world testing and a careful, phased implementation to avoid the pitfalls of past IT mandates.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Increased administrative burden on physicians; financial viability of small and rural practices; negative clinical consequences from poorly deployed technology.
specificPoints: Emphasize the opportunity cost—mandating ePA development pushes aside other, more desired IT functionalities. They also highlight the real-world barrier of inadequate broadband in rural areas (SCMA).
Delay any mandates until standards are mature and other IT requirements are implemented; rescind regulations entirely (AAPS).

The South Carolina Medical Association warns that "onerous policies could push physicians out of rural areas and small practices where a lack of broadband and the need for low overhead precludes electronic record keeping".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Implementation feasibility; system capacity overload due to competing mandates (Cures Act); ensuring standards are thoroughly tested in the real world before being required.
specificPoints: They provide a 'view from the trenches' of IT deployment, citing a history of 'wasted resources on failed implementations' and standards that 'do not achieve desired goals' because they weren't grounded in real-world experience.
The federal government should fund/support real-world testing; delay mandates until after 2023; harmonize existing documentation requirements first.

AHIMA recounts a decade of experience where 'end-users that deploy technology to care for patients have found that new technical approaches and regulatory requirements are not sufficiently grounded in real-world experiences'.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Prescriptive regulations stifling innovation; certification processes creating anti-competitive 'walled gardens'; mandates dictating business models.
specificPoints: As market participants, they argue that competition and flexibility produce better, more cost-effective solutions than top-down mandates.
Make certification voluntary/optional; focus on high-level goals, not specific technologies; allow flexible 'all of the above' approaches to data exchange.

Voluware provides a sharp critique that certification, in practice, "has served to create walled gardens that have been erected and utilized in an anti-competitive, and anti-innovative manner by incumbents".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Opposing "one-sided" requirements that place the burden solely on payers; avoiding duplicative workflows with existing standards (X12); preserving flexibility for different health plans.
specificPoints: They highlight the ecosystem nature of the problem, arguing that mandates on them are useless without corresponding requirements for providers and vendors to connect.
Develop a coordinated roadmap for all stakeholders; allow use of existing standards; avoid superseding state-level rules.
Cigna states that "one-sided requirements...will not result in the broad adoption necessary to achieve the benefits of ePA".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Extreme privacy violations; data security for vulnerable populations; erosion of civil rights through technology.
specificPoints: Connects the proposed rule to specific past harms from other federal health IT mandates (EVV GPS tracking of disabled people), providing a stark warning about unintended consequences. This voice grounds the abstract policy debate in severe, real-world human risk.
Require extreme legislative oversight and congressional review before any system is built; establish very strict, clear-cut privacy standards and rules for what data can be shared.

The commenter notes a previous rule led to a situation where "it was ruled unconstitutional to gps track sex offenders but suddenly was okay to track the services of the disabled because there were no safe guards".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Unintended Consequences of Past Mandates: The individual commenter provides a stark and compelling warning by connecting the proposal to the tangible harms of a previous federal IT mandate (the 21st Century Cures Act's EVV). The example of disabled people being subjected to GPS tracking, which had been ruled unconstitutional for sex offenders, is a powerful illustration of how broad rules without clear safeguards can violate civil rights.

Certification as an Anti-Competitive Tool: The Health IT developer articulates a sophisticated critique that certification, while appearing beneficial in the abstract, in practice "has served to create walled gardens that have been erected and utilized in an anti-competitive, and anti-innovative manner by incumbents." This reframes certification from a quality-assurance tool to a potential market-distorting weapon.

The 'ON/OFF Switch': A New York urology practice proposed that any mandated ePA integration must include an 'ON/OFF switch' controlled by the provider. This simple, practical concept crystallizes the deep-seated fear among clinicians of losing control over their workflows to technology dictated by payers or the government.

Data-Backed Security Risk: The Association of American Physicians and Surgeons grounds its general concern about security in specific data, citing that in 2021, "44,993,618 such records were stolen or exposed," with nearly three-fourths due to hacking. This adds quantitative weight to the risks of further digitization.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type patterns: A clear distinction emerges between the needs of small/rural practices and larger systems. Commenters representing physicians in these settings (SCMA, NY Urology) highlight fundamental barriers like lack of broadband and low overhead, fearing that a one-size-fits-all mandate would be impossible for them to meet and could drive them out of practice.

Experience correlations: Commenters with direct, hands-on experience implementing large-scale health IT projects (CHIME, AHIMA) are less focused on the ideological opposition to regulation and more on the pragmatic, operational sequence of events. Their primary message is about timing, testing, and system capacity.

Unintended consequences: Multiple commenters independently identified the risk that a mandate would shift the industry's focus from meaningful PA reform to a narrow, check-the-box compliance exercise, ultimately failing to solve the underlying problem (AMA, AHIMA). Another identified pattern is that prescriptive rules intended to standardize can paradoxically stifle the innovation needed to truly improve the system (RadNet, Voluware).

keyQuotations.quote: [We] urge ONC and the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) to focus on enforcing existing standards for prior authorization transactions and strengthening policy requirements that minimize disruptions to clinical care.

Our experience has been while Certification in the abstract would appear to have merit, in actual practice within the healthcare IT ecosystem, this has served to create walled gardens that have been erected and utilized in an anti-competitive, and anti-innovative manner by incumbents.

It’s been ruled unconstitutional to gps track sex offenders but suddenly was okay to track the services of the disabled because there were no safe guards or specific modifications put in place to protect consumers from harm or data breaches.
Standards that have not completed robust real-world testing are not suitable for mandated use in health policy.
We feel that there is already too much governmental involvement in insurance and payment for medical services, and we oppose adoption or imposition of new electronic standards...
One-sided requirements...will not result in the broad adoption necessary to achieve the benefits of ePA.
The AMA cautions against requiring ePA use to 'work out the kinks' by codifying it in regulation now. This would shift industry focus to federal program compliance instead of PA reform.

We strongly advise the ONC to refrain from being overly prescriptive... By focusing on the intent and not any specific technology, ONC can capitalize on, rather than inhibit, innovations already happening in this space.
ONC must exercise caution around certification requirements that unintentionally determine business models for the industry and risk stifling innovative approaches.

At AAPS we have heard multiple times about electronic health record (EHR) systems being hijacked or down for days. These problems do not occur with such severity when records are on paper or handled by phone.
Many rural physicians do not have adequate broadband access and small practices do not have the necessary technology to support mandated electronic prior authorization.
[A premature standard] would have negative administrative, financial, and clinical consequences downstream on clinicians.

10.1.themeDescription: Expanding Scope Beyond the Initial Authorization

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus among all stakeholder groups that the functional scope of electronic prior authorization (ePA) must be expanded beyond the initial request to be meaningful. Commenters advocate for a holistic, "soup-to-nuts" automated system that covers the entire pre-service workflow, including electronic appeals, peer-to-peer scheduling, status tracking, and episode-based authorizations for a full course of treatment. The central tension is not whether to expand the scope, but the pace and technical strategy for implementation, with providers demanding a single, comprehensive solution to avoid creating new digital bottlenecks that would offer only marginal value.

consensusPoints.text: An End-to-End Workflow is Essential. Nearly all commenters assert that automating only the initial PA submission provides limited or insufficient value. They call for a holistic system that covers the entire "pre-service requirements" or "soup-to-nuts" workflow. The American Medical Association (AMA) demands a "seamless, 'soup-to-nuts' automated PA process without relying on ancillary components". OneHealthPort warns that a limited solution will be of "insufficient value to provider organizations" and that "cobbling together and integrating a variety of solutions...only adds to administrative complexity". Cerner Corporation advocates for expanding the scope to cover entire "'pre-service requirements' workflows and downstream secondary processes".

exceptions.text: CyncHealth, while supporting expansion, suggests standardizing the initial authorization form first before tackling more complex areas like concurrent authorizations.

consensusPoints.text: Incorporate Electronic Appeals and Peer-to-Peer (P2P) Processes. A strong majority of commenters, particularly provider organizations and advocacy groups, insist that the ePA system must include functionality for submitting appeals and scheduling P2P reviews electronically. The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) recommends ONC "consider the need for capabilities to support electronic appeal and peer-to-peer review". The Michael J. Fox Foundation urges ONC to "incorporate functionalities that allow providers to directly appeal PA denials for patients", citing OIG data that 75% of MA plan denials are overturned on appeal. The Connecticut Hospital Association specifically requests the ability to "enable online scheduling of a peer-to-peer review".

Authorize Episodes of Care, Not Just Single Services. Many stakeholders, especially those in specialty care and large health systems, recommend moving towards authorizations that cover an entire episode or course of treatment. The Alliance of Dedicated Cancer Centers (ADCC) states that an authorization should cover "all components of care outlined in the guidelines and not require an authorization for each component of the care plan". WEDI recommends developing the capability to "‘bundle’ PAs for episode-related services into one transaction”. Cerner suggests considering prior authorization for a "'course of treatment' or a bundle of services for a condition/episode (e.g., joint replacement with surgery, inpatient stay, post-acute care, rehab, durable medical equipment)".

Provide Real-Time, Bi-Directional Status Updates and Notifications. There is broad agreement from providers, payers, and patient advocates on the need for transparent, real-time status updates throughout the PA lifecycle for all parties involved. Cleveland Clinic advocates for "real-time notification of PA request status, allowing patients to view status updates...directly within their electronic medical record portal". Cigna recommends adding the ability for patients to "query ePA status for visibility and transparency". Anthem notes the need for bi-directional communication to flag where required information is missing to avoid manual processes and delays.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: broad agreement from providers, payers, and patient advocates

areasOfDebate.description: The central debate is whether to mandate a single, comprehensive ePA module covering all functionalities at once, or to allow for a phased, modular implementation starting with core features.

positions.label: All-in-One Mandate
positions.stance: Require a single, comprehensive health IT module that includes all ePA functionalities from start to finish.
positions.supportLevel: Strongly supported by major provider advocacy groups like the AMA and some health IT consultants.
keyArguments: Ensures a seamless "soup-to-nuts" experience for physicians
prevents confusion and added costs from "mix-and-match" modules
forces vendors and payers to support the complete workflow

positions.label: Phased & Modular
positions.stance: Develop a core ePA system first and add new functional modules (like appeals, P2P, etc.) over time.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed by at least one Regional Health Information Organization (RHIO).
keyArguments: Allows for a faster launch of core functionality without being delayed by more complex components
provides flexibility to add new capabilities as standards and technology mature
avoids a one-size-fits-all approach

areasOfDebate.description: The key point of contention is the timing for mandating electronic transfer of prior authorizations between payers, balancing the need for care continuity against the maturity of technical standards.

positions.label: Implement Now
positions.stance: Mandate standards for electronically transferring active and pending prior authorizations when a patient changes health plans.
positions.supportLevel: Advocated by large health systems, provider organizations, and the EHR Association.
keyArguments: Critical for ensuring continuity of care for patients with ongoing treatment needs
reduces immense administrative burden and care disruption caused by re-authorizing existing care plans

positions.label: Defer for Now
positions.stance: Acknowledge the need but argue that the standards are not yet mature enough for regulation.
positions.supportLevel: Position held by a major payer association.
keyArguments: The Implementation Guides for payer-to-payer exchange are underdeveloped and don't yet account for complex data like pending decisions or attached clinical documentation
premature certification would be ineffective

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Fragmented workflows that require separate authorizations for each step of a care plan; care delays and negative outcomes for patients; massive administrative burden from manual appeals, P2P scheduling, and tracking status.
specificPoints: They emphasize the real-world clinical friction points, such as an MRI being denied because the authorized code (w/o contrast) differs slightly from the clinically necessary one (w/ contrast), or the 'snowbird' problem where patients lose their authorizations when moving temporarily.
Proposed solutions: Episode-based or 'family of codes' authorizations; a centralized 'work queue' in the EHR to manage all PA tasks; transferable authorizations between facilities and plans.

Key examples: UCSF CDHI provides detailed end-to-end workflow descriptions for DME, specialty medications, and procedures, highlighting the need for a seamless process from prescribing to renewal and appeals.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member burnout due to administrative hassles; patient harm from delayed or abandoned care; lack of transparency in payer decision-making and appeals processes.
specificPoints: They aggregate the individual pain points of their members into strong, unified policy demands, such as the AMA's call for a single 'soup-to-nuts' module and ASCO's focus on the frustration of useless 'pending' statuses.

Proposed solutions: Mandating the full end-to-end workflow, including renewals and appeals; requiring specific denial reasons; bundling PAs for episodes of care; creating public reporting metrics on PA approvals and denials.
Key examples: The Alliance of Dedicated Cancer Centers (ADCC) highlights how cancer care plans are broken into dozens of separate PA requests, delaying time-sensitive treatment.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Vendors, Consultants & Standards Bodies
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The need for clear, stable, and comprehensive technical standards (like HL7 FHIR IGs); ensuring solutions are practical and integrate into diverse clinical workflows; avoiding regulations that lock in suboptimal processes.
specificPoints: They focus on the 'how' – the technical architecture, APIs, and data standards needed to build a holistic system. They identify gaps in current standards, such as support for inpatient admission extensions (MCG Health) or payer-initiated workflows (Telligen).

Proposed solutions: Adopting the full scope of Da Vinci IGs; creating bi-directional APIs for payers to request information; standardizing a 'work queue' concept within EHRs; enabling consumer-facing apps to query status.

Key examples: Massachusetts Health Data Consortium / NEHEN provides a detailed recommendation for a certified 'referral/authorization module' that functions as an active work queue dashboard within the EHR.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Receiving incomplete submissions that require manual follow-up; managing complex requests with multiple line items or exceptions; enabling bi-directional communication to efficiently request missing clinical data.
specificPoints: While supporting automation, payers emphasize their need to request information, not just receive it. They see the ePA system as a two-way street. Anthem highlights the need to handle payer-specific business edits and member matching issues.

Proposed solutions: Bi-directional APIs allowing payers to query provider systems (Medical Mutual of Ohio); ability to partially approve/deny requests (UnitedHealth Group); patient notification features (Cigna).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: All utilization management processes (not just PA) are barriers to care; patients are often left out of the loop and burdened by appeals; lack of transparency.
specificPoints: These groups push the definition of the problem. PatientRightsAdvocate.org argues that the scope must include concurrent and retrospective reviews, not just prior authorization.

Proposed solutions: Mandate electronic appeals processes that are easy for patients and providers to use; provide patients with direct access to PA status; include formal legal notices within the electronic workflow (CalHHS).

noteworthyInsights.insight: "Family of Codes" as a Simple Fix: CGH Medical Center offers a brilliantly simple and concrete solution: if a specific CPT code is approved (e.g., MRI without contrast), all codes in that "family" (e.g., MRI with contrast) should also be considered approved. They claim this "is the number one item that could improve the prior authorization process."

The "Snowbird" Problem: Moffitt Cancer Center gives a powerful human-centered example of system failure: "snowbirds" who travel south for cancer treatment find their existing authorizations are facility-specific and non-transferable, creating immense barriers to continuing care.

Partial Automation Adds Complexity: OneHealthPort makes the crucial counterintuitive point that "cobbling together and integrating a variety of solutions to meet all of the pre-service steps only adds to administrative complexity and the associated cost burden," challenging the notion that any automation is better than none.

Beyond "Prior" Authorization: PatientRightsAdvocate.org cogently argues that focusing only on prior authorization is too narrow, as concurrent and retrospective reviews are functionally similar barriers to care and should be included in any holistic solution.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-Type Patterns: Large, integrated health systems (UCSF, Providence, BJC HealthCare) and specialty cancer centers (Moffitt, ADCC) are the most forceful advocates for a comprehensive, episode-based workflow. Their experience managing complex, multi-stage care journeys gives them a clearer view of the failures of a piecemeal system.

Unintended Consequences: Multiple commenters implicitly or explicitly warn of the unintended consequence of partial automation: creating a new, frustrating digital bottleneck. Automating only the initial PA request could accelerate denials, forcing providers into an even more clogged and frustrating manual appeals process, ultimately worsening the problem.

keyQuotations.quote: [Automating only one piece] is unacceptable to assume physicians will 'mix and match' modules. Physicians need assurance that EHRs support a seamless, 'soup-to-nuts' automated PA process without relying on ancillary components.
Cobbling together and integrating a variety of solutions to meet all of the pre-service steps only adds to administrative complexity and the associated cost burden.
Without an improvement in HIPAA related items, this [authorizing a family of codes] is the number one item that could improve the prior authorization process and reduce costs to the system.
It is critical to recognize that submission of the actual prior authorization request is just one step in a series of steps in a workflow to get a patient on an appropriate therapy or treatment plan.
The authorization should cover all components of care outlined in the guidelines and not require an authorization for each component of the care plan.

Many payers give status updates that simply read, 'pending,' but with no indication of what further information might be needed for a final decision. An explanation of the cause of the delay...would also help to streamline the process and potentially decrease the number of denials and appeals.

We seek an end-to-end solution allowing a paperless, transparent ability for a provider to: Prescribe a diabetes device. See the patient’s share of cost estimate. Electronically submit...necessary supporting documentation...Receive a response with transparent reason for approval/denial. Have transparency into expirations.
The RFI’s exclusive focus on prior authorization, to the exclusion of other utilization management processes, is a significant limitation that must be addressed in rulemaking.

Lack of open integration unnecessarily increases administrative costs within the healthcare ecosystem, adds to the hours healthcare workers must work to complete a task, adding to burnout in the industry and most importantly delays care for patients.

Strongly recommend EHRs adopt a referral/PA module approach with functionality to support intake, update, sending, and historical record keeping of all Referral/PA transactions. This is highly suggested for usability, management, and coordination.

10.2.themeDescription: Role of Third-Party and Non-Prescribing Submitters

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment overwhelmingly supports expanding the set of authorized users of the electronic Prior Authorization (ePA) system beyond the ordering physician to reduce administrative burden and prevent care delays. A strong majority of stakeholders—including clinical labs, medical device manufacturers, specialty providers, and technology vendors—advocate for a flexible, distributed model where various entities can initiate or contribute to PA requests. The primary point of contention comes from DMEPOS suppliers, who argue that the ordering prescriber must remain the sole driver of the process. The dominant recommendations focus on technical and regulatory changes to enable this expanded access, such as creating new registration pathways for third parties, standardizing how to identify the correct Third-Party Administrator (TPA), and ensuring open APIs for integration.

consensusPoints.text: The ePA Ecosystem Must Accommodate Third-Party Administrators (TPAs) and Utilization Management Organizations (UMOs). A strong majority of commenters, including providers, payers, and standards bodies, agree that the current system inadequately handles situations where payers delegate PA to external entities. This creates confusion and process failures. For example, OneHealthPort noted it is "not always clear when a TPA is involved or how to interact with them". UnitedHealth Group recommended that systems must have the ability for providers to "verify which third-party administrator (TPA)/payer is responsible for coverage". WEDI identified handling designated UMOs as a "missing functional capability".

The Entire Care Team Needs Visibility into PA Status. There is broad agreement that access to query the status of a PA request should not be limited to the original submitter. Rendering providers, facilities, and other involved parties need the ability to check on pending requests to coordinate care effectively. For example, UnitedHealth Group stated the system should "allow any involved care team members to query the payer on the status of a PA request". OneHealthPort recommended that the "ordering provider or the rendering facility, as well as the rendering provider, [be able to] query a payer’s system for updates".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A diverse group of stakeholders, from payers to specialty providers, endorsed this view.

areasOfDebate.topic: Locus of Control for PA Initiation
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centered on which entity should be ultimately responsible for initiating and submitting the prior authorization request.

positions.label: Broad Delegation
positions.stance: Allow a wide range of third parties (labs, manufacturers, vendors, patients, support staff) to initiate or submit PA information directly.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, including all labs, device manufacturers, tech vendors, and most specialty providers.
keyArguments: Reduces Physician Burden: Alleviates the crushing administrative workload on clinicians and their staff.
Improves Efficiency: Leverages the expertise and resources of specialized entities who are better equipped to handle PAs for their specific products or services.
Prevents Care Delays: Avoids critical delays, such as when labs must wait for a provider to act before testing a time-sensitive specimen.

positions.label: Strict Gatekeeping
positions.stance: The ordering prescriber must be the sole initiator and driver of the PA request.
positions.supportLevel: A distinct minority view, articulated powerfully by the DMEPOS supplier community.
keyArguments: Clinical Accountability: The prescriber holds the complete clinical record and is best positioned to affirm medical necessity.
Prevents Burden Shifting: Avoids placing suppliers "in the middle" of a process where they lack the necessary clinical information, leading to new inefficiencies.
Clear Responsibility: Maintains a clear line of responsibility for the PA decision and documentation.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Being prohibited by payer rules from initiating or tracking PAs; significant care delays while waiting for providers to act; risk to specimen integrity.
specificPoints: The tangible risk of having to "perform the ordered tests absent knowledge of whether the payer will authorize them" rather than "compromise specimen integrity".
Mandate that any electronic solution must "permit laboratories to submit prior authorization requests directly" and query for status updates.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Being technically locked out of ePA portals due to registration requirements (NPI/Tax ID); being forced to use inefficient fax and phone calls, which slows patient access.
specificPoints: They provide essential "prior authorization support services" for their complex and innovative technologies, but are barred from using modern tools.
HHS should "provide a mechanism for medical device manufacturers to utilize web portals to submit prior authorization requests" by allowing registration with an identifier other than an NPI.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring the ePA standard supports an open ecosystem of "best-of-breed" solutions, not just native EHR functionality; achieving true interoperability requires open APIs and EHR write-back capabilities.
specificPoints: Clearinghouses like The SSI Group are "ideally situated to facilitate the exchange" due to their existing connections between all providers and plans.
AI vendors like Olive AI argue they can manage high-volume, complex PA workflows more effectively than general EHRs.
Proposed solutions include certifying third-party vendors and their technology and ensuring APIs allow third-party apps to access necessary data and write PA status back to the EHR.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense administrative burden of PA; care delays caused by process friction; specific operational roadblocks like out-of-network portal lockouts.
specificPoints: Specialized cancer centers like Dana-Farber are disproportionately affected by being unable to submit ePA for out-of-network patients, forcing them to use "antiquated communication methods (e.g., phone or fax)".
Commenters like CHIME highlighted the exclusion of entire care settings like Long-term Post-Acute Care (LTPAC).

Proposed solutions include allowing out-of-network providers access to portals, permitting submission by paraprofessionals under physician supervision, and even empowering patients to initiate their own PAs.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring operational feasibility and integration across a complex network of proprietary systems and delegated entities (UMOs/TPAs).
specificPoints: As operators of TPAs (e.g., Cigna's eviCore), they acknowledge the need for the system to formally support and integrate these third-party utilization managers.

Proposed solutions include building functionality to help providers like UnitedHealth Group identify the correct TPA for a given service and allowing broad status query capabilities for the entire care team.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Specimen Integrity Imperative: The argument from clinical laboratories (Labcorp, ACLA) that PA delays force them to choose between risking non-payment or compromising a patient's biological specimen is a uniquely powerful and concrete example of how administrative friction can lead to direct clinical consequences.

A Simple Technical Barrier with Huge Impact: AdvaMed’s identification of the NPI/Tax ID portal registration requirement as the specific mechanism locking out an entire class of support services is a highly actionable insight. It pinpoints a discrete technical design choice that creates a major system-wide bottleneck.

Flipping the Script on Data Exchange: RadNet's suggestion that providers or their agents could "create their own APIs that contain prior-authorization documentation for the payer to query" is an innovative proposal that reverses the typical payer-centric data flow and could give providers more control over their data.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Specialization Drives the Need for Delegation: The most urgent calls for expanded third-party access come from stakeholders dealing with specialized or complex care: advanced imaging (RadNet), cancer treatment (Dana-Farber), innovative medical devices (AdvaMed), and specialty lab tests (ACLA, Labcorp). Their experiences suggest a one-size-fits-all, physician-only model is least effective at the cutting edge of medicine.

The "TPA Black Box": A consistent pattern of frustration emerges around the lack of transparency and standardized processes for dealing with delegated entities like TPAs and UMOs. Commenters across the spectrum (providers, payers, standards bodies) see this as a critical failure point that any new regulation must solve.

keyQuotations.quote: [T]he current design of prior authorization web portals requires users to register under a physician or facility Tax ID and National Provider Identifier (NPI). This registration requirement does not allow third parties to register...and forces prior authorization support service staff to continue using outdated and inefficient methods...such as faxing and phone calls.

Requiring a prior authorization request to originate with a healthcare provider instead of a laboratory can result in delays...the laboratory is compelled to wait for the healthcare provider to submit the request and communicate the approval prior to completing the test.
Rather than compromise specimen integrity, laboratories act in the best interest of the patient and perform the ordered tests absent knowledge of whether the payer will authorize them.

To implement an effective PA program, the PA request must be initiated and completed by the ordering prescriber...CMS should avoid positioning suppliers in the middle and make it clear the ordering prescriber is responsible.

Because of their unique position as intermediaries between virtually all healthcare providers and plans, clearinghouses are ideally situated to facilitate the exchange of healthcare information required for prior authorization.
ONC should ensure medical ePA functionality is available both native and adjunct to the core HIS/EHR system...Innovation should not be limited to existing EHR vendor systems.
[It is] not always clear when a TPA is involved or how to interact with them for utilization management of specialty services.
The submission of prior-authorization documentation and supporting information should not be limited to any specific entity or technology.

2.1.themeDescription: Endorsement of Da Vinci IGs as the FHIR Standard

sections.executiveSummary: An overwhelming and cross-stakeholder consensus emerged in strong support of adopting the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) as the official ePA standard. Payers, providers, and technology vendors alike view the IGs as mature, tested, and ready for immediate implementation, representing the only viable path forward for a modern, end-to-end electronic prior authorization workflow. The dominant recommendation is for ONC to move swiftly to incorporate these specific IGs into the Health IT Certification Program without delay or deviation, as any alternative path would disrupt significant industry investment and stall progress on reducing administrative burden.

consensusPoints.text: The Da Vinci IGs are the correct standard for ePA. A vast majority of commenters, including payers, providers, vendors, and standards bodies, explicitly endorsed the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs (CRD, DTR, PAS) as the right choice. They are seen as providing the necessary specifications for a modern, automated ePA workflow. For example, UnitedHealth Group states, "We support development of Da Vinci IGs and do not recommend other approaches be considered or adopted." Lantana Consulting Group "unequivocally endorses the HL7 Prior Authorization Implementation Guide (PAS IG)" for all ePA functionalities. Epic affirms that "The three FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs for prior authorization (CRD, DTR, PAS) enable end-to-end ePA workflows."

The Da Vinci IGs are mature and ready for adoption now. A strong majority of commenters, particularly technology vendors and payers who have invested in the technology, asserted that the IGs have been sufficiently tested, balloted, and piloted to be considered ready for production use and inclusion in certification criteria. They argue forcefully against any delays. For example, Humana believes the "Da Vinci IGs are mature enough for adoption as part of HIT certification criteria," noting they have undergone years of Connectathon testing. Mettle Solutions states, "The current Da Vinci IGs ARE ready for adoption by ONC into the certification program. ONC should proceed with all due haste in order to minimize provider burden ASAP." Cohere Health, which uses the IGs in production, states, "It is Cohere’s view that the IGs have reached a point of stability and that they are a useful guide for industry."

The three IGs (CRD, DTR, PAS) form a complete, end-to-end solution. Most stakeholders view the combination of the three IGs as a comprehensive package that addresses the entire ePA process, from discovering requirements to submitting documentation and receiving a decision. For instance, The California Health and Human Services Agency notes that CRD, DTR, and PAS would collectively "create a complete prior authorization system." The Electronic Health Record Association (EHRA) agrees the IGs "provide a good starting point for outlining the flow and interactions for an end-to-end PA workflow." Anthem states the IGs "have the potential to create an end-to-end ePA standard."

areasOfDebate.description: While endorsement is the defining characteristic of this theme, subtle disagreements exist regarding the specifics of implementation and certification, particularly the pace of adoption.

positions.label: All-at-Once
positions.stance: Mandate all three IGs (CRD, DTR, PAS) for immediate adoption.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal contingent of technology vendors and large payers.
keyArguments: The IGs are ready and work as a system; delaying any part diminishes the overall value and prolongs provider burden.
The industry needs a clear, decisive signal.

positions.label: Phased Rollout
positions.stance: Implement the IGs in stages, starting with the simplest.
positions.supportLevel: A few commenters, including a key multi-stakeholder advisory body.
keyArguments: A phased approach is more practical.
CRD is a simple query and a viable starting point that can deliver immediate value while the industry prepares for the more complex DTR.

areasOfDebate.description: Disagreements exist on whether the current IGs are fully complete and sufficient as-is, or serve as a strong foundation that will require future work.

positions.label: Sufficient as-is
positions.stance: The IGs sufficiently capture the needed specifications and are ready for national rollout.
positions.supportLevel: Most commenters, including payers and technology developers with direct experience.
keyArguments: The IGs have been extensively tested and are being used in production; they are robust enough to be considered a national standard.

positions.label: Good Foundation
positions.stance: The IGs are an appropriate base but will likely require future augmentation.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller number of commenters, primarily large provider systems.
keyArguments: The IGs are a great starting point but may need additional work to support the full complexity of "connected process automation" in diverse, real-world clinical environments.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Achieving operational efficiency, modernizing technology infrastructure, and establishing a clear, predictable standard for interoperability with providers.
specificPoints: They emphasize that FHIR is a modern technical framework, making it easier and cheaper to find qualified developers and use available tooling.
They also note that the IGs can build on existing investments made for the Patient Access API rule.
They offer unanimous and strong support for ONC to adopt the Da Vinci IGs immediately and without considering alternatives.

A key concern is that proposing certification criteria based only on the FHIR Release 4 base standard without the specific Da Vinci IGs would be functionally useless and impractical for certifying workflows.

Key examples of support include UnitedHealth Group and Humana who both state the IGs are mature and ready for adoption, while AHIP calls them a "reasonable floor for consistency and interoperability."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing the immense administrative burden of prior authorization, eliminating care delays that harm patients, and decreasing clinician burnout. Any delay in adopting a functional, automated standard directly translates to continued administrative waste and dangerous delays in patient access to care.
specificPoints: They bring the frontline experience of workflow disruption. Allina Health uniquely argues that payers should be required to pull data from provider FHIR APIs first, shifting the burden of data aggregation from the provider to the payer.
There is strong support for the implementation of the Da Vinci IGs as a concrete way to solve their daily operational pain points.
UnityPoint Health supports the IGs as they "appropriately encompass standards that common EHR vendors are driving towards."
The American Hospital Association believes the IGs will "promote a substantial reduction in the time to complete and successfully transmit requests."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Having a stable, single, and clear technical standard to build against; avoiding wasted investment; and ensuring their products can meet certification and customer needs.
specificPoints: They provide evidence of the IGs' technical readiness from pilots and connectathons. They also warn of the significant disruption and cost that would be imposed on early adopters if ONC were to pivot away from the Da Vinci standards.
They overwhelmingly recommend the immediate adoption of the Da Vinci IGs into certification criteria.

A major concern is disruption to industry momentum. Choosing a different standard would invalidate years of collaborative work and significant financial investment. Cohere Health, an early adopter, warns that moving away from the IGs "would be very disruptive."
Mettle Solutions states they have "already built a payer FHIR Server that complies with the Da Vinci IGs."
Cerner affirms the IGs provide "substantial initial guidance" for the core ePA flow.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Validating and capitalizing on years of collaborative, multi-stakeholder standards development work; ensuring the industry moves forward with a modern, tested framework.
specificPoints: As the developers and facilitators of the IGs, they provide the most detailed commentary on the standards' maturity, balloting status, and testing history, acting as the ultimate subject matter experts.
They uniformly recommend that ONC leverage the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs for certification.
To prevent interoperability failures, it's recommended that ONC should specify the same version of the IGs for both payer and provider health IT systems.
The HL7 Da Vinci Project states the IGs "are considered valuable and actionable in their current state."
HL7 notes the PAS IG "has been extensively tested in numerous connectathons, indicating its readiness for production implementation."

noteworthyInsights.insight: Several commenters, including Aneesh Chopra & Brendan Keeler and Medical Mutual of Ohio, noted that the significant payer investment in FHIR servers for the Patient Access API rule can be leveraged to accelerate the adoption of the Da Vinci IGs, lowering the cost and complexity of implementation.

Several commenters, including Aneesh Chopra & Brendan Keeler and Medical Mutual of Ohio, noted that the significant payer investment in FHIR servers for the Patient Access API rule can be leveraged to accelerate the adoption of the Da Vinci IGs, lowering the cost and complexity of implementation.

Anthem articulated a compelling business case for FHIR that goes beyond simple interoperability: "FHIR is built on a modern technology framework... finding qualified technical staff for implementation should be easier... the tooling needed for FHIR is widely available and often free," which lowers costs for everyone.
Real-world implementation success provides powerful evidence of readiness. Cohere Health reports using the PAS and DTR IGs "hundreds of times each day" in production.
Real-world implementation success provides powerful evidence of readiness. HealthHelp has experience transitioning from legacy X12 to the Da Vinci PAS IG.

Allina Health proposed a paradigm shift: instead of providers being responsible for pushing documentation, payers should be required to first pull available data from provider FHIR APIs, a direct consequence of Cures Act investments that has not yet been fully realized.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The most prominent pattern is the remarkable consensus across disparate and often adversarial stakeholder groups. Payers, providers, and vendors are all aligned in their endorsement of the Da Vinci IGs as the single path forward. This level of agreement is rare in healthcare policy and signals strong industry readiness.

Stakeholders who are most deeply involved in the development and early implementation of the IGs (e.g., HL7, Da Vinci, Mettle, Cohere, major payers) express the highest degree of confidence, declaring the standards "ready now." Those with broader, more complex implementation environments (e.g., large health systems, EHR vendor associations) tend to frame them as a "good foundation," suggesting an awareness of the challenges in deployment at scale.

The comments show a clear convergence of industry efforts over several years. Mentions of extensive testing at connectathons, the Da Vinci HIPAA Exception, and active pilots demonstrate that the endorsement is not theoretical but is based on years of collaborative, practical work.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] current Da Vinci IGs ARE ready for adoption by ONC into the certification program. ONC should proceed with all due haste in order to minimize provider burden ASAP.
If ONC were to move away from this approach, it would be very disruptive to early adopters who have already invested in this standard.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Cohere Health, Inc., Health IT Vendor

keyQuotations.quote: We believe that health plans should be required to attempt to retrieve clinical data needed to approve a prior authorization request by using the USCDI FHIR APIs that providers have already implemented in their systems. This will minimize the need for duplicative documentation and reduce redundancy...
We support development of Da Vinci IGs and do not recommend other approaches be considered or adopted.
The HL7 FHIR Da Vinci PAS IG has been extensively tested in numerous connectathons, indicating its readiness for production implementation, even with the X12 translation component.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Health Level Seven International (HL7), Standards Development Organization

keyQuotations.quote: The three DaVinci IGs (CRD, DTR, PAS) represent a reasonable floor for consistency and interoperability.
Streamlining the prior authorization process through FHIR-enabled APIs will only succeed if these requirements are built into certification.
keyQuotations.sourceType: American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA), Provider Association

keyQuotations.quote: FHIR is built on a modern technology framework... finding qualified technical staff for implementation should be easier than with competing frameworks... Use of industry-support standards will lower the barriers for entry of new, innovative developers, and potentially lowers the costs to both providers and payers.
The substantial work by DaVinci workgroups and industry support for the IGs represents a tipping point for further adoption in operating rules and certification rules.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Massachusetts Health Data Consortium / NEHEN, Regional Consortium

keyQuotations.quote: Leveraging the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs is an opportunity to move forward with FHIR standards in order to work to address the primary drivers of PA burden, while taking advantage of the experience with and investment in existing standards like the X12 278.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Health Level Seven International (HL7), Standards Development Organization

2.2.themeDescription: Concerns about Da Vinci IG Maturity and Readiness

sections.executiveSummary: An overwhelming consensus exists among stakeholders that the HL7 Da Vinci Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) are not sufficiently mature, tested, or stable for a federal mandate at this time. The core tension is not if the IGs are immature—virtually all agree they are—but how to respond: the vast majority advocate for delaying any mandate in favor of extensive, real-world pilot testing, while a small but vocal minority argues that a mandate is the necessary catalyst to drive the standards to maturity. The dominant recommendation is to pursue a phased approach, beginning with incentivized pilots to address significant gaps in testing, functionality, and stability before codifying the IGs in regulation.

consensusPoints.text: The Da Vinci IGs are Immature and Not Ready for a Federal Mandate. Nearly all commenters explicitly state the IGs are immature, nascent, not ready for certification, or not ready for widespread adoption. This view is held universally across all stakeholder types, including providers, payers, vendors, and government entities.

exceptions.text: A very small minority (Mettle Solutions, Drummond Group) believe the IGs are sufficiently tested or that a mandate should proceed despite immaturity to accelerate progress.

consensusPoints.text: Current Testing is Insufficient and Overly Reliant on "Happy-Path" Scenarios. A strong majority, particularly provider groups and payers, argue that testing has been limited to controlled "Connectathon" environments that do not reflect the complexities of real-world clinical practice.

exceptions.text: The HL7 Da Vinci Project acknowledges the need for continuous improvement but defends its agile, iterative process as a strength.

consensusPoints.text: The "Standard for Trial Use" (STU) Designation Indicates Immaturity. Many stakeholders from all categories point to the official HL7 "STU" classification as prima facie evidence that the IGs are not finalized and are expected to change, making them unsuitable for regulation.

The IGs Rely on Other Nascent or Immature FHIR Components. A significant number of technically-focused commenters, especially vendors and provider groups, express concern that the Da Vinci IGs' functionality depends on other technologies like CDS Hooks and CQL, which are themselves not widely adopted or mature.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A significant number of technically-focused commenters

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Federal Response to IG Immaturity
areasOfDebate.description: The core debate centers on the correct federal action in response to the acknowledged immaturity of the Da Vinci IGs: whether to delay a mandate to allow for more testing and stabilization, or to implement a mandate precisely to force the standards to mature more quickly.

positions.label: Delay & Test
positions.stance: Postpone any mandate until the IGs have undergone extensive, real-world testing and have reached a stable, near-normative state.
positions.supportLevel: The overwhelming majority of commenters, including nearly all provider groups, payers, and health IT vendors.
keyArguments: Avoids locking in flawed or incomplete standards that will require costly rework.
Prevents worsening provider burden with a half-baked solution.
Ensures the final standard is scalable, secure, and functional beyond simple "happy-path" use cases.

positions.label: Mandate to Mature
positions.stance: Proceed with a mandate to provide a clear market signal and accelerate the maturation process.
positions.supportLevel: A very small but distinct minority, primarily from the Health IT certification and testing sector.
keyArguments: Without a regulatory driver, industry adoption and testing will languish indefinitely.
Past experience shows that delaying a mandate for FHIR APIs led to more problems and non-standard solutions.
A mandate forces the community to collaborate and resolve issues, speeding up the path to maturity.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Mandating immature IGs will worsen provider burden, create new workflow complexities, and delay patient care. They fear technology that only works in "happy-path" scenarios will fail in their complex, real-world environments. Key stakeholders include AMA, AHA, AAFP, Cleveland Clinic, and BJC HealthCare.
specificPoints: Emphasize the human cost of failed technology, connecting it directly to clinician burnout and negative patient outcomes. They are deeply skeptical of lab-tested solutions that haven't been vetted in diverse, resource-constrained practice settings.
Insist on robust, real-world testing in practices of all sizes and specialties before any mandate.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The instability of the IGs creates significant development risk. Constant "breaking" changes between versions will lead to expensive, wasted effort and rework. The lack of clarity on which systems are responsible for which functions complicates development. Key vendors include Epic, Cerner, Allscripts, MEDITECH, and athenahealth.
specificPoints: They focus on the technical and financial unsustainability of building products on a "moving target." They highlight the ripple effect of IG immaturity, such as the dependency on other unstable components like CDS Hooks.
Delay certification until IGs are normative or, at a minimum, allow flexibility in certification to accommodate evolving versions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The IGs are not functionally complete and have not been tested for complex, real-world scenarios. They also highlight the significant, un-tested infrastructure buildout required on their end, arguing that recent work on Patient Access APIs is not a sufficient foundation. Key organizations include AHIP, BCBSA, Cigna, and Anthem.
specificPoints: They point out specific functional gaps, such as the inability to handle multi-part orders or the problematic requirement to map clinical to administrative terminologies.
Defer certification until IGs are tested at scale; name them "provisional" standards to encourage further development without the finality of a rule.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: This group is divided. Some, like CAQH CORE, align with the majority, citing limited operational testing. Others, such as Drummond and AEGIS, see the immaturity differently, arguing from experience that mandates are what create maturity by forcing industry engagement. Key stakeholders include HL7 Da Vinci and the aforementioned organizations.
specificPoints: This group provides the "inside baseball" view on the standards development and adoption lifecycle. HL7 Da Vinci defends its agile process, while others argue a mandate is needed to drive maturity.
Proposed solutions range from "delay and test" to "mandate to mature."

noteworthyInsights.insight: The 'Mandate to Mature' Paradox - Drummond Group offers a compelling counter-argument to the overwhelming consensus. They argue from past experience with Patient Access APIs that delaying a mandate for a standard to mature can be more harmful, as it creates a vacuum filled by non-standard, one-off solutions, ultimately causing more chaos than mandating an immature-but-agreed-upon standard and forcing the community to fix it.

Real-World Complexity Example - Cigna provides a powerful, concrete example of the testing gap, noting that prototypes have focused on simple, single-item orders (like DME), while real-world medicine involves complex, bundled orders (like a specialty drug infusion requiring both medical and pharmacy ePA) that the IGs have not been tested against.

Tangible Technical Hurdles - Dr. Troy Kaji of Contra Costa Health Services moves beyond general complaints to a specific, quantifiable technical burden: "FHIR may need an order of magnitude greater number of certificates... This may make a FHIR pathway require that a given organization have an order of magnitude greater level of resourcing to implement."

It's the Data, Stupid - The Massachusetts Health Data Consortium points out that even with perfect IGs, "Availability of high-quality data from FHIR resources is a greater barrier to fully automated ePA." This highlights that the transport standard's maturity is moot if the underlying clinical data in EHRs isn't structured and available via FHIR.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Experience Correlations: Commenters with direct implementation experience (e.g., Telligen, Voluware, HealthHelp) provide highly specific critiques of IG components, such as the complexity of CQL, inadequate error handling, and shortcomings in synchronous transaction models.

Facility-Type Patterns: Organizations representing safety-net and resource-constrained settings (e.g., OCHIN) express heightened concern, noting that the burden of implementing unvetted and unstable standards will disproportionately fall on providers who lack large IT teams and resources.

Unintended Consequences: A recurring pattern is the fear that a premature mandate will have the opposite of its intended effect: instead of reducing burden, it will increase it by forcing clinicians and developers to grapple with faulty, unstable technology, leading to more workarounds, wasted time, and burnout.

keyQuotations.quote: [We] have some reservations about the readiness of the Da Vinci Implementation Guides (IGs).
The proposed IGs are at the Standard for Trial Use (STU) ballot level and are not yet normative (finalized).
Connectathon testing, which accounts for the vast majority of Da Vinci IG testing, relies on 'happy-path' scripted scenarios and predefined input data.

Such standards need to be evaluated for adequacy (i.e., tested in real-world environments and across a diverse range of setting and scenarios, not just in select connectathons) before being included in a certification program.

It is our opinion that ONC’s past decision to delay FHIR adoption of Patient Access APIs while still requiring non-standardized implementation of APIs caused more problems than adopting an immature standard and working with the community to accelerate the maturity of those standards...
All health IT developers must have complete IGs at the outset; a piecemeal approach would be detrimental.
Limited pilot implementers lead to drastic changes between balloting rounds of the Da Vinci IGs, which requires large development projects for developers.
Promoting or requiring the use of any standard before it is proven successful in the marketplace is problematic and concerning given the scale that the standards would need to be used nationwide...
The use cases that have driven the standards development are an incomplete approximation of the full complexity of prior authorization workflows in actual practice.
Until there are replicated examples of pilot tests that have successfully scaled, these [FHIR-based Da Vinci IGs] are not ready.
In a landscape where most payers have only recently built and implemented Patient Access APIs, it is unreasonable to assume that payers have full FHIR capabilities.
However, we are concerned about the limited number of real-world deployments where these technologies have been market tested.
The most important principle that we promote is to “keep it simple” to get the CRD/DTR/PAS Da Vinci IGs ready for production.
...premature adoption presents its own set of challenges that may be more onerous than the present issues with PA generally.

2.3.themeDescription: Support for Continued Use or Enhancement of X12

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment strongly supports leveraging the existing, HIPAA-mandated ASC X12 278 transaction standard rather than replacing it. There is overwhelming consensus that X12 is the law, industry has invested heavily in it, and any new solution must coexist with or build upon it. The core tension lies not in whether to support X12, but how: one camp advocates for strict enforcement to combat payer non-compliance, while another argues that enforcement is futile without clear implementation guides to prevent the payer-specific customizations that have historically crippled adoption. The dominant recommendation is for a dual-pathway future where X12 remains a supported option alongside emerging FHIR standards, with a critical need for federal action to standardize implementation and ensure interoperability between the old and new frameworks.

consensusPoints.text: The X12 278 standard is the legal requirement and must be supported. A vast majority of commenters from all stakeholder groups (providers, payers, standards bodies, vendors) acknowledge that the X12 278 transaction is the current HIPAA-mandated standard for electronic prior authorization and cannot be disregarded. This is seen as a foundational, non-negotiable reality.

New solutions should not discard existing industry investment in X12. A strong majority, particularly standards bodies, consultants, and large organizations, emphasized that the industry has invested billions of dollars and decades of experience in X12 infrastructure. They argue that a "rip and replace" approach would be disruptive, wasteful, and ignore valuable lessons learned.

X12 should coexist with and be integrated into new FHIR-based workflows. Nearly all commenters agree that the future is not "X12 vs. FHIR" but "X12 and FHIR." The consensus is that new standards must accommodate, translate to, or operate in parallel with X12 to ensure a smooth transition and support all market participants.

Current adoption of X12 is low due to inconsistent implementation and lack of an attachments standard. There is broad agreement that despite being the mandate, X12 278 adoption is poor. Commenters from every sector, including providers (AHA), vendors (Epic), and standards bodies (WEDI), cite payer-specific customizations and the historical failure to name an electronic attachment standard as the primary causes of this failure.

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Best Integrate X12 with Emerging FHIR Standards

positions.label: Mandatory Translation
positions.stance: Future FHIR-based systems must include a certified capability to translate transactions into the HIPAA-mandated X12 278 format and back.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by key standards developers (HL7, Da Vinci Project), consultants (Point-of-Care Partners), and physician advocates (Regulatory Relief Coalition).
keyArguments: This approach honors the legal requirement of HIPAA; it leverages the efficiencies of modern FHIR APIs while respecting existing payer-side investments in X12 infrastructure; it provides a necessary bridge to the future without disrupting the entire ecosystem at once.

positions.label: Parallel Pathways
positions.stance: Allow X12 and FHIR to exist as separate, equally valid pathways, with translation being an optional, potentially separate, certifiable capability.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by regional health exchanges (NEHEN), health IT vendors (EHRA), and implied by others wanting flexibility (Epic).
keyArguments: Forcing translation from FHIR to X12 is inefficient if both trading partners could simply use X12 natively; provider-facing EHRs do not operate in the X12 space, so translation is an intermediary function and should be certified separately; this approach provides maximum flexibility for trading partners to use the method that works best for them.

areasOfDebate.topic: The Primary Reason for X12's Historical Failures

positions.label: Lack of Enforcement
positions.stance: The X12 278 standard is sufficient, but payers have not been required to comply with it, leading to widespread non-adoption and reversion to manual processes.
positions.supportLevel: The dominant view among healthcare providers and their associations.
keyArguments: Payers and their third-party vendors ignore the mandate, creating immense provider burden; the standard provides a uniform mechanism that would work if it were simply enforced; federal agencies should finalize and enforce existing rules before creating new ones.

positions.label: Lack of Guidance
positions.stance: The X12 278 standard is too vague, allowing for payer-specific customizations that have made a single, scalable implementation impossible for providers and vendors.
positions.supportLevel: The dominant view among health IT vendors and some standards bodies.
keyArguments: Without strict implementation guides, each payer creates a unique version of the 278, defeating the purpose of a "standard"; enforcement is meaningless if there isn't a single, clear target to enforce; this problem will persist even with FHIR-to-X12 translation, as the translation would still need to accommodate every payer's custom X12 version.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Payer non-compliance with the existing X12 278 mandate; administrative burden from being forced to use disparate portals and manual processes; care delays caused by payer inaction.
specificPoints: They experience the consequences of non-enforcement daily. They emphasize that payers often use third-party vendors for specialty services who also do not use the 278 transaction, adding another layer of complexity.
Unanimously call for federal agencies (HHS/CMS) to finalize and enforce the requirement for payers to use the 278 transaction for all prior authorization communications.

The Alliance of Dedicated Cancer Centers (ADCC) states, "Many payers do not comply or only comply in part with the prior authorization 278 transactions, requiring providers to resort to phone calls or other portals."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring a smooth evolution of standards; protecting industry investment; achieving true interoperability by bridging administrative (X12) and clinical (FHIR) data.
specificPoints: They provide the "how-to" for interoperability, focusing on the technical mechanics of translation, the importance of operating rules, and the need for harmonization between standards.

Advocate for a hybrid approach that leverages FHIR's strengths while translating to X12 to meet legal requirements and honor existing infrastructure. They champion using operating rules and implementation guides to ensure consistency.
The HL7 Da Vinci Project explains that their FHIR Implementation Guide includes the ability to "translate" FHIR to X12, which "leverages FHIR efficiencies while honoring existing investments."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Implementability and scalability of any proposed standard; the high cost of supporting multiple, inconsistent payer-specific requirements.
