specificPoints: They are on the front lines of building the software and highlight why standards fail in practice. Epic’s point that payer customization—not the standard itself—is the root cause of low adoption is a critical technical insight.

Demanding clear, prescriptive implementation guides for X12 if it is to be a viable option. EHRA recommends that translation be a separately certifiable capability for intermediaries, not a burden on provider-facing EHRs.
Epic states that "Payer customization of X12 278 made its implementation infeasible for providers despite system capabilities," a problem that has existed for over 15 years.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining operational stability; ensuring new standards can integrate with their existing X12-based systems; flexibility in choosing standards based on the business need (e.g., medical vs. pharmacy benefit).
specificPoints: They confirm they are current users of X12 and are invested in its continued use. They advocate for a measured approach that harmonizes, rather than disrupts, their established workflows.

Support for FHIR-to-X12 translation to connect new front-end systems with their existing back-end infrastructure. Advocate for flexibility, allowing the use of different standards (X12, NCPDP) depending on who is processing the request.
Anthem, a major payer, states that a new ePA standard should "support translation for systems still reliant on X12" and that they explicitly "support initial X12 278 translation."

noteworthyInsights.insight: The "Why" of Failure: Epic Systems Corporation provides a crucial insight that reframes the debate: the failure of X12 adoption was not due to a flaw in the standard itself, but the lack of clear implementation guides, which allowed payers to create endless customizations. This made it "infeasible" for vendors to build a scalable solution. This suggests that enforcement alone is insufficient without prescriptive guidance.

Data-Backed Success: The National Uniform Claim Committee (NUCC) points to a National Government Services (NGS) pilot using the X12 275 transaction (for attachments) with an embedded HL7 C-CDA. The results were an 80% reduction in medical review denials and payment time cut from 35 days to 17. This provides powerful evidence that X12 transactions, when implemented well with a clear use case, can be highly effective.

Architectural Distinction: The Electronic Health Record Association (EHRA) highlights that "Provider health IT does not operate in the X12 space; intermediaries may handle translation." This is a key architectural point, suggesting that certification for X12 translation should target intermediaries (clearinghouses) rather than being a required capability within every provider's EHR system.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Provider vs. Vendor Diagnosis: A clear pattern emerges in diagnosing X12's failures. Healthcare Providers overwhelmingly see it as a problem of enforcement ("the will"). Health IT Vendors and some standards bodies see it as a problem of standardization ("the way"). Any successful policy must address both perspectives.

The Central Role of Intermediaries: Multiple commenters (EHRA, RRC, Quest, SSI Group) highlight the critical but often invisible role of clearinghouses and other intermediaries in handling X12 transactions and potential translations. Policy must account for this multi-party reality, not just a simple provider-payer connection.

X12 as a Bridge, Not a Destination: The consistent pattern is a pragmatic acceptance of X12's mandated role. The entire conversation focuses on building a bridge from the current X12-based reality to a future FHIR-enabled ecosystem, with debates centered on the best architectural design for that bridge (e.g., translation vs. parallel tracks).

keyQuotations.quote: [It is] important to enforce the prior authorization 278 transactions related to exchanges between health care parties.

Payer customization of X12 278 made its implementation infeasible for providers despite system capabilities. Industry adoption of X12 278 for prior authorization is limited due to a lack of clear implementation guides and customization by health plans.

Leveraging the HL7 FHIR Da Vinci Burden Reduction IGs is an opportunity to move forward with FHIR standards...while taking advantage of the experience with and investment in existing standards like the X12 278.
The point is to add new functionality, but not remove existing investments, to fill gaps in the existing HIPAA standards suite.

We strongly believe that the requester of the PA should drive the transaction standard used. ...if a health plan is processing the PA for a medical service, then the Accredited Standard Committee (ASC) X12 278 transaction set should be used.
The cost of engaging a clearinghouse to translate X12 into FHIR-enabled processes is costly and may be prohibitive for smaller and mid-size practices.

Provider health IT does not operate in the X12 space; intermediaries may handle translation. We recommend that the ability to translate data in/out of X12 should be a separate certifiable capability...
Any further improvements to the prior authorization process, electronic or otherwise, should build on the 278 transaction sets.

Quest Diagnostics encourages ONC to support development of FHIR to X12 translation that can be published for all the healthcare industry to use, vs. individual efforts which might not be interoperable and could be much more costly to develop.

2.4.themeDescription: Call to Deprecate the X12 Standard for ePA

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus among payers, providers, technology vendors, and standards bodies that the ASC X12 278 transaction standard is an obsolete and inadequate mechanism for modern electronic prior authorization (ePA). Commenters universally cite its low adoption, inability to handle necessary clinical data, and the significant cost, complexity, and data integrity risks of translating between X12 and modern FHIR APIs. The primary debate is not if the industry should move to a full FHIR-based ePA workflow, but how and how quickly. The dominant recommendation is for HHS to take decisive regulatory action—either by amending HIPAA or providing broad, simplified exceptions—to remove the X12 mandate and clear the path for an end-to-end FHIR-based ePA process.

consensusPoints.text: The X12 278 Standard is Obsolete, Inadequate, and Fails to Meet Modern Needs. Commenters describe the X12 278 standard as outdated, inefficient, and fundamentally unsuited for the clinical data exchange required for modern ePA. They characterize it as a "1970s era approach" that is limiting and functionally inadequate. For example, New York Urology Specialists state the existing X12 278 standard is "dysfunctional and insufficient," failing to provide real-time responses or attach required documentation. BCBSA calls the X12N 278 transaction "limiting because it often does not include all the information needed for plans to make PA decisions," which ironically "encourage[s] the use of web portals and fax." The CMS Data Element Library workgroup finds X12 "functionally inadequate for sharing the detailed clinical content necessary to support prior authorizations."

exceptions.text: Medical Mutual of Ohio was the sole commenter expressing a desire not to support additional standards outside of X12 278, representing a clear outlier position.

consensusPoints.text: Translating Between FHIR and X12 is Burdensome, Costly, and Risks Data Integrity. A strong majority of commenters, particularly payers and technology vendors, agree that mandating a translation layer between FHIR and X12 introduces significant problems. They argue it adds unnecessary complexity, development costs, and creates a high risk of losing critical clinical information in the process. For example, Kaiser Permanente is "concerned that supporting both FHIR and X12 standards in the same process... may lead to data integrity issues" and finds it "costly and inefficient... to translate back-and-forth." UnitedHealth Group warns that "there is a risk of data loss or errors when translating between the more robust FHIR standards and the less robust X12 health care transactions." Elsevier states that requiring translation would "create an unnecessary and undesirable level of complexity and burden to PA vendors and other HIT vendors."

The X12 Standard's Low Adoption is Proof of its Failure. Numerous major stakeholders, including large payers and health IT vendors, cite the historically low adoption of the X12 278 transaction as direct evidence of its practical failure in the marketplace. For instance, Anthem, a major payer, states, "in our experience the X12 278 standard has never been fully implemented by the provider community, and is not an end-to-end solution for the prior authorization process." Gainwell Technologies provides a stark anecdote: "in my health plan experience the x12 278 file was never utilized by our providers... I was always told by our Provider Relations team that the 278 is too difficult and faxing or online submission is easier." Cigna notes that "The HIPAA 278 transaction standard has low adoption because it often does not allow for all necessary supporting clinical information."

areasOfDebate.description: While consensus against X12 is strong, minor divergences exist on the strategy for transitioning away from it.

positions.label: Immediate Replacement
positions.stance: HHS should act decisively to eliminate the X12 requirement for ePA and replace it with a FHIR-based standard, ending support for a failed legacy system.
positions.supportLevel: A significant cohort, including major trade associations and technology innovators.
keyArguments: Continuing to support X12 in any form wastes resources, stifles innovation, and perpetuates a broken system; a clean break is needed to realize the full benefits of FHIR.

positions.label: Phased Sunset
positions.stance: HHS should allow for a transitional period where both X12 and FHIR can be used, while creating policies and exceptions that strongly favor FHIR to ensure its eventual, natural dominance.
positions.supportLevel: Several large, integrated health systems and major payers.
keyArguments: Acknowledges that legacy systems require time to adapt; an interim period with enforcement discretion prevents disruption while still steering the industry toward the modern standard.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Provider abrasion caused by X12's limitations (driving use of portals/fax); data integrity risks and inefficiencies of FHIR-X12 translation; X12's inability to carry sufficient clinical data, which undermines the goal of automation.
specificPoints: As the recipients of PA requests, they have a unique view into the low adoption and poor data quality of the X12 278 transaction. It is highly significant that these organizations are among the most vocal in calling for the deprecation of a mandated HIPAA transaction standard.
Overwhelmingly support regulatory change (amending HIPAA or providing broad exceptions) to allow for an end-to-end FHIR ePA workflow.

Cigna and AHIP both explicitly call for policy changes to allow PA to occur "completely within the FHIR framework, without HIPAA X12 transactions." Anthem notes X12 has "never been fully implemented by the provider community."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The high cost, proprietary nature, and technical complexity of implementing and maintaining X12 and the associated FHIR translation logic.
specificPoints: They emphasize the practical, on-the-ground difficulties of working with X12. MEDITECH notes that provider-facing health IT "does not operate in the X12 transaction space." Voluware calls X12 the "most expensive and least flexible way" to exchange PA data.
Urge ONC not to include FHIR-to-X12 translation as a requirement in any certification criteria, and to let the market (e.g., clearinghouses) handle translation if needed, separate from core EHRs.
Telligen argues forcing X12 compliance "will hinder interoperability progress." 1upHealth claims there is "no valid reason other than incumbency protection" for the translation requirement.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The fundamental architectural mismatch between a batch-file standard (X12) and a modern, real-time API standard (FHIR). The proprietary nature of X12 hinders open development and testing.
specificPoints: They speak with authority on the technical design of the standards. The HL7 Da Vinci Project clarifies their FHIR IG is explicitly designed to function without X12 if the requirement were removed.
Change HIPAA law to remove the X12 requirement, or at minimum, grant broad, easily accessible exceptions for FHIR-based ePA.

The CMS Data Element Library workgroup provides a unique insight that the proprietary nature of X12 prevents non-paying implementers from participating effectively in connectathons, hindering IG refinement. WEDI urges simplifying the HIPAA exception process.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The daily dysfunction of current standards, data loss during translation, and the inefficiency of supporting multiple, incompatible workflows.
specificPoints: They experience the direct impact of failed standards on their administrative workload and patient care.
A move to a new, centralized, real-time API-based system to replace fragmented and failing standards.

New York Urology Specialists describes how X12 278 "fail[s] routinely due to lack of standardization across health plans." Cleveland Clinic expresses concern about "the risk of key information being lost during the translation process."

noteworthyInsights.insight: The proprietary nature of X12 hinders development. The CMS Data Element Library workgroup points out that because X12 is a proprietary, paid standard, developers who have not licensed it cannot see the codes being tested during industry connectathons, making it impossible to effectively test and refine FHIR-to-X12 mapping, thereby hampering open, collaborative development.

The anecdote from Gainwell Technologies that their own provider relations team consistently reported that providers find the X12 278 "too difficult and faxing or online submission is easier" is a powerful, ground-level indictment of the standard's failure to deliver on its promise.

Christopher Gracon provides a unique regulatory analysis, arguing that requiring a payer to accept a non-standard transaction like a FHIR-based PAS could "break" the logic of existing HIPAA rules (45 CFR 162.923a), which place the onus of translation on the entity initiating the non-standard transaction.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A clear pattern emerges where the type of critique varies by the commenter's role. Technology vendors and standards bodies focus on the technical flaws (proprietary nature, data loss, architectural mismatch). Payers and providers focus on the operational failures (low adoption, driving manual workflows, administrative burden). This alignment across different perspectives creates a comprehensive picture of X12's shortcomings.

Multiple commenters, including BCBSA and Cigna, identify a key unintended consequence: the inadequacy of the mandated X12 278 standard has directly contributed to the proliferation of proprietary payer portals and a reliance on fax machines, fragmenting workflows and increasing provider burden—the exact opposite of what a universal standard is supposed to achieve.

keyQuotations.quote: [X12] solidif[ies] us in a 1970s era approach to communication which will slow developers that hope to make an impact on the cost curve or improve care.
The HIPAA 278 transaction standard has low adoption because it often does not allow for all necessary supporting clinical information.

Anecdotally, in my health plan experience the x12 278 file was never utilized by our providers... I was always told by our Provider Relations team that the 278 is too difficult and faxing or online submission is easier.
We are concerned that supporting both FHIR and X12 standards in the same process, as might be required under current HIPAA transaction standard requirements, may lead to data integrity issues.

Continuing to enable FHIR through a “lowest common denominator” HIPAA 278 transaction standard will not result in desired outcomes because stakeholders are required to translate FHIR to HIPAA 278, exchange information, and then translate from 278 back to FHIR.

X12 is difficult because it is proprietary... Implementers who have not paid for the X12 standard cannot see the codes being tested during connectathons and therefore won’t have all of the pieces necessary to IG refinement.
Challenges with X12N 278 encourage the use of web portals and fax for PA workflow, driving provider abrasion.
Requiring certified health IT to translate from FHIR to X12 would create an unnecessary and undesirable level of complexity and burden to PA vendors and other HIT vendors.
The ongoing challenges that stakeholders face translating between FHIR and X12 would disappear if CMS would eliminate the X12 requirement for prior authorization requests.
ONC and CMS should support legislative changes for the PA process to occur entirely within the FHIR framework, without HIPAA X12 transactions or temporary regulatory exceptions.
It has been our experience that the most expensive and least flexible way to [exchange data for prior authorizations] thus far has been in using HL7 and X12 278.

We believe the certification criterion should not account for a full HIPAA transaction compliant workflow but should allow payers and providers to utilize FHIR standards for data exchanges without having to translate data into a HIPAA compliant transaction.

3.1.themeDescription: Support for FHIR Documents for Attachments

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming consensus that FHIR is the correct future standard for healthcare attachments, offering a more modern, flexible, and automatable alternative to legacy formats. However, a sharp divide exists between stakeholders on timing and implementation. Payers and technology vendors advocate for rapid, and in some cases immediate, adoption, citing FHIR’s superiority for enabling AI-driven reviews and its readiness demonstrated in pilot testing. Conversely, EHR vendors and provider organizations express significant concerns about the immaturity of the standards, the lack of current adoption, and the substantial developer burden, cautioning against a premature mandate and advocating for a more flexible, data-set-centric approach over a rigid document-based one.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters across all stakeholder types endorse moving toward a FHIR-based approach for attachments, viewing it as superior to CDA. They cite its modern architecture, support for structured data, and alignment with other interoperability initiatives. For instance, Humana supports FHIR Documents over the existing CDA Attachment IG because FHIR Documents support structured data, images, and CDA documents, and Allina Health states a preference for health plans to implement FHIR-based APIs as the industry standard for this purpose.

exceptions.text: Drummond Group, a certification body, uniquely recommended that "FHIR Documents IG as attachments should not be part of a certified implementation for electronic prior authorization" at this time.

consensusPoints.text: Most stakeholders who commented on this point recognized that establishing a FHIR Document standard for ePA would create positive spillover effects, supporting other administrative and clinical workflows like HEDIS reporting, risk adjustment, and medical reviews. For example, Medical Mutual of Ohio noted that FHIR Documents have wide ranging application beyond Prior Authorization, specifically for HEDIS/STARS Quality Program and Risk Adjustment submissions. Similarly, Mettle Solutions, LLC stated the use of FHIR Documents would support more effective exchange for prepayment and post-payment medical record review. Epic Systems Corporation also noted that future FHIR use for clinical data exchange in PA could promote alignment with standards for other purposes.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters who addressed the topic agreed

areasOfDebate.topic: Readiness and Timing for a Mandate
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether FHIR Documents are mature enough for a near-term mandate, with technology vendors and payers advocating for immediate adoption while EHR vendors and providers urge caution due to implementation costs and readiness concerns.

positions.label: Ready Now
positions.stance: Mandate FHIR Documents immediately or in the very near term.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily technology vendors and some large payers.
keyArguments: The standard is mature enough, tested in Connectathons, and technically superior; mandating it will accelerate the move away from inefficient legacy systems and unlock automation.
Payers are "anxious to begin receiving FHIR documents."

positions.label: Premature
positions.stance: The standard is too immature for a near-term mandate.
positions.supportLevel: EHR vendors, provider associations, and several large payers.
keyArguments: The standard is not widely adopted or supported by developers; FHIR Documents are not on EHR roadmaps.
A premature mandate would impose significant costs and burden for a technology that is not ready for production use.

areasOfDebate.description: A key debate exists between a document-centric approach, which treats FHIR Documents as a container similar to CDA, and a data set-centric approach, favored by EHR vendors, which involves assembling discrete FHIR resources without creating a new document wrapper.

positions.label: Document-Centric
positions.stance: Use FHIR Documents as a direct replacement for CDA documents, serving as a container for all attachment information.
positions.supportLevel: Some technology consultants and utilization management firms.
keyArguments: This is a clear, evolutionary step from CDA; tools exist to convert CDA to FHIR Documents; it provides a good interim step toward a fully FHIR-native world.

positions.label: Data Set-Centric
positions.stance: Avoid creating a new 'document' and instead assemble a set of discrete FHIR resources (e.g., DiagnosticReport, Condition) as needed for the attachment.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of EHR vendors and their trade association.
keyArguments: Creating a new document is an unnecessary, burdensome transformation step if the data already exists as FHIR resources.
A data-set approach is more automated, flexible, and truly FHIR-native, avoiding the creation of new documents where none existed before.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Developer burden, implementation costs, and avoiding inefficient workflows.
specificPoints: They strongly emphasize the distinction between a 'document-centric' and a 'data set-centric' approach. They argue that requiring the creation of a FHIR Document as a wrapper is a redundant step that negates the benefits of a truly automated, resource-based FHIR exchange.
Allow submission of a flexible data set of FHIR resources as defined in the PAS IG, rather than mandating the creation of a new FHIR Document.

EHRA members do not support required translation into or creation of a document where one didn't exist before...The introduction of an extra transformation step to populate a PAS request is unnecessary if FHIR Documents are solely used for packaging.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Achieving automation, improving data quality for reviews, and reducing administrative overhead.
specificPoints: Payers are keenly focused on the downstream benefits of receiving structured data. They see FHIR Documents as the key to enabling automated review against clinical rules, a significant leap from parsing PDFs or CDA documents.

Most support a FHIR-first approach, but differ on timing. Humana believes the standards are ready for production, while UHG and Cigna urge caution, citing immaturity and recommending an optional, phased-in approach.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden, ensuring any new standard works seamlessly within EHR workflows, and avoiding unfunded mandates for immature technology.
specificPoints: They are on the front lines of PA burden and are supportive of any technology that promises relief. However, they are also wary of new requirements that are not yet stable or widely supported, which could worsen their situation.
Support for FHIR as the long-term goal, but recommend a cautious timeline aligned with broader FHIR adoption (e.g., 2024 or later) to avoid disruption.
FAH recommends ONC 'create a timeline aligned with FHIR adoption alone and proposing certification criteria starting in 2024 or later' due to concerns that FHIR Documents are not ready today.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Promoting a coherent, modern, and truly interoperable standards ecosystem.
specificPoints: These groups provide deep technical insight. They highlight the superiority of FHIR's architecture (REST APIs, JSON/XML, granular queries) and its ability to be 'broken into its constituent resources' for discrete processing.
Strongly advocate for a 'native FHIR approach' and skipping legacy standards. AMIA explicitly recommends adopting only FHIR Documents to 'avoid confusion and potential interoperability issues'.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The most sophisticated insight came from Cerner and EHRA, distinguishing between using FHIR to create a flexible data set of needed information versus forcing that data into a new document wrapper. This distinction is critical for achieving true automation and avoiding redundant work.

Mettle Solutions painted a vivid picture of the future state, arguing that FHIR Documents are the key to unleashing 'a wave of artificial intelligence, machine learning and other advanced technologies to assist nurse reviewers'. This highlights the powerful business case for payers and tech companies.

Telligen provided a well-articulated technical design pattern, suggesting FHIR Documents for 'pushing' small attachments and a DocumentReference-based 'pull' mechanism for large files. This practical advice addresses real-world technical challenges like session timeouts.

Aneesh Chopra & Brendan Keeler proposed a novel, patient-centric use case: 'payers may post FHIR documents to reflect the outcomes of an adjudicated prior authorization request,' enabling better patient access to their PA status.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A clear pattern emerged where payers and technology companies (who stand to gain from automation) are pushing for aggressive adoption, while providers and EHR vendors (who bear the implementation cost and risk) are urging caution and a more measured, flexible approach.

Commenters like Cerner advocate for a 'leapfrog' strategy, moving directly to a fully FHIR-native environment. This contrasts with the more common call from provider groups for a phased, multi-year roadmap that acknowledges current readiness gaps.

A subtle but significant pattern is the push by sophisticated technical commenters (Cerner, EHRA, Epic) to move beyond the 'attachment' or 'document' metaphor entirely, toward a more fluid exchange of discrete data points, which they argue is the true promise of FHIR.

keyQuotations.quote: [T]he payer community is poised to unleash a wave of artificial intelligence, machine learning and other advanced technologies to assist nurse reviewers and coders in the review of medical records once providers begin submitting FHIR documents...

We recommend the PAS IG approach that enables attaching individual data points and various documents... and do not support focusing on a document-based approach for supporting information when the Da Vinci approach of using a data set is appropriate for automated PA workflows.

While having both Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) Attachments and FHIR Documents could benefit implementation, it would inevitably lead to confusion and potential interoperability issues. Therefore, FHIR Documents is the most appropriate protocol to adopt.

The FHIR standard for attachments (C-CDA on FHIR) is still immature and not widely utilized, and as a result, if it is included in the certification criteria, it should be an optional separate module.
We believe an all-FHIR formatted data exchange approach is an opportunity to 'leapfrog' due to current ePA progression and FHIR US Core use for data gathering.

FHIR Documents are not ready for prior authorization use today... If ONC adopts certification criteria using attachment standards for ePA, FAH recommends creating a timeline aligned with FHIR adoption alone and proposing certification criteria starting in 2024 or later.
FHIR documents are better than CCDA documents as they support the automatic review by advanced technology systems against the payers rule sets.

Using FHIR Documents on its own does not enable automated decision-making because these documents are more focused on metadata rather than the content itself. Other FHIR resources...should be implemented fully to expose more structured fields...
Adoption of FHIR and Restful API enables greater scalability and a pathway away from point-to-point interfaces, intermediaries, and expensive consulting services...

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on adopting the HL7 C-CDA R2 Attachment IG is sharply divided, revealing a core tension between pragmatism and technological purism. A significant number of provider organizations and some payers view C-CDA as a mature, widely available standard that represents a practical and immediate improvement over fax-based workflows. However, a strong and vocal contingent of health IT developers, informatics professionals, and some large health systems argues that mandating C-CDA would be a step backward, imposing a rigid, document-based model that increases burden, stifles innovation, and creates interoperability conflicts with emerging FHIR-based solutions. The dominant recommendation from proponents is to adopt C-CDA as an interim step, while opponents urge regulators to bypass it entirely in favor of more modern, data-driven standards.

consensusPoints.text: C-CDA is a mature and widely adopted standard for general clinical documentation. A strong majority of commenters, including both proponents and opponents of its use for attachments, acknowledge the ubiquity and maturity of C-CDA as a foundational standard for exchanging clinical documents across the healthcare ecosystem. This shared understanding forms the basis for all subsequent arguments.

exceptions.text: Consensus breaks down completely on whether this general adoption translates to suitability or readiness for the specific use case of prior authorization attachments. Opponents argue its use for this purpose is minimal.

consensusPoints.text: If C-CDA is adopted, its implementation needs careful consideration and further work. Commenters on both sides of the issue agree that simply mandating the standard is insufficient. Proponents and opponents alike recommend further action to ensure successful implementation.

areasOfDebate.topic: Suitability of C-CDA for Modern Prior Authorization

positions.label: Practical & Viable
positions.stance: C-CDA is a proven, available standard that offers a practical path to digitize attachments.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by major provider and payer associations, lab companies, and standards maintenance organizations.
keyArguments: Proven Success: A large-scale implementation by National Government Services (NGS) demonstrated significant cost savings, an 80% reduction in denials for missing documentation, and cut payment times in half.
Wide Availability: Providers and systems are already familiar with C-CDA, making it a 'less disruptive starting point' and more feasible for many systems than newer standards.
Better than Status Quo: It represents a significant and achievable improvement over current fax and portal-based manual processes.

positions.label: Burdensome & Obsolete
positions.stance: C-CDA is an inflexible, document-centric standard unfit for automated, data-driven workflows.
positions.supportLevel: Held by a majority of health IT developers, informatics professionals, and several large health systems and payers.
keyArguments: Increases Burden: It is a 'less flexible standard that could have the unintended consequence of increasing burden' by requiring data to be packaged into a document, an unnecessary transformation step.
Hinders Automation: As a 'document-driven model,' it contains long text strings and requires 'another layer of translation for automation,' unlike discrete data exchange.
Stifles Innovation: Standardizing on C-CDA 'stymies the ability of providers to pursue badly needed cost savings initiatives' and 'blockades the evolution of higher forms of data representations.'

positions.label: Adopt as Interim
positions.stance: Implement C-CDA now as a transitional standard while FHIR matures.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily provider associations and some payers looking for immediate relief.
keyArguments: The industry needs an improvement now, and C-CDA is available while FHIR-based solutions require a 'substantial testing period.'
It can serve as a 'starting point' or 'transition standard' to move the industry off paper without waiting for a perfect, future solution.

positions.label: Skip C-CDA
positions.stance: Do not mandate C-CDA; focus all efforts on modern FHIR-based solutions.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily health IT certification bodies, informatics associations, and health IT developers.
keyArguments: Adopting both CDA and FHIR would 'inevitably lead to confusion and potential interoperability issues.'
It is a wasted investment in a standard that is 'expected to be replaced by FHIR Documents.'
Modern IGs (like the PAS IG) already have better methods for attaching data, making a dedicated C-CDA for packaging redundant.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing immediate administrative burden from manual/fax processes; ensuring any mandated standard is mature and tested.
specificPoints: A pragmatic focus on what is achievable in the 'nearer-future' to provide relief for their members, even if it's not the perfect long-term solution.
Adopt C-CDA as a starting point or interim standard while FHIR is tested. Before any mandate, ONC should survey current use and test maturity.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Avoiding wasted development on redundant or obsolete standards; preventing regulations that create inefficient workflows (e.g., unnecessary data transformations); promoting modern, data-centric interoperability.
specificPoints: A deep technical understanding of the implementation burden. They argue forcefully that C-CDA for this purpose is an anti-pattern that creates more work than it saves.
Do not require C-CDA for attachments, especially not for packaging data. Focus on the Da Vinci PAS IG approach which handles data points and various document types directly.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Receiving necessary clinical data efficiently; standardizing attachments; ensuring data received is structured and usable for automated review.
specificPoints: A split view. Some, like UHG and BCBSA, see C-CDA as a feasible, well-supported standard. Others, like Cigna, are concerned about variability, lack of discrete data, and receiving more than the minimum necessary information.
Support C-CDA as a viable option. Allow for an incremental, pilot-based approach to mature the standards.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Finding solutions that work across both provider and payer functions; avoiding inflexible standards that increase clinician burden.
specificPoints: Experiencing the friction from both sides. Ascension provides a stark warning based on their experience that C-CDA can 'increase, rather than eliminate, burden.'
Kaiser makes the nuanced suggestion to use C-CDA for claim attachments, a different use case.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring technical soundness and clear, non-conflicting implementation pathways; promoting true interoperability.
specificPoints: They provide the most polarized, data-driven arguments. NUCC presents powerful positive evidence from a real-world implementation. In direct opposition, AMIA and Drummond warn of market confusion and technical problems if C-CDA is pursued for this purpose.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data-backed Success Story: The National Uniform Claim Committee provided a powerful, data-rich counterpoint to theoretical concerns. Their description of the National Government Services (NGS) program using C-CDA in an X12 275 transaction—resulting in an 80% reduction in denials and cutting payment time from 35 to 17 days—is the most compelling evidence that C-CDA can be highly effective in practice.

The 'Packaging' vs. 'Data' Distinction: The EHR Association and MEDITECH clearly articulate a core technical objection: using a C-CDA document merely to 'package' data that could be sent as discrete elements via a modern IG is inefficient and creates unnecessary work. This highlights the fundamental clash between document-centric and data-centric exchange philosophies.

Use Case Specificity: Kaiser Permanente introduced a crucial nuance by recommending C-CDA for claim attachments but not explicitly for prior authorization. This suggests that C-CDA's suitability is not universal and may be better fitted for post-service documentation than for pre-service authorization workflows.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Provider vs. Technologist Divide: A clear pattern emerges where healthcare provider organizations tend to favor the pragmatic, available C-CDA standard as an improvement over the status quo, while technology developers and informatics experts strongly oppose it as an obsolete and burdensome approach that hinders progress.

The 'Interim Solution' Framing: Many who support C-CDA frame it as a 'starting point', 'nearer-future' option, or 'transition' standard. This indicates a broad, underlying consensus that FHIR is the ultimate goal, with the debate centering on whether C-CDA is a necessary or counterproductive intermediate step.

Integration with X12: Supporters often do not see C-CDA in isolation. They explicitly connect it to the existing X12 275 transaction standard, viewing it as a payload within a well-established administrative framework, which strengthens the argument for its feasibility.

keyQuotations.quote: [The NGS] use of these standards has demonstrated not just the technical success of the transactions, but also a cost savings for all organizations participating. Overall, they have seen up to an 80% reduction in medical review denials and appeals associated with missing documentation. The time to payment has decreased from an average of 35 days to an average of 17 days.

The continued practice of blockading the evolution of higher forms of data representations and transports by standardizing on C-CDA documents stymies the ability of providers to pursue badly needed cost savings initiatives that could be delivered by technology today.

Based on Ascension’s experience to date, while Consolidated Clinical Document Architecture (CCD-A) is widely used, it is a less flexible standard that could have the unintended consequence of increasing burden rather than eliminating it.
While having both Clinical Document Architecture (CDA) Attachments and FHIR Documents could benefit implementation, it would inevitably lead to confusion and potential interoperability issues.
We do not support focusing on a document-based approach for supporting information when the Da Vinci approach of using a data set is appropriate for automated PA workflows.
Cigna believes there is no one CCDA template that supports all PA documentation scenarios; it's subject to variability and could send more than minimally necessary info.

If regulators launch a substantial testing period for the FHIR-based solution, an attachment standard utilizing the widely available C-CDA format paired with the 278 transaction may be most appropriate for nearer-future prior authorizations.
CDA Attachments would better accommodate improvements over time to meet payer and provider needs.

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming consensus from all stakeholder groups—providers, payers, and health IT vendors—that the mandated translation of data from modern FHIR APIs to the legacy X12 278 format is a significant source of burden, cost, and inefficiency. Commenters universally warn of data loss, increased error rates, and the creation of an unnecessary intermediary layer that undermines the very purpose of adopting FHIR. The central tension is between pragmatists who see the translation as a flawed but necessary bridge to maintain HIPAA compliance, and a larger chorus of voices urging policymakers to eliminate the underlying X12 requirement to unleash the full potential of a modern, FHIR-native process.

consensusPoints.text: The FHIR-to-X12 Translation is Burdensome, Costly, and Inefficient. Nearly all commenters explicitly state that the translation process is complex, adds cost, introduces risk, and negates the efficiency gains of FHIR. Stakeholders from every category agree on this fundamental point. They describe it as an "unnecessary complication", a "solution on top of a solution", and a process that "diminish[es] efficiencies".

Translation Creates Significant Risk of Data Loss and Errors. A strong majority of commenters, particularly those with deep technical or clinical data experience, express grave concerns that the translation process is "lossy" and will lead to critical data integrity issues. They note that the richer, more structured data in FHIR cannot be fully represented in the X12 format, increasing the risk of errors, pended claims, and potential delays in care.

The Translation Process Requires an Intermediary Layer (e.g., Clearinghouses). Most stakeholders across all types acknowledge that the translation process would necessitate the involvement of an intermediary, typically a clearinghouse. This is viewed negatively by most as adding another "toll-taker", increasing costs, and introducing another point of potential failure or delay in the process.

areasOfDebate.topic: Should ONC Certification Include the FHIR-to-X12 Translation?
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether the FHIR-to-X12 translation capability should be a required part of ONC's Health IT Certification Program, given the burden and complexity it introduces.

positions.label: Exclude from Certification
positions.stance: Certify the FHIR workflow only up to the point of translation, leaving the translation itself to other entities like clearinghouses.
positions.supportLevel: The strong majority view, held by most health IT vendors, many payers, and several large provider and trade associations.
keyArguments: Provider-facing EHRs do not operate in the X12 space, and forcing them to would add huge development costs and complexity.
Translation is a specialized function best handled by intermediaries.
Including it in certification adds an 'extra layer of complexity without significant added benefit'.

positions.label: Include in Certification
positions.stance: Certify the entire end-to-end workflow, including the translation from FHIR to the HIPAA-compliant X12 standard.
positions.supportLevel: A smaller minority view, primarily held by state government entities and some provider advocacy groups concerned about operational reality.
keyArguments: To ensure a truly functional, HIPAA-compliant system, the entire process must be tested and certified.
If X12 remains the legal requirement, ignoring the translation step in certification is ignoring reality.
Mandating this capability ensures smaller practices aren't left behind if they need to connect to payers still using X12.

positions.label: Separate Certification
positions.stance: Address the translation capability as a separate, distinct certifiable module, not as an integral part of the core FHIR API certification.
positions.supportLevel: A compromise position offered by several health IT vendors and their association.
keyArguments: This approach acknowledges the need for a functional translation without burdening all health IT (especially provider-facing EHRs) with the requirement.
It allows specialized systems (like clearinghouses) to certify their translation tools.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Development burden, cost, and liability for a 'problematic' translation process. They are concerned about payer-specific variations in X12 implementation and the 'longer development timelines' this would create.
specificPoints: They firmly assert that their provider-facing systems 'do not operate in the X12 transaction space' and that the translation function belongs to intermediaries like clearinghouses or payers.

Overwhelmingly recommend that ONC either exclude translation from certification or make it a separate, optional certification. They want targeted guidance on translation but do not want to be responsible for building it.
Allscripts states they 'should not be responsible for FHIR to X12 mapping'.
Quest Diagnostics worries that individual translation efforts 'might not be interoperable and could be much more costly to develop'.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data integrity, process inefficiency, and being forced to maintain two parallel systems. They are highly aware that FHIR is a 'more robust data model' and worry about data loss when 'dumbing down' to X12.
specificPoints: As the recipients of the final transaction, they highlight how the 'lowest common denominator' X12 standard is insufficient for complex clinical dialogue and often results in pended requests. They also note the challenge of synchronizing changes between the two standards.

Most large payers advocate for a full FHIR-to-FHIR pathway, urging policymakers to remove the X12 'ceiling'. In the interim, many support certifying only up to the point of translation, and expanding pilots for pure FHIR exchange.
Kaiser Permanente, as an integrated system, finds it 'costly and inefficient for providers and payers to maintain and execute both standards'.
Anthem states their preferred end-state 'is a standard that functions independently of X12 278'.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Increased direct costs from needing new clearinghouse services, and the risk of patient care delays due to data errors from the translation.
specificPoints: They connect the technical issue directly to administrative simplification goals, noting the translation 'runs contrary to HIPAA’s administrative simplification goals'.
A small practice perspective highlights the complexity of dealing with 'multiple clearinghouses' for a single transaction.
Generally want to avoid the translation. The AMA and AHIMA recommend ONC first publicly vet the results from FHIR-only pilots before mandating a flawed translation workflow.
Cleveland Clinic voiced direct concern about 'the risk of key information being lost during the translation process'.
The Regulatory Relief Coalition uniquely requested that FHIR-enabled processes mandate functionality for X12 translation to prevent smaller practices from being unable to connect to payers.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data-backed Observation: Apixio provides a concrete estimate that eliminating the translation step and associated clearinghouse lag could shorten the prior authorization process by 36-48 hours, a 'substantial' improvement that would directly improve time to treatment.

Unexpected Connection/Insight: An individual commenter astutely points out the fundamental architectural mismatch: FHIR is designed for point-to-point connections, creating a network complexity of (X providers * Y payers), whereas X12 relies on a more efficient hub-and-spoke clearinghouse model. This highlights that simply replacing the standard without addressing the network model is insufficient.

Compelling Personal Example: A urologist draws a parallel to the 835 Electronic Remittance Advice standard, noting that even 8 years post-mandate, implementation is still fraught with challenges, suggesting a mandate for a complex FHIR-X12 translation will not guarantee a smooth rollout.

Hidden Complexity: Providence, an integrated health system, points out that the burden goes beyond just FHIR-to-X12; their internal UM systems use custom transaction codes that would also require complex mapping from FHIR, revealing another layer of implementation difficulty.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Pragmatists vs. Purists: A clear pattern emerges between commenters who view the translation as a necessary, if flawed, 'temporary bridge' to maintain HIPAA compliance while the industry transitions (ACHP, HL7 Da Vinci) and those who see it as a fatal flaw that must be removed entirely to achieve true modernization (Cigna, 1upHealth, Mettle).

Facility-type Patterns: Large, integrated systems like Kaiser Permanente and Providence are uniquely positioned to articulate the dual burden of maintaining two standards and the internal data integrity risks, as they function as both provider and payer.

Unintended Consequences: Multiple commenters identify the risk that this policy, intended to reduce burden, will actually increase it by creating new costs for clearinghouses (AHA), introducing new points of failure (Allscripts), and creating a permanent 'barrier to success' rather than a temporary bridge (ACHP).

Experience Correlations: Commenters with deep technical expertise in both FHIR and X12 (e.g., PACIO Project SMEs) highlight the very real-world challenge of a limited talent pool with expertise in both standards, making high-quality mapping difficult to achieve at scale.

keyQuotations.quote: [We feel] there is no valid reason other than incumbency protection to run these prior authorizations through heavily lossy FHIR ó X12 data reformulations... Patients and the public should not be subjected to another layer of 'toll-takers' adding costs.
Converting between FHIR and X12 creates possibilities of failure, especially given payer variation... [It is] a solution on top of a solution.
We are concerned that supporting both FHIR and X12 standards in the same process, as might be required under current HIPAA transaction standard requirements, may lead to data integrity issues.

The translation of FHIR information into and out of the 278 transaction would likely require providers to procure additional clearinghouse services in order to successfully complete the transaction, which runs contrary to HIPAA’s administrative simplification goals...
The ongoing challenges that stakeholders face translating between FHIR and X12 would disappear if CMS would eliminate the X12 requirement for prior authorization requests.

This interruption causes unnecessary inefficiencies for both payers and providers... We recommend that... there should be consideration of how to make an exception for the use of the FHIR Bundle without the translation. This could potentially reduce the lag-time... by 36-48 hours.
ACHP notes that this bridge risks becoming a long-term barrier to success and ultimately does little to reduce health plan processing burden.
...the cost of engaging a clearinghouse to translate X12 into FHIR-enabled processes is costly and may be prohibitive for smaller and mid-size practices (and for smaller payers).
Requiring certified health IT to translate from FHIR to X12 would create an unnecessary and undesirable level of complexity and burden to PA vendors and other HIT vendors.
Rather, we would suggest that the PAS API data is passed through the Clearinghouses instead of the 278.
There is no current way to have exchanges like there are for X12 and NCPDP Standard Transactions... This is much more efficient than needing to connect X number of providers to Y number of payers...

4.2.themeDescription: Role of Clinical Decision Support (CDS) Integration

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming consensus on the concept of integrating Clinical Decision Support (CDS) into the ePA process to provide real-time guidance at the point of care. However, this broad support for the "what" is met with deep divisions and significant concerns about the "how." The primary tension exists between the potential for proactive guidance to reduce downstream burden and the risks of disrupting clinical workflows, shifting administrative work onto clinicians, and mandating the use of standards, like CDS Hooks, that many stakeholders view as technically immature and difficult to scale.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters across all stakeholder types—including providers, payers, vendors, and advocacy groups—support the fundamental goal of integrating PA requirement checks and decision support directly into the clinical workflow. The ability to identify PA needs, see alternatives, and understand documentation requirements in real time is seen as an essential capability for reducing administrative burden. The American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) states that identifying when PA is applicable using CDS is a "key element for a solid foundation of ePA". Payer UnitedHealth Group agrees that embedding PA checks and decision support "directly into the clinical workflow at the point of care helps providers identify alternative treatment options proactively". Provider alliance Premier Inc. "strongly supports the provision of information regarding prior authorization requirements and payer documentation needs within providers’ EHR workflow".

exceptions.text: This consensus is strictly conceptual. Support fractures completely when discussing specific standards (like CDS Hooks) and the precise point of integration (e.g., clinician ordering vs. back-office administrative workflow).

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters, particularly provider and advocacy groups, agree that a simple flag indicating "PA required" is insufficient. They advocate for a more robust CDS that provides actionable information, such as why a PA is needed, what alternatives do not require PA, and what specific documentation is missing. Clinician Peter Basch warns that a simple indicator "may lead to more inappropriate requests... with many clinical attachments for payers to adjudicate," arguing the system must explain why PA is needed. The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) recommends integrating information about PA requirements and "alternatives that would not require PA, similar to e-prescribing systems". UCSF CDHI recommends the process "immediately and transparently identify prior authorization requirements, so physicians can provide the correct information on the first submission".

areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether the CDS Hooks standard is mature enough for mandatory adoption in certification or if it is too nascent and risky, posing significant implementation burdens.

positions.label: Ready for Primetime
positions.stance: Proponents argue that CDS Hooks is a mature and essential standard that should be adopted and required in certification to enable robust, real-time integration.
positions.supportLevel: A vocal group of health IT vendors, standards implementers, and consultants.
keyArguments: The standard is already used by large EHRs (Epic, Cerner); it enables multiple other valuable CDS use cases beyond ePA; and delaying its adoption will prolong administrative burden.

positions.label: Immature & Risky
positions.stance: Opponents and cautious stakeholders contend that CDS Hooks is too nascent, complex, and not widely adopted, and that mandating it would create massive implementation burden and risk system failure.
positions.supportLevel: A significant number of health IT vendors, payers, provider systems, and technical experts.
keyArguments: The standard is still evolving; it is "barely used" today even in advanced settings; scaling it across thousands of providers and payers would be an "unmitigated disaster"; a requirement would be overly burdensome for developers.

areasOfDebate.description: This debate revolves around whether PA-related CDS should be embedded directly into the clinician's ordering workflow for proactive guidance or kept separate to avoid disrupting patient care and shifting administrative burden.

positions.label: Embed in Clinical Ordering
positions.stance: Proponents believe CDS must be embedded directly into the CPOE/ordering process to guide the clinician's decision-making in real time, preventing incorrect orders and unnecessary PA submissions from ever occurring.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily payers, some tech vendors, and some provider systems focused on system-level efficiency.
keyArguments: This provides proactive guidance and prevents downstream rework; it allows clinicians and patients to make better shared decisions; it is the only way to suggest formulary alternatives at the most impactful moment.

positions.label: Keep Out of Clinical Ordering
positions.stance: Opponents argue that forcing PA-related tasks into the clinician's ordering workflow is disruptive, shifts administrative burden onto the most expensive resource (the physician), and interferes with the patient-provider interaction.
positions.supportLevel: Practicing clinicians, specialty providers, and a major EHR vendor.
keyArguments: PA is an administrative, not clinical, function; it will increase uncompensated physician time and burnout; it presumes clinical data is neatly structured, which is false; it should be optional or "disable-able."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Disrupting the patient encounter with administrative tasks; shifting uncompensated burden onto clinicians; the cost and complexity of implementing new, unproven technology.
specificPoints: They emphasize the sanctity of the clinical workflow and the practical reality that much of the required clinical data is in unstructured notes, not discrete fields, making full automation difficult. They are acutely aware of the risk of increased burnout.
Make CDS integration optional or disable-able
leverage existing CDS tools to bypass PA entirely
focus on providing transparent rules rather than forcing disruptive interactions
RadNet firmly states, "Prior-authorization is an administrative, not clinical, function and we advise against changes that affect clinical operations"

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Mandating "nascent" or "evolving" standards like CDS Hooks creates significant development burden and risk; lack of clarity in implementation guides; payer readiness to support complex APIs.
specificPoints: They are divided. Some, like Wolters Kluwer, see a strategic advantage in mandating standards they have already adopted. Others, like Allscripts and Epic, see the practical challenges and advocate for a more cautious, phased approach, warning against workflow disruption.
Make CDS Hooks optional
pilot the technology first
focus certification on more mature IGs like CRD and PAS, while deferring the more complex DTR IG
Epic warns that the DTR IG’s approach of "triggering decision support to prompt providers to fulfill documentation requirements in the middle of their workflow could be disruptive to patient care"

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Their own technical readiness to implement complex standards like CDS Hooks at scale.
specificPoints: They are highly supportive of in-workflow CDS because it allows them to guide providers toward cost-effective, covered alternatives before an inappropriate service is ordered, representing a major efficiency gain and cost-control mechanism for them.
Support using CDS Hooks and SMART on FHIR as the logical approach
consider simpler, less prescriptive standards
Cigna notes that this approach would allow the plan to "access EHR API directly," enabling automated checks within the provider's workflow

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient privacy (AMA); preserving clinical autonomy (ASNC); ensuring new technology reduces, not increases, physician burden (AAFP).
specificPoints: These groups synthesize the views of their members, often supporting the high-level goal while raising critical caveats. The AMA's concern that CDS Hooks could lead to payers receiving more than the "minimum necessary" patient data is a key risk highlighted by this group.
Concurrently develop privacy and security protections with functional capabilities
ensure any CDS information is tied to a clear clinical rationale
use CDS to show alternatives
The AMA urges that EHR development must "uphold minimum necessary principles when using CDS Hooks and other triggers"

noteworthyInsights.insight: An "Unmitigated Disaster" in the Making? - Individual technical expert Brendan Keeler provides a stark, quantitative warning against underestimating the complexity of scaling CDS Hooks: "Imagining a network of 10000 or 100000 provider orgs (not all advanced) doing CDS Hooks cross-enterprise to 1000 payers for 10000 plans is to imagine pain itself. It will be an unmitigated disaster." This highlights the gap between a single-system pilot and a national network.

A "Gold Carding" Incentive - Elsevier and Dana-Farber Cancer Institute propose a powerful paradigm shift: instead of just streamlining PA, use of a trusted CDS tool should eliminate the need for it. Elsevier states this "would obviate the need for prior authorization, while simultaneously promoting evidence-based medicine". This frames CDS not as a PA facilitator, but as a PA replacement.

The Clinician's "Disable" Button - A practicing urologist, Dr. Shteynshlyuger, makes a crucial recommendation born from front-line experience: providers must have the ability to "DISABLE" intrusive ePA functionality within the EHR, giving them control over their own workflow and preventing technology from harming care delivery.

Distinguishing "Good" vs. "Bad" Integration - Epic Systems Corporation offers a nuanced view, distinguishing between different Da Vinci IGs. They support CRD (checking if PA is needed) and PAS (submitting the PA) but warn that the DTR IG (using CDS to prompt for documentation during ordering) is highly disruptive and should not be mandated, providing a pathway for a more phased, less controversial implementation.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Concept-vs-Reality Gap: There is a chasm between the universal support for the concept of real-time CDS and the deep divisions over the reality of its implementation. The debate is not about if this should be done, but how and with which tools (e.g., the divisive CDS Hooks standard).

The Vendor Divide: Health IT vendors are not a monolith. A clear split exists between evangelists for CDS Hooks who have invested in the standard and see its potential (e.g., Wolters Kluwer, Mettle) and pragmatists/skeptics who are concerned about its immaturity, development cost, and risk (e.g., Allscripts, Epic, Telligen).

The Workflow Battleground: A fundamental tension exists between payers, who see the clinician ordering process as the ideal point to insert decision support to control costs, and clinicians, who see this as a dangerous intrusion of administrative tasks into the patient care space. This represents a core conflict in priorities between financial/administrative efficiency and clinical focus.

keyQuotations.quote: [I]magining a network of 10000 or 100000 provider orgs (not all advanced) doing CDS Hooks cross-enterprise to 1000 payers for 10000 plans is to imagine pain itself. It will be an unmitigated disaster.
Prior-authorization is an administrative, not clinical, function and we advise against changes that affect clinical operations.
The DTR IG’s approach of triggering decision support to prompt providers to fulfill documentation requirements in the middle of their workflow could be disruptive to patient care.

ONC's vision... forcing doctors to complete PA forms during ordering... will 'require physicians to spend additional uncompensated time, increasing administrative burden, lowering productivity, increasing wait times, and limiting care access.'
Indeed, we believe that the use of CDS can and should frequently eliminate the need for ePA requirements by payers.

Evaluation of appropriateness does not require interaction with the health plan outside the EMR, as a properly enabled CDS tool can reliably determine if a patient meets utilization management rules...
Some HL7 standards (e.g., CDS Hooks) needed for ePA currently lack controls limiting payer access to patient data, which is a significant privacy concern.

We suggest making rules available in a computable format, such as CQL, so that a CDS service (such as CDSHooks) could consume those rules directly and alert clinicians in real time when prior authorization is applicable.
At the very least, an ePA standard should allow the system to alert the prescribing physician whether a prior authorization will be required when the prescriber is preparing to place an order...
Simply getting an indicator that an MRI requires prior authorization 'may lead to more inappropriate requests for an MRI, with many clinical attachments for payers to adjudicate...'
However, more testing and CDS Hooks maturity is required before it is ready for tight integration within an electronic prior authorization process.
In sum, adoption of CDS Hooks will not only help facilitate and streamline the ePA process, it also significantly strengthens overall CDS functionality within certified EHR systems.

4.3.themeDescription: Feasibility Concerns about Clinical Quality Language (CQL)

sections.executiveSummary: There is near-unanimous agreement among commenters that implementing Clinical Quality Language (CQL) to codify payer coverage rules represents a profound and potentially infeasible challenge. The core tension is whether this is an insurmountable technology problem due to CQL's immaturity and the complexity of clinical logic, or a massive but necessary "business transformation" for payers. The dominant recommendation is to delay or phase in any CQL mandate, start with simpler use cases, and explore alternative technologies like APIs to avoid jeopardizing the entire prior authorization reform effort.

consensusPoints.text: A vast majority of commenters explicitly describe the process of using CQL to define coverage rules as exceptionally difficult, complex, burdensome, and a major barrier to adoption. Stakeholders across all categories—payers, EHR vendors, providers, and technical experts—agree on the scale of this challenge. For example, Cigna noted that simple CQL development is arduous and PA criteria are significantly more complex. AHIP stated that the CQL specification and system engines do not currently perform reliably, and codifying policies would be a burden. The EHRA added that DTR capabilities are administrative processes typically outside the scope of EHRs.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A vast majority of commenters (13 of 15)

exceptions.text: The standards organizations that developed the guides (HL7, Da Vinci Project) acknowledge the difficulty but frame it as a manageable "business transformation" for payers rather than a fundamental technology flaw. One standards-focused consultant (Lantana) was explicitly supportive, recommending that rules should be in a processable format like CQL.

consensusPoints.text: Most stakeholders who accept the premise of CQL, including its strongest proponents, advocate for an incremental or phased rollout, seeing it as the only viable path forward. For example, HL7 advocates for an incremental process for ePA adoption, starting with rules for items and services that leverage fully structured data. The EHRA recommends that the initial focus should be on CRD and PAS interactions, with DTR as a later stage. Telligen, Inc. also suggests that CQL may be introduced in later years due to its complexity.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most stakeholders who accept the premise of CQL

areasOfDebate.topic: The Nature of the CQL Challenge

positions.label: Technology Problem
positions.stance: CQL as a language and rules-based engine is fundamentally ill-suited for the complexity of clinical policies and is not a mature technology.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from payers and some tech vendors
keyArguments: CQL systems don't perform reliably; rules-based logic has a 40-year history of struggling with clinical complexity; it is arduous to develop even for simple measures.

positions.label: Business Transformation
positions.stance: The technology is sound, but the primary challenge is the immense operational lift for payers to translate their policies into a computable format.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily held by standards bodies
keyArguments: This is about payers undertaking a necessary evolution to declare their data needs in a structured way; the issue is process and policy codification, not the language itself.

positions.label: Mandate CQL
positions.stance: Require CQL as the standard for computable rules to ensure interoperability and true automation.
positions.supportLevel: A small minority view
keyArguments: Without a computable format like CQL, systems will revert to exchanging PDFs and HTML pages; it enables local evaluation of rules by CDS services.

positions.label: Allow Alternatives
positions.stance: Do not mandate CQL exclusively; allow the industry to innovate with other technologies like APIs and Machine Learning.
positions.supportLevel: A plurality of commenters
keyArguments: A strict CQL mandate stifles innovation; better alternatives may exist, such as calling a payer's API which can leverage more advanced AI/ML; this avoids distributing and maintaining complex logic across thousands of provider systems.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Technical infeasibility of codifying complex policies into CQL; the immense burden and cost on scarce resources; the immaturity and unreliability of the technology.
specificPoints: As the authors of the rules, they are most attuned to the nuance and complexity that they believe cannot be captured in a rules-based language. They highlight the difficulty of maintaining and distributing this logic.
Proposed solutions include using better alternatives like APIs that allow for innovation (Cigna) or delaying or avoiding the mandate due to the burden (AHIP).
Cigna notes that maintaining a CQL component for each plan, benefit, and EHR endpoint seems infeasible.
ACHP states flatly that CQL is "not widely used... and is not easily understood by any payers or providers".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The complexity of implementing DTR capabilities that rely on CQL; managing data collection requirements from thousands of health plans; the fact that these administrative workflows are outside the traditional scope of EHRs.
specificPoints: As the consumers of the CQL, they are focused on the practical nightmare of integrating and executing logic from innumerable sources within the provider workflow, a task they see as unmanageable.
Proposed solutions include decoupling DTR/CQL from other ePA components and delaying its implementation to a later stage.
EHRA highlights the challenge of "managing data collection requirements across 1500+ health plans".
1upHealth points to the "inherent disconnect between the complexity of clinical care and the ability of branching logic rules to represent that complexity".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring successful adoption of the standards; balancing the ambition of automation with the practical realities of implementation.
specificPoints: As creators and deep experts in the standards, they provide the rationale for CQL while also acknowledging the implementation hurdles. They tend to see a path forward, albeit a challenging one.
Proposed solutions include a phased, incremental approach starting with simple use cases (HL7/Da Vinci) or leaving the technical method open to innovation beyond just CQL (Chopra/Keeler).
The Da Vinci Project frames the issue as a "business transformation issue, not a technology issue" for payers.
Lantana advocates for computable rules in CQL to avoid exchanging static web pages or PDFs.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The foundational assumption that EHR data is structured enough for automation is false.
specificPoints: Provides a crucial ground-truth perspective, arguing that the debate over CQL is premature because the underlying clinical data is largely unstructured, making automated rule execution impossible.

Proposed solutions include mandating that payers pre-populate forms with data they already have and focusing on standardizing the minimum necessary data elements before attempting to automate their collection.
"The assumption that clinical data within EHRs is 'discrete' and 'categorized' enough for automated prepopulation... is 'mostly not true'".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Business Transformation vs. Technology Flaw: The most significant framing difference comes from the standards bodies (HL7, Da Vinci), which argue the challenge is a "business transformation issue, not a technology issue". This contrasts sharply with payers and vendors who see the technology itself as immature and unsuited for the task.

The Missing Foundation: The provider comment from NY Urology Specialists provides a powerful reality check, arguing the entire debate is premature. The lack of structured, discrete clinical data in EHRs is the foundational barrier that no amount of advanced logic in CQL can overcome.

A History of Failure: 1upHealth's observation about "approximately 40 years of challenges implementing effective clinical decision support... using rules-based systems" provides crucial historical context, suggesting that the problems with CQL are not new but part of a long-standing struggle to codify complex clinical knowledge.

An Alternative Architectural Vision: Cigna's proposal to use payer-hosted APIs instead of distributing CQL logic to thousands of provider endpoints represents a distinct and compelling alternative. They argue this is a better-architected approach that centralizes logic and enables more advanced innovation like AI/ML.

emergingPatterns.pattern:
Role-Based Divide: A clear pattern emerges based on the commenter's role in the ecosystem.
Creators (Standards Orgs): See CQL as a necessary, though challenging, evolution.
Implementers (Payers, EHR Vendors): See CQL as technically infeasible, prohibitively burdensome, and immature.
End-Users (Providers): See the foundational clinical data as the true, unaddressed problem.

Unintended Consequence: The CQL mandate may be counterproductive. Telligen notes that the mandate is actively "hampering adoption" of the Da Vinci DTR guide, suggesting that its inclusion could sink the entire effort rather than advance it.

keyQuotations.quote: [CQL] is not widely used within health plan IT infrastructure and is not easily understood by any payers or providers.
There is an inherent disconnect between the complexity of clinical care and the ability of branching logic rules to represent that complexity.
Cigna is concerned about the feasibility for payers/PA service companies to encapsulate and regularly distribute authorization logic in CQL to provider health IT systems.
The assumption that clinical data within EHRs is 'discrete' and 'categorized' enough for automated prepopulation or rule application by payers is 'mostly not true'.
The CQL mandate in DTR IG is hampering adoption, making it a challenge regarding readiness to take IGs to production.

A challenging aspect will be payers expressing the data needed for PA decision-making in CQL for automated documentation collection and evaluation. The Da Vinci community views this as a business transformation issue, not a technology issue.

[We] recommend that the manner in which questionnaire items are pre-filled should be left out of scope for this first round of regulations, rather than focusing exclusively on CQL as the DaVinci DTR specification currently does.
HL7 CQL specification and system engines do not currently perform reliably and rely on codification of natural language clinical policy documents.
DTR capabilities would be particularly challenging for EHRs due to managing data collection requirements across 1500+ health plans...
Recognize the significant effort involved in codifying payer PA rules, which requires a substantial business transformation for payers to support PA automation.

4.4.themeDescription: Necessity and Requirements for Digital Signatures

sections.executiveSummary: Public comment on the necessity of digital signatures for ePA is deeply divided, revealing a core tension between workflow automation and administrative accountability. A strong contingent, primarily Health IT developers and standards bodies, argues that a signature requirement is redundant, adds friction, and fundamentally undermines the goal of a fully automated process, pointing to existing system audit trails as sufficient. Conversely, an equally strong group of provider associations, laboratories, and payers insists that signatures are essential for legal assent, data integrity, and preventing claim denials. The dominant recommendation thrust, from those on both sides of the issue, is for regulatory clarity—either by forgoing the requirement entirely or by adopting clear, modern, and interoperable standards for implementation.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters, regardless of their position on a signature mandate, agree that ensuring the integrity and provenance of data submitted for prior authorization is essential. They differ on whether a signature is the best mechanism to achieve this.

exceptions.text: MCG Health states that "Maintaining data integrity for authorization requests over time is essential" even while opposing a signature requirement. Providence "recognizes the importance of... ensuring data integrity". Payers like AHIP and Cigna link signatures directly to this goal.

consensusPoints.text: Most technically-focused commenters who addressed implementation details noted that existing or emerging HL7 FHIR standards are capable of handling digital signatures.

exceptions.text: Anthem explicitly recommends that the "HL7 FHIR Da Vinci CDex IG... should be considered for additional prior authorization documentation". Lantana Consulting points to the core FHIR Provenance resource as sufficient for the task. Even HL7 Da Vinci, while arguing against the requirement, notes that if included, the CDex IG could support it.

areasOfDebate.topic: Necessity of a Mandated Signature Requirement
areasOfDebate.description: The core debate centers on whether mandating a digital signature enhances accountability or merely adds unnecessary friction to an automated workflow.

positions.label: Unnecessary Friction
positions.stance: A formal signature requirement is redundant and would impede the core goal of automation.
positions.supportLevel: A large and vocal coalition, including nearly all Health IT developers (Cerner, MEDITECH), their trade association (EHRA), and key standards bodies (HL7, HL7 Da Vinci). Roughly half of commenters took this stance.
keyArguments: Existing certified EHR systems already have robust user authentication, access controls, and audit trails that establish provenance, making a separate signature step unnecessary.
The primary goal of ePA is automation; forcing a manual review and signature step re-introduces human intervention, adding burden, friction, and potential for delays.
It risks "digitizing the current paper process instead of transforming it."
