specificPoints: As the builders of the technology, they focus heavily on the technical feasibility and logical sequencing of development. They are the primary source of the detailed 'Stage 1 (Payer), Stage 2 (Provider)' implementation model.

Strongly advocate for a staged approach where payers are required to certify first, establishing a stable target. Recommend starting with CRD and PAS IGs, delaying the more complex DTR implementation.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers & Their Associations (e.g., AHA, AAMC, UCSF, Trinity Health)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Minimizing disruption to clinical workflow and patient care; managing the cost and time required for implementation and staff training; avoiding a new, technically complex process that is more burdensome than the old one.
specificPoints: Emphasize the need for a "glide path" and "sufficient time". Some, like UnityPoint Health, highlight the need to phase implementation differently by setting (inpatient vs. ambulatory) based on readiness.
Request a long transition period (3-5 years suggested by APTA); urge that implementation be preceded by robust, real-world testing.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Payers & Payer Associations (e.g., AHIP, Cigna, Anthem, BCBSA)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring standards are mature and tested before mandates; having adequate time for internal technology development and business process transformation.
specificPoints: They also strongly support phasing, often with a focus on business value. They are a key source for the "start with CRD" recommendation due to its high volume and relative simplicity.

Propose an incremental roadmap starting with high-priority needs like CRD. Suggest establishing a "regulatory 'floor'" for standard versions, allowing the industry to adopt newer versions without new rulemaking.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Standards/Interoperability Orgs (e.g., HL7, WEDI, CAQH CORE)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring a logical, successful adoption pathway that builds on existing processes and leads to widespread, effective use.
specificPoints: They provide the "why" behind iterative approaches, citing agile development models and the need to learn from real-world experience. They emphasize the importance of a clear, public roadmap to guide the industry.
Recommend an incremental approach to allow payers to undergo the necessary "business transformation from a document-driven to a data-driven approach".

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Danger of Delay: Drummond Group offers a powerful counter-narrative to waiting for perfect standards. They argue from experience that ONC's past decision to delay FHIR adoption while requiring non-standard APIs "caused more problems than adopting an immature standard and working with the community to accelerate the maturity." This suggests that mandating an imperfect but standard direction is better than allowing a non-standard free-for-all to develop.

Differentiated Readiness by Setting: UnityPoint Health provides a compelling real-world example of variable readiness within a single health system. They state their inpatient hospital settings would be ready to "go live... on day one," but other sites of care still migrating to a common EHR would not. This highlights that a one-size-fits-all timeline may not be practical even within one organization, let alone across the industry.

A "Regulatory Floor" as a Solution to Versioning: Anthem proposes a sophisticated regulatory approach to handle evolving standards: mandate a "floor" version but allow voluntary adoption of newer, approved versions. This would enable continuous improvement without waiting for the slow cycle of formal rulemaking, a practical solution to the problem of technology outpacing regulation.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type Patterns: Commenters identified that implementation readiness and complexity differ significantly by care setting. UnityPoint Health distinguishes between more-ready inpatient settings and less-ready ambulatory ones. The CMS Data Element Library Workgroup calls for a specific phased-in approach for post-acute care (PAC) providers.

Role-Based Sequencing Preferences: A clear pattern emerged where Health IT vendors, who bear the direct burden of software development, are the most vocal and detailed proponents of the "payer-first" sequencing model. This is a logical position based on their need for a stable technical target.

Unintended Consequences of Delay: Multiple commenters, most notably the Drummond Group, implicitly or explicitly identify a key unintended consequence of delaying mandates to wait for standard maturity: the proliferation of proprietary, non-standard solutions, which ultimately worsens the interoperability problem the regulation seeks to solve.

keyQuotations.quote: [A]ny substantial change in the technology and/or standards used in health care information exchange should be... incorporated according to an appropriate glide path to minimize systematic disruption.

It is our opinion that ONC’s past decision to delay FHIR adoption of Patient Access APIs while still requiring non-standardized implementation of APIs caused more problems than adopting an immature standard and working with the community to accelerate the maturity of those standards.

We recommend that ONC first establish industry-wide regulations... Once these initial regulations are in place, ONC should then consider following the technological standards and implementation timelines.
A staged approach provides an anchor around payer-supported interactions defined in Da Vinci IGs for provider Health IT to target development.

Implement iterative real-world prior authorization system testing before any absolute mandate for use in practice, to ensure all necessary systems for end-to-end workflows are working together as envisioned.
Transitional steps replacing existing systems are typically easiest to adopt and implement with minimal disruptions to the Health IT infrastructure and patient care it supports.
The industry cannot shift from current to emerging standards overnight.

We recommend ONC and CMS identify a “floor” for standards with initial IG versions that support CMS rule functionality... Organizations can implement enhanced functionality from updated IGs but must remain compliant with the updated version, while also supporting the 'floor' version.
ONC should consider the cumulative regulatory burden on providers when setting ePA timelines.
We recommend a staged/iterative approach implementing use case specific IGs, as it allows health IT developers to iterate first with simpler use cases that build in complexity over time.
We recommend that any prior authorization solution be fully developed and tested prior to wide scale industry rollout.
The timeline for implementation should have a two-track goal: Soft implementation of the exchange. Hard implementation deadline to follow.

6.2.themeDescription: Mandate for Rigorous Real-World Testing and Piloting

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus across all stakeholder groups that any mandate for new ePA standards must be preceded by rigorous, real-world testing and piloting. Commenters forcefully argue that current "connectathon" testing, which relies on scripted "happy-path" scenarios, is dangerously insufficient. The central tension lies between a small minority of standards developers who believe the Da Vinci Implementation Guides (IGs) are mature enough to advance, and the vast majority of providers, payers, and vendors who fear a premature mandate will increase costs, exacerbate clinician burnout, and delay patient care, particularly in small, rural, and underserved settings. The dominant recommendation is for a federally supported, transparent piloting phase to validate technical feasibility, measure return on investment, and prove genuine burden reduction before any standards are codified in regulation.

consensusPoints.text: Connectathon testing is insufficient; real-world piloting is essential. The American Medical Association (AMA) argues that testing must move beyond "`happy-path`" demonstrations to ensure technology functions well "across practice settings and in the real-world". The American Hospital Association (AHA) insists on testing "in a real-world setting, rather than a controlled environment like an HL7 Connectathon" to assess scalability, privacy, and the ability to complete administrative tasks. Blue Cross Blue Shield Association (BCBSA) warns that "Certifications performed in a demonstration environment do not always translate during implementation and can cause issues with interoperability".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 30) explicitly state this position. The sentiment is near-unanimous across providers, payers, vendors, and their respective associations.

exceptions.text: A few commenters, primarily those directly involved in developing the standards, feel current testing has been sufficient to move to the next stage.

consensusPoints.text: Pilots must include diverse provider settings, especially small, rural, and specialty practices. The Regulatory Relief Coalition (RRC) recommends that "the practicability of e-PA should be tested in a broad range of provider settings, including small practice settings, where resources that can be dedicated to IT may be limited". OCHIN, representing a national network of community health centers, calls for standards to be "fully test[ed]... in representative work environments in underserved communities to understand, at a minimum, the relative value of investments and workflow changes needed for ePA". The Medical Group Management Association (MGMA) states that testing "in a broad range of provider and care settings will allow stakeholders to preemptively identify workflow issues".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly provider associations and organizations representing vulnerable communities, stress this point.

consensusPoints.text: Testing must measure financial and operational impact, not just technical function. The AHA calls for pilots to "provide proof of adequate return on investment (ROI)". The AMA urges ONC to "require pilot sites/early implementers to publicly report ROI metrics (time spent, time to care, electronic PA % capacity, cost savings, direct/indirect costs)". Apixio recommends pilot studies monitor "process metrics (e.g., time spent on initial implementation) and outcomes metrics (e.g., length of time to authorization decision, time spent preparing/reviewing a request)".
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most provider and payer commenters emphasize that pilots must go beyond technical conformance to measure real-world value.

areasOfDebate.topic: Readiness of Da Vinci Implementation Guides (IGs)
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over the maturity of the Da Vinci Implementation Guides and whether they are ready for a regulatory mandate without further real-world testing.

positions.label: Not Ready for Mandate
positions.stance: The proposed IGs are not mature enough for widespread, mandated implementation without significant further real-world testing.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of commenters, including nearly all provider associations, health systems, payers, and many IT vendors.
keyArguments: Testing has been limited to "happy-path" connectathons, which do not reflect real-world complexity, as noted by AMA.
The IGs have not been sufficiently tested in production or at scale across diverse provider and payer environments, as highlighted by Premier Inc. and Labcorp.
Regulation should not be used to force maturity; market validation and proven success must come first, an argument made by AHIMA and Voluware.

positions.label: Ready to Advance
positions.stance: The Da Vinci IGs are sufficiently vetted and mature to begin a phased implementation or expanded, formalized testing.
positions.supportLevel: A small minority, primarily composed of the standards developers and closely aligned technology vendors.
keyArguments: The IGs have been vetted and tested, particularly the FHIR-to-X12 translation, which is production-ready, according to the HL7 Da Vinci Project.
Testing has been sufficient, and payers are "anxious to begin receiving FHIR documents from providers," as stated by Mettle Solutions.
The IGs have reached a level of maturity that supports wider and expanded formalized testing, rather than needing to start from scratch, a point made by Kaiser Permanente.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: A premature mandate will worsen administrative burden, increase costs for already strained practices, introduce new workflow problems, and ultimately delay patient care.
specificPoints: They consistently champion the needs of small, rural, independent, and specialty practices, arguing a one-size-fits-all rollout will fail these critical providers, as noted by RRC and MGMA.

Mandate pilots before rulemaking; require public reporting of ROI and burden reduction metrics from pilots; test in diverse settings; provide financial incentives for provider participation in testing.

The RRC notes that ePA is unique among HIPAA transactions because it "impact[s] patients directly, and the need for thorough testing cannot be overemphasized". ACLA describes disastrous real-world consequences when payers launch new programs without adequate testing, resulting in a reversion to "fax submission and/or phone call to payers involving long hold times often without resolution".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The high cost and complexity of development and testing without a clear, standardized protocol or a proven business case; ambiguity on how to test at scale.
specificPoints: They highlight the practical, technical barriers to testing. Allscripts notes it is "Unclear how testing between Allscripts' software and payers/health plans will occur" and that they cannot "test efficiently at scale, not with each payer separately".

ONC should provide a standardized testing protocol and a reference implementation or "test harness" for vendors to test against, as suggested by Drummond Group and Allscripts. They also stress that market validation must precede standardization, a point from Voluware.
Lantana Consulting Group argues that pilots with "well-documented findings will be critical to show cost savings, which would incentivize Health IT CIOs to implement without additional incentives".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring true end-to-end interoperability, especially between different standards (e.g., FHIR and X12); avoiding being mandated to use a standard that has not been proven at scale.
specificPoints: They emphasize the need to test the entire ecosystem, including multiple proprietary systems and utilization management (UM) vendors, not just a single payer-provider dyad, as noted by Cigna.
Create a HIPAA "innovation exception" to allow for real-world testing of new standards, a recommendation from Anthem and WEDI. Conduct end-to-end pilots to get a better view of paths forward.
BCBSA warns that "Certifications performed in a demonstration environment do not always translate during implementation and can cause issues with interoperability".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring a robust and reliable process for maturing standards; having adequate artifacts (test scripts, data) to enable effective testing.
specificPoints: They provide deep technical insights into the flaws of current testing. AEGIS.net reveals that in their experience, over 50% of technically "successful" (200 OK status) FHIR message exchanges were actually non-conformant with the IG, highlighting a critical gap in simple validation.

Fund a "proving ground" for maturing IGs, a suggestion from HL7. Develop and require the use of detailed testing artifacts (TestScripts, test data) within IGs to remove ambiguity, as recommended by AEGIS.net. Streamline the HIPAA exceptions process to facilitate testing, as proposed by WEDI.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data-backed observation on testing failure: AEGIS.net provided a critical, data-driven insight that challenges the very definition of a successful test. Their finding that "In more than 50% of the Test Executions completed with a status 200 OK... the FHIR message... was found to be non‐conformant" proves that simple connectivity is not a measure of valid interoperability, and more rigorous conformance testing is essential.

Compelling real-world failure example: The American Clinical Laboratory Association (ACLA) powerfully illustrates the consequences of inadequate testing, describing how payers launching unprepared systems forced providers to revert to "decades-old technology like facsimile machines and phone calls" with untrained staff, leading to chaos and unresolved PA requests. This makes the abstract risk of a premature mandate concrete and visceral.

Unexpected connection to workflow burden: The American Medical Informatics Association (AMIA) offers a crucial insight: even if an ePA system is technically efficient, it could simply shift the administrative burden. They warn, "if electronic prior authorization creates efficiencies but then causes time constraints (e.g., requiring providers to address many in-basket messages about denials/approvals), efficiency may be lost, and patient care could suffer". This highlights the need to evaluate the entire clinical workflow, not just the transaction itself.

Regulatory process as a barrier: WEDI points out that the HIPAA statute itself, by mandating current standards, can create a legal barrier to the real-world testing of new, innovative standards. This identifies a systemic issue that may require a specific regulatory fix, such as an "innovation exception," to enable progress.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type patterns: There is a stark and consistent pattern of concern for providers in non-institutional or under-resourced settings. Commenters repeatedly single out small, rural, specialty, and safety-net providers (like FQHCs) as being most vulnerable to a flawed rollout due to their limited IT budgets and staff (RRC, OCHIN, MGMA).

Experience correlations: A clear pattern emerges based on a stakeholder's role in the ecosystem. Those closest to developing the standards (HL7 Da Vinci Project, Mettle Solutions) express the most confidence in their maturity. In contrast, those responsible for implementing, paying for, and using the standards in the real world (providers, payers, IT vendors) are overwhelmingly cautious and demand more proof.

Unintended consequences: Multiple commenters identify potential negative knock-on effects beyond simple technical failure. These include shifting administrative burden to different tasks or staff members (AMIA), increasing short-term costs that practices cannot bear (RRC), and creating a loss of industry faith in future IT initiatives if this one is rolled out poorly (MGMA).

keyQuotations.quote: [Of] all HIPAA transactions, e-PA are the only ones that impact patients directly, and the need for thorough testing cannot be overemphasized.
Market validation must precede standardization.

More real-world testing is needed in physician practices of all sizes and medical specialties. Before requiring the guides to be included in EHR certification, the AMA urges ONC to make sure the technology functions well across practice settings and in the real-world verses “happy-path” demonstrations...
In more than 50% of the Test Executions completed with a status 200 OK [meaning the message was received], the FHIR message (information exchange) was found to be non‐conformant to the FHIR IG...
We would ask the ONC to fund pilot projects and find out the risks and benefits, plus effort involved, prior to proposing as certification criteria.
Standards that have not completed robust real-world testing are not suitable for mandated use in health policy.

...if electronic prior authorization creates efficiencies but then causes time constraints (e.g., requiring providers to address many in-basket messages about denials/approvals), efficiency may be lost, and patient care could suffer.
Testing would be extensive beyond almost anything rolled out recently.
Promoting or requiring the use of any standard before it is proven successful in the marketplace is problematic and concerning given the scale that the standards would need to be used nationwide...

For months while the payer’s programming and testing that should have occurred prior to implementation remains in process, prior authorization has been by fax submission and/or phone call to payers involving long hold times often without resolution.
We caution ONC from adopting any standard transaction until it has been fully tested with multiple entities sending and receiving.

Giving developers the time to adequately test the ePA APIs is imperative to ensuring their functionality maps onto clinical workflows without adding more administrative and clinical burden to healthcare entities which adopt them.

6.3.themeDescription: Debate on Mandatory Adoption by All Stakeholders

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on this theme demonstrates an overwhelming and cross-stakeholder consensus: electronic prior authorization will only succeed if all parties—providers, payers, and vendors—are required to adopt standardized processes simultaneously. Commenters vividly describe the current market failure as a "chicken and egg" stalemate where voluntary adoption has led to fragmented implementation and burdensome dual workflows. The dominant recommendation is for regulators to implement synchronized, symmetrical mandates for all stakeholders to finally break the gridlock and achieve the long-promised benefits of a fully electronic system.

consensusPoints.text: Widespread, synchronized adoption by all stakeholders is essential for success. An overwhelming majority of commenters across all stakeholder types—providers, payers, vendors, and their associations—agree that electronic Prior Authorization (ePA) can only succeed if all parties are required to adopt and use the same standardized processes. Voluntary or partial adoption is seen as the primary reason for past failures and the main obstacle to future progress.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters who addressed this theme (at least 45 distinct commenters) explicitly or implicitly supported this view.

exceptions.text: While the goal of universal adoption is universally supported, a small minority (e.g., Olive AI) suggests that market-based incentives and transparency could be used to drive adoption in addition to, or as an alternative to, strict mandates.

consensusPoints.text: Voluntary adoption leads to fragmented, dual workflows. There is broad agreement that allowing voluntary adoption forces stakeholders, particularly providers, to maintain multiple systems: the new electronic process for a small fraction of payers and the old manual processes (phone, fax, portals) for everyone else. This negates any potential efficiency gains and can increase administrative burden.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, especially provider groups and health IT vendors.

positions.label: Mandate All Stakeholders
positions.stance: The government must require all parties—payers, providers, and health IT vendors—to adopt and use standardized ePA processes concurrently.
positions.supportLevel: A vast majority of commenters, including nearly all provider groups, vendor associations, and several large payers/payer groups.
keyArguments: Voluntary adoption has demonstrably failed, creating a "chicken and egg" stalemate where no single party will invest without certainty that others will.
Only a universal mandate can create the network effects necessary to achieve full burden reduction and ROI for all parties.
Mandates are necessary to force the market past the "wait and see" approach adopted by many vendors and payers.

positions.label: Incentivize Adoption
positions.stance: Use market forces, financial incentives, and public reporting to drive adoption rather than relying solely on strict mandates.
positions.supportLevel: A small but notable minority, primarily a single technology vendor.
keyArguments: Market competition can be a powerful driver; making payer ePA performance metrics (e.g., turnaround times) public would empower consumers to choose more efficient plans.
Incentives, similar to those in the early days of Meaningful Use, could spur industry-wide adoption without the rigidity of a mandate.
Mandates on their own don't guarantee a good user experience; incentives can encourage innovation.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The crushing administrative burden of maintaining dual workflows (manual and electronic); the high cost and inefficiency of navigating thousands of unique, proprietary payer portals and forms; and the resulting delays in patient care.
specificPoints: They are on the frontline of the fragmentation. They emphasize that even if their EHRs are certified and capable, the system fails because payers are not required to connect in a standardized way.
Mandate that all payers (especially Medicare Advantage, Medicaid MCOs, and commercial plans) adopt a single, standardized, and automated ePA process that integrates into their EHR workflow.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The risk of investing heavily in building APIs and ePA infrastructure that goes unused by providers ("one-sided requirements"); the operational cost of maintaining parallel systems if provider adoption remains voluntary.
specificPoints: They frame the issue as needing "symmetrical" or "two-sided" requirements. They stress that they can build the technological "roads," but providers and their vendors must be compelled to "drive on them" for the investment to be worthwhile.
If payers are mandated to build APIs, then providers must be concurrently mandated or strongly incentivized to use them, and EHR vendors must be required to build the connections.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The lack of a business case to invest in developing ePA solutions for a voluntary, fragmented market; the technical impracticality of supporting countless proprietary payer systems; the risk that costly certification will be for naught if providers don't use the features.
specificPoints: They provide a crucial insight that mandates are not a reward for mature standards, but a catalyst for their development and maturity. They describe a "wait and see approach" that can only be broken by regulation.
Implement a clear, comprehensive mandate for all parties simultaneously. Make ePA a required, not optional, part of certification to create market certainty and justify development investment.

noteworthyInsights.insight: Mandates Drive Maturity, Not the Other Way Around - AEGIS.net, an IT testing vendor, offers a powerful counterargument to a "wait and see" approach. They provide direct evidence that standards and implementation guides only mature rapidly after a regulatory mandate creates a real-world incentive for implementers to participate in testing and development. They argue that "getting to 'ready for adoption' is accelerated by such mandates, not a prerequisite for them."

Partial Adoption May Increase Denials - Medical Mutual of Ohio presents a compelling, counter-intuitive risk. If payers are mandated to provide faster turnaround times but providers are not mandated to use the electronic system, providers using manual methods may fail to submit documentation in time, leading to an increase in denials and appeals, worsening the problem.

Quantifying the Dual Workflow - The American Medical Informatics Association puts a number on the central problem, stating that "An estimated 75% of providers who use electronic prior authorization also use other prior authorization methods." This single statistic vividly illustrates the failure of partial adoption.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The "Chicken and Egg" Stalemate is the Dominant Narrative: Commenters from all groups repeatedly describe a market failure where providers wait for payers to adopt universal standards, payers wait for providers to adopt capable technology, and vendors wait for both to create a viable market. This deadlock is cited as the primary justification for a comprehensive, synchronized federal mandate to force all parties to move at once.

Mandate Sequencing is Critical: The debate has moved beyond if mandates are needed to how they must be structured. There is a strong pattern of demand for symmetrical, concurrent requirements. Commenters repeatedly warn that a mandate on one stakeholder group without corresponding requirements on the others will be ineffective and potentially counterproductive.

Learning from Past Failures: Multiple commenters reference the failure of previous voluntary efforts as the reason mandates are now seen as the only viable path forward. The experience with slow, limited voluntary API use (CyncHealth) and the success of mandates in other areas like e-prescribing (Troy Kaji MD) are used to frame the current debate.

keyQuotations.quote: [W]e would like to see collaboration and vigorous testing of planned guidance on a voluntary basis, our experience has shown that the real work in molding specifications comes after it has been directed for use.
One-sided requirements... will not result in the broad adoption necessary to achieve the benefits.

It is entirely unreasonable to expect us [EHR vendors] to carry the weight of this only on our side when success cannot happen without commitment from EHR software developers and the payers / health plans on the other end.
A problematic situation would arise if not all payers participate in an integrated system or network. This would require providers to track participating and non-participating payers.
Without universal buy-in, efforts to standardize and automate this complex administrative process would be fruitless.

Currently, payers may be waiting for providers to adopt EHR systems with PA capabilities, while providers are waiting for payers to implement uniform standards for PA requests and responses. As a result, adoption has been slower than anticipated.
Waiting for providers to voluntarily share information with health care consumers does not work. The federal government must require providers to interact with payer APIs...

However, unless the standards are adopted by all involved, providers will have to continue to navigate the prior authorization process in the same ad hoc manner as they do currently, in addition to implementing new technologies.
If only a small percentage of a provider’s patients are covered by these plans, providers are unlikely to invest in a technology solution for such a small fraction of their PA volume.

Moving providers from voluntary adoption to mandated adoption will allow the industry to gain the operational efficiencies and reduce administrative burdens; thereby, truly achieving the goal of improving health outcomes for patients.
A streamlined, standardized ePA process should be utilized across all payers and pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) to significantly alleviate time and costs.
True interoperability will only occur when all actors in the health care system are held to the same standards.

6.4.themeDescription: Opposition to Mandating Provider Use of ePA

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on this theme is one of unified and emphatic opposition to mandating that providers use electronic prior authorization (ePA) systems at this time. A broad consensus across all stakeholder types—including provider organizations, health IT vendors, and even a major payer—argues that such a mandate is premature and would unfairly burden clinicians before payers have implemented effective, standardized solutions and vendors have created user-friendly products. The dominant recommendation is to first ensure payer and vendor readiness, and then to encourage voluntary provider adoption through positive incentives rather than penalties, ensuring flexibility and choice for diverse practice settings.

consensusPoints.text: Payers and vendors must adopt mature, standardized solutions before any provider requirements are considered. Nearly all commenters explicitly state or strongly imply that the responsibility for developing, implementing, and standardizing ePA technology lies with payers and health IT vendors first. They argue it is illogical and unfair to mandate use of a system by the final link in the chain (providers) before the foundational infrastructure is stable and effective.

exceptions.text: The American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP) states, "Vendor and payer adoption of specific standards must precede any required adoption by physicians." EHR vendor Cerner Corporation argues against mandating provider use "before payers have established clear implementation standards." The American Medical Association (AMA) recommends that before requiring physician participation, "ONC and CMS should address specific gaps in implementation and adoption across the ecosystem."

consensusPoints.text: A provider mandate would disproportionately harm small, rural, and under-resourced practices. A strong majority of commenters, particularly provider associations and those representing non-hospital settings, express grave concern that a one-size-fits-all mandate would crush practices that lack the financial resources, IT infrastructure, and dedicated staff of large health systems. Many of these practices were ineligible for past incentives like the Meaningful Use program.

exceptions.text: The South Carolina Medical Association (SCMA) warns a mandate could "push physicians out of rural areas and small practices where a lack of broadband and the need for low overhead precludes electronic record keeping." The American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) notes that physical therapists in private practice and post-acute care facilities "will be much less likely to use electronic prior authorization capabilities...because they were ineligible for the Meaningful Use EHR Incentive Program." UnityPoint Health recommends HHS "phase-in enforcement efforts and penalties for providers, especially for smaller providers or those in settings without past Meaningful Use support."

consensusPoints.text: Mandating provider use would diminish incentives for vendors and payers to innovate and improve ePA systems. Several commenters make the sophisticated argument that if providers are forced to use whatever is available, the market pressure on vendors and payers to create efficient, effective, and user-friendly solutions will evaporate. A mandate would essentially lock in mediocre technology.

exceptions.text: Both the AAFP and the North Carolina Academy of Family Physicians (NCAFP) warn, "If electronic prior authorization is mandated for clinicians, incentives for CEHRT vendors and payers to implement efficient, effective solutions will diminish." Health IT developer Voluware, Inc. cautions, "Standards and certifications on provider workflows may have the effect of potentially inhibiting much needed innovation and progress if not carefully considered."

areasOfDebate.description: The primary debate is not about whether to mandate provider use—commenters are united against it—but about the best alternative path to encourage adoption.

positions.label: Positive Incentives
positions.stance: Promote voluntary provider adoption using financial rewards and positive adjustments in quality programs.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority of commenters, including healthcare alliances and technology companies.
keyArguments: Incentives spur industry-wide adoption without punishing providers who are not yet ready. This "carrot" approach avoids increasing provider burden and burnout while still encouraging progress.

positions.label: Provider Autonomy
positions.stance: Guarantee providers the explicit choice and technical ability to opt-in or opt-out of ePA systems based on their practice's needs and workflows.
positions.supportLevel: Articulated most forcefully by a practicing physician, with supporting sentiment from other provider groups.
keyArguments: A simple incentive may not be enough; providers need direct control over their workflows to prevent inefficient systems from being forced upon them. An "ON/OFF switch" is necessary to avoid uncompensated work and ensure ePA is only used when it is truly more efficient than alternatives (like for inexpensive generic drugs).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Increased administrative burden, exacerbating burnout, negative financial impacts on practices, and harm to patient care if forced to use flawed systems.
specificPoints: They represent the frontline clinicians who would bear the direct consequences of a premature mandate. They consistently frame the issue as one of logical sequencing: payers and vendors must build the system before providers can be expected to use it.
A near-unanimous recommendation against provider mandates. They advocate for exemptions from quality program penalties and focusing all initial regulatory pressure on payers and vendors.
The AMA worries a mandate would shift focus from "true PA reform" to "federal program compliance". APTA highlights how non-physician providers who missed out on prior IT funding would be left behind.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Being forced to develop and certify products against unclear or immature standards; a premature provider mandate creating conflicting priorities and development challenges.
specificPoints: As the builders of the technology, they emphasize the need for technical and implementation clarity from the payer side before provider-facing certification criteria are established.
Uniformly recommend delaying any certification criteria for provider-focused Health IT until payer-side APIs are implemented, stable, and standardized.
The Electronic Health Record Association (EHRA) states plainly, "Do not set certification criteria for provider-focused health IT until clarity exists..."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The practical, on-the-ground reality of workflow disruption, uncompensated time spent on administrative tasks, and the direct costs of implementation and training.
specificPoints: They provide concrete examples of how a mandate would fail in practice. They are less focused on high-level policy and more on the day-to-day operational impact.
Demand granular control over technology ("ON/OFF switch") and a phased-in approach to any future enforcement that recognizes the real-world sequence of vendor build -> testing -> provider training.

New York Urology Specialists calculates that even with ePA, "80% of the work will still be manual" for providers. UnityPoint Health emphasizes that provider implementation is entirely dependent on a prior "vendor (software developer) build".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring there are viable, non-electronic channels for receiving prior authorizations, as universal provider adoption of new standards is unrealistic.
specificPoints: As a recipient of PA requests, CVS Health provides a crucial reality check, confirming that even with existing electronic portals, a huge volume of requests still arrives via fax. This validates provider concerns about the slow pace of real-world adoption.
Policy must "acknowledge that not all providers will use new ePA standards."

noteworthyInsights.insight: Unexpected Corroboration from a Payer: CVS Health provides a powerful, unexpected validation of provider concerns. By stating that "providers cannot be expected to universally adopt" ePA standards and that "50-100% of the providers submit prior authorizations through faxes" in many markets, they confirm that the transition to electronic methods is far from complete, making a provider mandate entirely premature.

The "ON/OFF Switch" Concept: The demand from New York Urology Specialists for an "ON/OFF switch" is a brilliant, practical insight. It reframes the debate from simple adoption to one of provider autonomy and workflow control, acknowledging that "electronic" does not always mean "efficient."

Compelling Personal Example: The same urologist provides a perfect real-world example of rational non-adoption: forgoing a burdensome ePA process for a generic drug that a patient can buy for $20 at Costco. This highlights that a mandate would force inefficient behavior.

The Perverse Incentive Argument: The insight from AAFP and NCAFP that a provider mandate would reduce the incentive for vendors and payers to build good products is a sophisticated economic argument that highlights a critical unintended consequence of the proposed policy.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Facility-type patterns: A clear divide exists between the concerns of large, integrated systems and those of smaller, independent, or non-physician practices. Commenters representing family physicians (AAFP), physical therapists (APTA), rural doctors (SCMA), and post-acute care (CMS Data Element Library Workgroup) are most vocal about the disproportionate harm from a mandate due to lack of resources and exclusion from past IT incentive programs.

Unintended consequences: A recurring pattern is the fear that a well-intentioned mandate to accelerate ePA adoption would backfire. It would increase, not decrease, provider burden; stifle, not encourage, innovation; and shift focus from genuine PA reform to mere compliance.

The Dependency Chain: Commenters consistently illustrate a clear "dependency chain": provider adoption depends on vendor-developed tools, which in turn depend on standardized, functional payer APIs. There is a universal belief that any mandates must follow this chain, starting with payers, then vendors, and only then considering providers.

keyQuotations.quote: [P]roviders cannot be expected to universally adopt any ePA standards established by the Department. In many of our markets, 50-100% of the providers submit prior authorizations through faxes, even when we currently provide a provider portal.
Mandating electronic prior authorization could push physicians out of rural areas and small practices where a lack of broadband and the need for low overhead precludes electronic record keeping.
If electronic prior authorization is mandated for clinicians, incentives for CEHRT vendors and payers to implement efficient, effective solutions will diminish.
Certification criteria should require EHRs to offer an 'ON/OFF switch' for ePA service, controlled by the healthcare provider.

The goal should be to make it easier to submit and have industry standards to create efficiency and reduce burden by facilitating the use of electronic prior authorization without penalizing a setting for not having certified program status.

Any attempt to make electronic prior authorization requirements mandatory for providers prior to a clear and concise implementation plan being drafted and released will significantly increase burden on the health system.
Premier recommends that providers be incentivized to adopt automated technology and approaches for prior authorization processes, rather than mandated to do so.

Physical therapists...and long-term and post-acute care facilities will be much less likely to use electronic prior authorization capabilities...because they were ineligible for the Meaningful Use EHR Incentive Program.
Requiring physicians to use ePA prematurely would shift industry focus to federal program compliance instead of true PA reform.
HHS should phase-in enforcement efforts and penalties for providers, especially for smaller providers or those in settings without past Meaningful Use support.

6.5.themeDescription: Call for Provider Incentives and Financial Support

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus among all stakeholder groups that positive incentives and financial support are essential for successful provider adoption of electronic prior authorization (ePA). While a minority believe a truly efficient system would be its own reward, the vast majority argue that significant upfront costs, workflow disruption, and an uncertain return on investment make direct support necessary. Dominant recommendations center on integrating ePA into MIPS, providing targeted grants and technical assistance for smaller and under-resourced practices, and implementing measures to prevent EHR vendors from passing exorbitant costs onto providers.

consensusPoints.text: Positive Incentives are Essential for Adoption: A near-unanimous majority of commenters agree that some form of positive incentive, financial support, or technical assistance is critical to drive provider adoption of ePA. Support for this view comes from every stakeholder category, including provider groups like AHIMA, payers like Anthem, Inc., and providers like Ascension. The core agreement is that mandates alone are insufficient and that "carrots" are needed to overcome adoption barriers.

exceptions.text: A small minority, such as Greenway Health, RadNet, and Cleveland Clinic, posit that a truly efficient and burden-reducing ePA process would be its own incentive. However, even these commenters do not oppose the idea of additional support and acknowledge provider concerns about workflow changes and costs.

consensusPoints.text: Targeted Support for Under-Resourced Providers is Crucial: A strong majority of commenters, particularly provider associations, government agencies, and advocacy groups, emphasize that smaller, rural, safety-net, and specialty (e.g., behavioral health, long-term care) practices require specific, targeted financial and technical support. There is broad agreement that these providers, as highlighted by commenters like the Association for Clinical Oncology and the California Health and Human Services Agency, face the greatest adoption barriers and are at the highest risk of being left behind, which would exacerbate health inequities.

Providers Should Not Bear the Full Cost: A strong consensus exists among provider groups and healthcare systems that the financial burden of developing and implementing ePA technology should not fall solely or disproportionately on them. Commenters such as Cleveland Clinic and the American College of Surgeons frequently pointed out that EHR vendors pass on development costs and that payers, who created the PA burden, stand to benefit significantly from automation.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether the intrinsic value of an efficient ePA system is sufficient for provider adoption, or if extrinsic financial incentives are necessary to overcome implementation barriers.

positions.label: Intrinsic Motivation
positions.stance: A well-designed ePA system that is genuinely faster and more efficient than manual processes will be its own incentive for adoption.
positions.supportLevel: A notable minority, primarily some Health IT vendors and large provider systems
keyArguments: Providers will be naturally drawn to any tool that organically improves workflow and reduces administrative costs
past experience with real-time tools shows that clear benefits drive adoption without mandates

positions.label: Extrinsic Incentives
positions.stance: Intrinsic benefits are insufficient to overcome significant implementation costs, workflow disruption, and training burdens; direct financial incentives are required.
positions.supportLevel: The overwhelming majority of commenters across all stakeholder types
keyArguments: Upfront costs for technology and training are prohibitive, especially for small practices
return on investment is not guaranteed or immediate
providers fear shifting burdens to a new, complex workflow

areasOfDebate.description: Debate concerning which stakeholder group—payers, vendors, or providers—should be primarily responsible for the financial costs of implementing ePA.

positions.label: Payers Pay
positions.stance: Since payers impose prior authorization and reap savings from it, they should bear the cost of implementing the electronic solution.
positions.supportLevel: Several influential provider organizations
keyArguments: PA is an uncompensated mandate imposed by payers
it is inequitable to ask providers to pay to fix a problem created by payers for their own financial benefit

positions.label: Stop Vendor Fees
positions.stance: The primary financial barrier is EHR vendors passing on upgrade costs and charging prohibitive fees for new functionality. Policy should focus on preventing this.
positions.supportLevel: Several provider groups and a technology developer
keyArguments: Providers are a captive market for their EHR vendor
high fees for updates or third-party app integration stifle innovation and make adoption unaffordable

positions.label: Shared Investment
positions.stance: All stakeholders, including developers, clearinghouses, insurers, and providers, benefit from an efficient system and should share in the investment.
positions.supportLevel: A small number of large, integrated systems
keyArguments: A systems-level problem requires a systems-level investment
singling out one party is unproductive

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Prohibitive costs passed on by EHR vendors; the uncompensated nature of PA work; the disproportionate impact on small, rural, and specialty practices.
specificPoints: Most vocal in arguing that since payers created the PA burden, they should bear the costs of its automation. They also provide historical context, such as the exclusion of physical therapists from past incentives, as noted by APTA.

Proposed solutions include direct financial support/grants for practices, incorporating ePA into MIPS, policies to ensure vendors do not pass on excessive costs, and establishing new CPT codes to compensate for PA work.
The Texas Medical Association argues for new payment mechanisms. The American College of Physicians highlights that under-resourced practices who suffer most are least able to afford ePA.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Low provider adoption of ePA will render payer investments in API technology useless and inefficient.
specificPoints: They are strong proponents for provider incentives, framing it as a necessary condition for the success of the entire system. They recognize that without provider uptake, they will be forced to maintain costly dual systems (e.g., X12 and FHIR).
Urge CMS/ONC to create MIPS incentives for providers, provide technical assistance, and seek congressional funding for providers with low EHR adoption (e.g., behavioral health).
Anthem explicitly recommends developing provider incentives before issuing a final rule. AHIP notes that incentivizing both EHR vendors and clinicians is required.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Vendors, Consultants & Certification Bodies
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Overcoming provider resistance to change; ensuring a return on their development investment through provider adoption; the high cost of development and testing.
specificPoints: They provide a ground-level view of implementation challenges. Mettle Solutions offers a stark example of how EHR vendor fees for third-party app installation directly stifle innovation.

Proposed solutions include MIPS incentives, government-funded education and training, reviving programs like Regional Extension Centers, as suggested by WEDI, and even restricting EHRs from charging fees for standard-compliant apps, a point made by Mettle.
Greenway Health requests direct ONC support for vendors via JIRA boards and town halls. Point-of-Care Partners emphasizes the need to incentivize creation of holistic workflow solutions.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Upfront costs of implementation and training; workflow disruption; ensuring a clear return on investment (ROI); the burden on staff.
specificPoints: OCHIN, representing safety-net clinics, uniquely stresses the need to fund workforce development and training, not just technology. NY Urology provides the raw perspective of a small practice on the "uncompensated financial burden" of PA.
Proposed solutions include direct funding for specific settings (e.g., community mental health centers), MIPS incentives, and ensuring all stakeholders contribute to investment costs.
UnityPoint Health recommends upfront funding for ambulatory and community mental health centers. RadNet states plainly that if a phone call is faster, they won't use ePA.

noteworthyInsights.insight: EHR Vendor Fees as an Innovation Bottleneck: Mettle Solutions provides a crucial insight that goes beyond general concerns about cost. They articulate that EHR vendors charging fees for the installation of third-party SMART on FHIR apps—even free ones—creates a direct financial barrier that stifles the very innovation and interoperability the regulation aims to foster.

A Call to Revive a Proven Model (RECs): WEDI's recommendation to re-establish the Regional Extension Centers (RECs) is a highly practical and powerful suggestion. It grounds the abstract need for "technical assistance" in a specific, previously successful federal program, providing a concrete roadmap for supporting small and rural practices.

The Asymmetric Financial Incentive: Dr. Shteynshlyuger of NY Urology compellingly frames the issue as one of worsening financial asymmetry. He argues that ePA automation provides "80%+ savings to health plans, minimal financial benefit to physician practices," and that without direct provider compensation, automation simply speeds up uncompensated labor, making the imbalance even more severe.

Technology is Not Enough, Fund the Workforce: OCHIN delivers a vital message that support cannot only be for technology. Their insight that "Modernized technology and national standards are key ingredients... but not sufficient without the workforce able to deploy the systems as intended" highlights the critical need for funding training and workforce development in resource-challenged clinics.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Payer-Provider Alignment on Incentives: A striking pattern is the strong alignment between payers like Anthem and AHIP and providers on the need for provider incentives. While their motivations differ (providers seek to offset costs, payers seek to ensure uptake of their APIs), this shared conclusion creates powerful momentum for an incentive-based approach.

Facility-Type Differentiation: Views on incentives vary by provider type. Small, solo, and safety-net practices like NY Urology and OCHIN focus on direct grants and technical support to overcome fundamental resource gaps. Large, integrated systems like Cleveland Clinic and Kaiser are more likely to discuss MIPS incentives and system-wide workflow investments.

The Vendor Cost Choke Point: An implicit pattern across many provider comments from groups like AAFP, ACP, and AAHKS, and made explicit by the vendor Mettle, is the identification of the Certified EHR vendor as a key financial gatekeeper whose business model of passing on costs is a primary obstacle to the regulation's success.

keyQuotations.quote: "[PA] cannot be a 'money-losing activity' for providers nor a 'jackpot' for health plans... this 'asymmetric financial incentive will worsen with automation.'"

"Under-resourced health systems and practices currently suffer the most in terms of prior authorization burden. They are also the least likely to be able to afford the implementation expenses of electronic prior authorization."
"Absent that ROI [Return on Investment], especially in times of economic uncertainty, providers will be very unlikely to invest resources in untried and untested technologies."
"The costs of removing patient barriers should not be born solely by providers who would need to upgrade their EHR systems to implement new ONC standards that help increase efficiency for payors..."
"Conversely, providers will have no incentive to automate transactions if they have to wait days for a decision when they can get a response faster via a phone call or portal."

"ONC and HHS must fund safety-net provider engagement to enable them to... achieve capacity and sufficient time for clinical integration, and provide personnel training and obtain technical assistance and support."
"Anthem recommends that 'ONC and CMS develop provider incentives before issuing a final rule to ensure significant provider participation in ePA and other data exchange initiatives'."
"Mettle believes that the fees imposed by the DEVELOP vendors [EHRs] are a barrier to most physicians [for downloading and installing SMART on FHIR apps]."
"The repercussions of excluding physical therapists from Meaningful Use, leaving them without guidance or funding for CEHRT adoption, are mounting."
"Modernized technology and national standards are key ingredients to optimized delivery and interoperability, but not sufficient without the workforce able to deploy the systems as intended."
"To increase provider adoption, there should be direct incentives for ePA tool use, including EHR inclusion."
"The ACS believes that since payers created the additional practice costs related to staff resources due to PA, they should absorb the additional expenses associated with implementing e-PA."

6.6.themeDescription: Requirement for Payer Certification and Accountability

sections.executiveSummary: An overwhelming consensus among providers, health IT vendors, and professional organizations demands that payers and their IT systems be subject to the same certification, standards, and accountability measures as providers and their EHRs. This call for regulatory parity is rooted in deep-seated distrust and past experiences of payer non-compliance, with stakeholders warning that any reform will fail if the burden is placed solely on the provider side. While payers express a willingness to adopt new standards, the dominant recommendation is for a federal mandate requiring payer API certification, enforceable response times, and penalties for non-compliance to create a truly interoperable and equitable system.

consensusPoints.text: Payer Accountability is Essential for Success. Nearly all commenters across every stakeholder category—including providers, health IT vendors, professional organizations, advocacy groups, and even some payers—agree that any electronic prior authorization (ePA) system will fail if payers are not held accountable. They argue that the current one-sided regulatory burden on providers is unsustainable and illogical for a two-way transaction. At least 45 distinct commenters explicitly state that payers must be required to participate, be held to the same standards, or be certified. This is the single most dominant point of agreement in this theme. For example, Allscripts stated, "It is entirely unreasonable to expect us [EHR vendors] to carry the weight of this only on our side when success cannot happen without commitment from EHR software developers and the payers / health plans on the other end." The Federation of American Hospitals noted, "Certification criteria will be wasteful and lack utility if payers are not accountable for engaging with providers and patients in the development, maintenance, and use of electronic prior authorization technology." The American College of Cardiology added, "True interoperability will only occur when all actors in the health care system are held to the same standards."

exceptions.text: While agreeing on the need for payer participation, payers themselves (e.g., UnitedHealth Group, Anthem, BCBSA) emphasize the need for flexibility, ramp-up periods, and consideration of their unique workflows, stopping short of demanding a rigid certification mandate for themselves. One technical expert expressed skepticism that certification was the correct tool to solve underlying integration challenges.

consensusPoints.text: Standardized Payer APIs are a Prerequisite. A strong majority of commenters, particularly health IT vendors and provider organizations, state that requiring payers to build, certify, and expose standardized, non-proprietary APIs is a foundational requirement. They believe this will drive adoption on the provider/EHR side and is the necessary first step toward true interoperability. Over 25 commenters specifically call for payers to be required to implement and maintain standards-based APIs (e.g., FHIR-based). For example, Greenway Health, LLC stated, "The presence of standardized payer APIs for prior authorizations is essential to encourage health IT developers to adopt corresponding standards." MEDITECH recommended that, "ONC should work with CMS to focus adoption and certification of CRD, DTR, and PAS IGs by payers to establish clear implementation standards for payer interactions supporting prior authorization." Premier Inc. stated, "CMS needs to require payers to operationalize automated prior authorization programs and implement Provider-Access open, standards-based APIs to ensure payer transparency..."

exceptions.text: UnitedHealth Group cautions that standards adoption should not preclude payers from pursuing other innovative (potentially proprietary) approaches to interoperability where appropriate.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over the best method for ensuring payer compliance, ranging from a formal, government-mandated certification program to more flexible compatibility testing.

positions.label: Formal Certification
positions.stance: Mandate a formal, ONC-style certification program for payer IT systems, APIs, and ePA workflows.
positions.supportLevel: The majority view, held by nearly all health IT vendors, most provider organizations, and professional associations.
keyArguments: Creates a level playing field and 'rules of the road' for all participants; ensures technical conformity with standards (e.g., Da Vinci IGs); provides a clear mechanism for enforcement and public oversight.

positions.label: Compatibility Testing
positions.stance: Require payer systems to demonstrate compatibility with certified provider EHRs using common testing tools, without a full certification mandate.
positions.supportLevel: A minority view, most clearly articulated by one major health IT vendor.
keyArguments: Focuses on the practical outcome (interoperability) rather than the process (certification); avoids the administrative overhead and cost associated with formal certification, which might discourage payer adoption.

positions.label: Certification is Ineffective
positions.stance: Argues that certifying a payer's ePA module is insufficient because the real challenge is integrating that module with the payer's core legacy administrative platform.
positions.supportLevel: A single, but technically detailed, individual comment.
keyArguments: Payers are beholden to their main platform vendors, not a new certified ePA tool; integration between a new certified platform and the core system is non-standard and complex, making the certification of the 'front door' potentially meaningless.

areasOfDebate.description: Discussion on whether mandates should focus narrowly on technical API standards or broadly to include operational business rules and performance metrics.

positions.label: Technical API Focus
positions.stance: Mandates should primarily center on payer adoption and certification of the technical, standards-based APIs (e.g., Da Vinci CRD, DTR, PAS IGs).
positions.supportLevel: Primarily Health IT vendors and standards bodies.
keyArguments: Provides a clear, testable, and concrete first step; establishes the technical foundation upon which all other process improvements can be built; a 'payer-first' certification approach for APIs de-risks development for EHR vendors.

positions.label: Broad Operational Rules
positions.stance: Mandates must extend beyond technology to include business practice rules like strict response timeframes, transparency requirements, and public reporting of metrics.
positions.supportLevel: Nearly all provider groups, professional organizations, and patient advocates.
keyArguments: Technical APIs are useless if the underlying payer processes remain slow, opaque, or unfair; accountability must include performance standards (e.g., 24-hour response for urgent requests) and transparency (e.g., publishing denial rates) to truly reduce provider burden and prevent patient harm.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense, one-sided administrative burden of the current system; patient care delays and harm; lack of transparency into payer rules and denial reasons.
specificPoints: They experience the direct, daily consequences of payer inaction and inconsistency. Their comments are filled with frustration and a deep-seated distrust that payers will act in good faith without mandates.

They propose to mandate everything: payer certification, use of standard APIs, strict response timeframes (e.g., 24/72 hours), public reporting of PA metrics, penalties for non-compliance, and enforcement against retroactive denials.
As proposed by Allina Health, some recommend mandating that payers first attempt to retrieve necessary clinical data from provider USCDI FHIR APIs before requesting additional documentation.
The AAP states, "...there is no equivalent certification or penalty for payers to not follow the standard."
The Texas Medical Association is "not optimistic that payers will invest in the necessary technology tools."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Wasting development resources on certified EHR features that connect to nothing; business risk of a one-sided mandate; creating a workable, scalable technical solution.
specificPoints: They see the issue as a 'chicken-and-egg' problem and are adamant that the payer side must be built and certified first to create a stable target for EHRs to connect to. They frame it as a logical prerequisite for success.

They propose to mandate payer-side certification of specific Da Vinci IGs as a first step; certify the entire workflow, not just one side; and ensure payer systems are held to the same 'discipline and rigor' as their own.
Allscripts warns that addressing only the EHR side "leaves a massive risk that functionality will not be fully utilized."
EHRA explicitly recommends that in Stage 1, certification should focus on the payer side.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring standards are implementable within their complex workflows; maintaining flexibility to innovate; the cost and burden of new mandates; data security.
specificPoints: They present themselves as willing partners who are already investing in FHIR and interoperability. Their comments are couched in collaborative language but push back against rigid mandates, advocating for 'ramp-up periods,' 'flexibility,' and careful consideration of payer workflows before rules are set.

They encourage voluntary adoption driven by the value of certified modules; ensure any certification process is aligned with HIPAA and tested in real-world conditions; and a unique proposal from Kaiser Permanente suggests allowing compliance costs to be counted toward MLR Quality Improvement Activity.
UHG states they "intend to use these certified Health IT Modules" but also that payers should "retain the flexibility to adopt additional standards."
BCBSA notes they "have experienced issues integrating with certified health IT because certification validation is not performed under real-world conditions."

noteworthyInsights.insight: Kaiser Permanente proposed a unique and pragmatic financial lever: allowing payers to count ePA implementation costs as a Quality Improvement Activity (QIA) for Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) calculations. This would reframe a compliance cost as a value-add investment for payers.

The individual comment from Christopher Gracon offers a sophisticated critique, arguing that certifying a payer's ePA software module might be ineffective if it cannot seamlessly integrate with the payer's core claims and administrative platform, which is a non-standardized process. This highlights a potential failure point that a simple certification mandate might miss.

A physician, Dr. Walter Sklyar, illustrates the source of provider distrust with a vivid anecdote of a payment being delayed for two years because the insurer mailed a check without a suite number, calling it "not an uncommon scenario" and a symptom of systems with "little regulation, accountability, and oversight."

The assertion by Brendan Keeler that "25% of payers have ignored" previous, simpler FHIR mandates provides a concrete data point that validates the widespread provider and vendor fear that payers will not comply without significant enforcement.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A clear pattern emerged from Health IT vendors (EHRA, Greenway, MEDITECH) advocating for a staged rollout that certifies the payer side first. This reflects their business reality: they cannot justify building EHR functionality to connect to a non-existent or non-standardized target.

Multiple commenters, led by Allscripts, coalesced around the idea that the "entire workflow needs to be certified for all stakeholders." This concept of certifying the end-to-end process, rather than isolated components, was echoed by Ascension, EHRA, and others, framing interoperability as a shared responsibility.

While IT vendors focused on certifying the technical APIs, provider groups consistently expanded the definition of 'accountability' to include operational business practices. There is a pattern of providers arguing that technical success is meaningless without enforceable rules on response times, denial transparency, and clinical criteria.

The American College of Surgeons highlighted a potential unintended consequence: without oversight, payers could use automation to easily expand the number of services requiring PA or increase data requirements, thus passing even more cost and burden to physicians.

keyQuotations.quote: [It] is imperative that all health plans accept electronic standards for receiving clinical information.

If EHRs are held to the standard of having certified technology available to process PA application programming interfaces (APIs), there is no equivalent certification or penalty for payers to not follow the standard.”
The potential benefits of such standardization will be of limited value if payers are not accountable to timeliness and related standards of performance.
Without payer alignment at the front end, a new certification criterion for health IT products on the back-end won’t solve the problem of streamlining the prior authorization process.
If a payer doesn’t implement ePA for receiving and sending, then it becomes a problem for providers and patients.

It is entirely unreasonable to expect us to carry the weight of this only on our side when success cannot happen without commitment from EHR software developers and the payers / health plans on the other end.
As a healthcare provider, it is unbelievable to watch these plans enjoy the fruits of the wild wild west with little regulation, accountability, and oversight.
Given that CMS does not mandate a certification program for payer FHIR-based technology leaves a gap in the confidence of compliance and jeopardizes interoperability.

Although the payer is technically regulated by CMS and not ONC, both sides need to be compatible to achieve integrated care delivery. There is excellent value in seeking certification to ensure interoperability. There must be consistency and reciprocity across the EHR and payer sides...

Each party involved in the healthcare coverage authorization workflow, including the payer, must be held to the same applicable certification criteria to reduce burden for the patient, provider, and payer.

Certification criteria will be wasteful and lack utility if payers are not accountable for engaging with providers and patients in the development, maintenance, and use of electronic prior authorization technology.

Development of consistent standards for data and/or supporting documentation required from providers should be mandatory for payers to participate in future automated Health IT systems for prior authorization.
Software used by health plans should be required to demonstrate compatibility using the same testing tools as certified health IT.

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming consensus that implementing new ePA requirements demands a carefully planned, phased approach with sufficient lead time, rather than a single, aggressive deadline. The central debate is not if more time is needed, but how much, with recommendations ranging from 12 to 48 months. The key tension lies between the urgent need to alleviate provider burden and the pragmatic concerns of health IT vendors, payers, and large health systems about the immaturity of technical standards and the operational complexity of system-wide deployment, with most advocating for a cautious, sequential implementation that follows the finalization of related CMS payer rules.

consensusPoints.text: Sufficient Lead Time is Non-Negotiable - Nearly all commenters, regardless of stakeholder type, insisted that any new requirements must come with adequate time for development, testing, implementation, and training. This was the most universal point of agreement, framed as essential for a successful, non-disruptive rollout.

A Phased or Staggered Approach is Strongly Preferred - A strong majority of stakeholders, particularly health IT vendors, payers, and large provider systems, advocated for a phased or staggered implementation. They argued this would reduce risk, allow for learning and iteration, and manage the complexity of coordinating multiple actors.

Testing Must Precede Timelines - Many commenters, especially those with technical expertise, asserted that no feasible timeline can be set until the underlying standards (Implementation Guides or IGs) have undergone robust, real-world testing. They cautioned against mandating technology that is not yet proven to be stable and effective.

positions.label: Cautious & Deliberate (24-48 Months)
positions.stance: Require a 2 to 4-year timeline for full implementation after final rule publication.
positions.supportLevel: The most common position, held by a majority of Health IT vendors, payers, and provider trade associations.
keyArguments: Technical standards are immature ('in flux,' 'STU2') and require significant development, testing, and potential rework.
Complex software development cycles for EHR vendors take a minimum of 18-24 months from final specs to deployment.
Providers need an additional 12-18 months after vendor software is available for their own implementation, training, and workflow redesign.

positions.label: Urgent & Accelerated (6-12 Months)
positions.stance: Implement new standards within a year of the final rule.
positions.supportLevel: A minority view, held by some technology developers specializing in interoperability and some provider systems focused on rapid burden reduction.
keyArguments: The provider burden is an active crisis that cannot wait years to be addressed.
Existing Cures Act certified technologies and deployed FHIR servers can be re-used to accelerate adoption.
The Da Vinci IGs are mature enough for production and will be ready well before regulatory deadlines.

positions.label: CMS Rule First
positions.stance: Finalize the CMS rule mandating payer ePA capabilities before finalizing the ONC rule for Health IT certification.
positions.supportLevel: A significant bloc, including major trade associations for providers and standards bodies.
keyArguments: Health IT developers need certainty about what payers will be required to do before they invest millions in building certified software.
This sequence prevents developers from building to a standard that payers are not yet required to support, avoiding wasted effort and cost.

positions.label: Certify Ahead
positions.stance: Set the ONC certification effective date in advance of the CMS payer compliance date.
positions.supportLevel: A nuanced position held by at least one major physician advocacy coalition.
keyArguments: This ensures that provider-facing technology (certified EHRs) is ready and available the moment payers are required to go live.
It prevents a scenario where payers are ready but providers lack the necessary tools, thus delaying the benefits of the rule.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Vendors (e.g., Epic, Cerner, Allscripts, Greenway)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Predictability of requirements, stability of underlying technical standards, and sufficient time for the full software development lifecycle.
specificPoints: They provide detailed breakdowns of their internal timelines, universally citing a need for 18-36 months from the publication of a final, normative standard to having a certified product ready for deployment.
Establish a regular regulatory cadence, publish final standards well in advance of certification requirements, and build in separate time for provider adoption after vendor deployment.
Epic provides a clear model: "a minimum of 18 months" for developers, followed by "a further 12-18 months" for providers. Allscripts makes the strongest request for "a full three years (36 months)".

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Payers & Payer Associations (e.g., AHIP, BCBSA, UnitedHealth, Cigna)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: System-wide readiness, managing technical diversity among providers, and the cost of implementing immature or changing standards.
specificPoints: They emphasize a "glide path" and phased implementation (e.g., starting with one use case like Coverage Requirements Discovery) to manage complexity across their vast networks and legacy systems.

A clear, phased roadmap from the government, with ample time between stages. Anthem suggests a specific timeline: "diagnostic imaging after DME/labs in 18 months, then another service type after another 18 months".
Cigna states the proposed IGs "would take several years to implement" and require a "gradual move" away from legacy transactions.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Providers & Provider Associations (e.g., AMA, MGMA, Ascension, APTA)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Minimizing disruption from yet another IT mandate, ensuring sufficient time for workflow integration and staff training, and achieving tangible burden reduction. A distinct and urgent sub-theme is the need for mandated decision turnaround times.
specificPoints: They are focused on the end-user experience and the practical realities of clinic operations. They worry about the "human" side of implementation, including burnout and the cost of training.
Longer implementation timelines (3-5 years suggested by APTA).
Mandated payer decision turnaround times (e.g., 24 hours for urgent, 3-7 days for standard) as part of the rule (PAI; SCMA).
UnityPoint Health highlights the dependency chain: "provider implementation requires an underlying vendor (software developer) build, testing, and provider training."

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Standards Bodies & Technical Consultants (e.g., WEDI, CAQH CORE, Point-of-Care Partners)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The integrity of the process, maturity of the standards, and logical sequencing of regulatory requirements.
specificPoints: They act as system architects, focusing on the necessary pre-conditions for success. Their recommendations are grounded in deep experience with past standards rollouts.
