A "glidepath" where the CMS payer rule is finalized first, followed by the ONC certification rule, with staggered compliance dates for different stakeholders.
CAQH CORE leverages its certification experience to state that the process "is often 18 months or more from initial build to certification."

noteworthyInsights.insight: Sophisticated Regulatory Sequencing: The Regulatory Relief Coalition offered a particularly nuanced take: ONC should wait for the CMS final rule before finalizing its own, but the ONC certification effective date should be at least six months before the CMS payer compliance date. This ensures provider tools are ready the day payer mandates switch on, preventing a gap in capability.

Compelling Personal Analogy: Dr. Shteynshlyuger of New York Urology Specialists powerfully reframes the urgency of decision timelines: "pilots flying in over the weekend are not asked to wait until Monday to land; similarly, patients and healthcare practitioners should not wait until Monday to start treatment." This grounds the technical debate in the reality of patient care.

Data-backed Implementation Scale: The Federation of American Hospitals provides a concrete, sobering estimate that for large health systems, "piloting and enterprise adoption of a fully electronic PA process could take 35,000+ hours," quantifying the immense scale of the operational lift required beyond just software installation.

Architecture-Dependent Timelines: Gainwell Technologies points out that the implementation timeline is not monolithic; it depends on the chosen architecture. A direct EHR-to-payer API might take 9 months, whereas an approach using intermediary clearinghouses could take 12+ months, highlighting that 'how' the rule is implemented directly impacts 'how long' it will take.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Two Distinct "Timeline" Debates: The comments reveal two parallel conversations about time. 1) Implementation Timeline: The time needed for vendors, payers, and providers to build and deploy the technology (the focus of most vendors/payers). 2. Decision Timeline: The time payers should be allowed to make a prior authorization decision (the focus of most providers). Policymakers should recognize these are separate but related issues.

Vendor-Provider Timeline Dependency: A clear pattern emerged where vendors define their timeline (e.g., 18-24 months) and then explicitly state that the provider timeline (e.g., another 12-18 months) must begin after the vendor timeline is complete. This highlights a critical path dependency that adds up to a multi-year total implementation window. (e.g., Epic).

Experience Breeds Caution: Commenters with direct, hands-on experience in large-scale standards implementation (e.g., CAQH CORE, Epic, major payers) were almost uniformly the most cautious, recommending longer, phased timelines. Newer, more specialized tech companies (e.g., Mettle, Cohere) were more likely to advocate for accelerated timelines, suggesting a correlation between a stakeholder's scope of responsibility and their risk tolerance.

keyQuotations.quote: [P]ilots flying in over the weekend are not asked to wait until Monday to land; similarly, patients and healthcare practitioners should not wait until Monday to start treatment.

Providers desperately need this functionality but will be deprived of these important burden-reducing features if ONC delays. ONC should proceed with all due haste in order to minimize provider burden ASAP.
Allscripts is skeptical given policymaker urgency... Adopting criteria before standards are firmly established increases unnecessary burden for compliance.

Based on our experience with the Patient Access guides, we would expect something in the area of 18-24 months would be needed by most implementers to design to these specifications, test thoroughly, and potentially re-implementing to a more mature version of the specifications.
The optimum glidepath would have the CMS prior authorization final rule released before the rule establishing ONC ePA certification requirements, ensuring health IT developers confidence in criteria.
[I]t will take some time before e-PA is technologically viable in full.

It is critical to consider the maturity of the specific IGs to accommodate the expanded scope, and these factors must be accounted for in establishing realistic effective dates and before certification is considered.
[P]rovider implementation requires an underlying vendor (software developer) build, testing, and provider training.
Greenway generally supports at least 18 – 24 months of development, implementation, and testing by developers until it is ready for deployment in the field for any new standard requirement.

We urge ONC to wait until the CMS Final Rule is published before finalizing e-PA Certification Program requirements... [but] the effective date of the ePA Certification Program requirement should be at least six months in advance of the effective date of CMS’ e-PA regulations for payers.

sections.executiveSummary: An overwhelming consensus exists among all stakeholders that close, continuous coordination between ONC, CMS, and other HHS agencies is essential for successful prior authorization (PA) reform. Stakeholders universally fear that disjointed or conflicting rules will stall adoption, increase administrative burden, and waste investments. Significant debate surrounds the how and when of this coordination, particularly regarding the harmonization of legacy HIPAA (X12) standards with modern FHIR APIs and the proper sequencing of payer-facing (CMS) and technology-facing (ONC) regulations. The dominant recommendation is for HHS to develop a single, public regulatory roadmap that synchronizes requirements and timelines for all parties to prevent confusion and ensure technology automates a reformed, not a broken, process.

consensusPoints.text: Virtually all commenters explicitly state that ONC must work closely with CMS and other federal partners. This is the most consistent point of agreement in the entire theme, spanning every stakeholder type from providers and payers to vendors and advocacy groups. The core rationale is that since PA involves both clinical workflows (ONC's domain) and payer processes (CMS's domain), any effective solution must be jointly developed and implemented. For example, the American College of Cardiology asks ONC to "work closely with CMS and other regulatory agencies to ensure policies governing PA and payer requirements align with technical standards," payer trade group AHIP recommends ONC work with CMS to "outline a roadmap for ePA that leverages the Certification program... to outline consistent requirements and fair compliance dates for all stakeholders," and the Regulatory Relief Coalition states it is "critical for CMS and ONC to continue to work hand in hand in addressing concerns related to PA."

A strong majority of commenters agree that HHS must avoid creating separate, conflicting, or parallel standards pathways for administrative (HIPAA) and clinical (ONC Certified Health IT) transactions. The fear is that having different rules for different market segments or transaction types will create confusion, increase development costs, and ultimately hinder the adoption of electronic prior authorization (ePA). For example, CAQH CORE strongly warns that "having different requirements for prior authorization and attachments under ONC authorities and HIPAA is likely to confuse the industry and thus stall adoption." Premier Inc. stresses it is "imperative to avoid setting different requirements, standards, and/or processes via different rules," warning of additional costs and burdens for providers. The American College of Physicians argues that harmonization is crucial to "save physicians and staff from duplicative work in the long-term."

positions.label: Dual-Track / Harmonize
positions.stance: Allow the use of both legacy HIPAA X12 and modern FHIR standards, but ensure they are harmonized and can work together.
positions.supportLevel: The most common position, supported by many large providers, health IT vendors, and standards groups.
keyArguments: This approach acknowledges the massive installed base of X12 transactions and avoids the disruption of a "rip and replace" mandate. It allows the industry to transition to FHIR over time while maintaining current functionality.

positions.label: Replace with FHIR
positions.stance: HHS should formally replace the X12 standard with FHIR for prior authorization transactions to create a single, modern pathway.
positions.supportLevel: A minority but influential view, primarily held by major payers and forward-looking technology developers.
keyArguments: Maintaining two standards indefinitely creates unnecessary complexity and cost. A decisive move to FHIR would accelerate innovation, simplify development, and unlock the full potential of API-based interoperability.

positions.label: CMS First
positions.stance: CMS must finalize regulations for payers to streamline and standardize PA processes before ONC mandates technology standards for EHRs.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily supported by healthcare providers and some provider-focused advocacy groups.
keyArguments: It is illogical and inefficient for technology vendors to build solutions without knowing the specific processes and requirements payers will be held to. Defining the "what" (payer rules) must precede defining the "how" (technology certification).

positions.label: Simultaneous Alignment
positions.stance: ONC and CMS must develop and issue their respective rules in a tightly coordinated fashion with aligned effective dates.
positions.supportLevel: A common view among payers, trade associations, and some vendors who want to ensure reciprocity.
keyArguments: If one side (e.g., payers) is regulated without a reciprocal requirement on the other (e.g., providers/vendors), adoption will be one-sided and ineffective. Synchronized timelines are necessary for all parties to invest and implement in parallel.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden, preventing care delays, and ensuring technology solves process problems rather than just digitizing them.
specificPoints: Strong emphasis that inter-agency coordination must go beyond technical standards to address comprehensive PA reform. They fear that a successful ePA system could simply make it easier for payers to issue more "low-value" authorizations, increasing net burden.

Sequence rulemaking so CMS defines PA process rules first, then ONC creates technology standards. Urge ONC/CMS to establish "guardrails" on payer use of data and influence on medical decisions (as mentioned by AAFP).
UCSF CDHI warns against "merely sending more low-value prior authorizations electronically."
BJC HealthCare insists on CMS setting PA practice standards before ONC acts on technology.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring consistent and reciprocal requirements for all stakeholders (providers, vendors), achieving regulatory certainty with fair compliance dates, and managing implementation costs.
specificPoints: They push for ONC and CMS to create a unified roadmap that holds providers and their vendors to the same standards and timelines that payers must meet. They also seek financial mechanisms to offset compliance costs.
Allow compliance costs to be included as Quality Improvement Activity (QIA) expenses in Medical Loss Ratio (MLR) calculations (as proposed by Kaiser Permanente and Anthem).
Create provider incentives (e.g., MIPS) to use ePA technology (as proposed by Cigna).
BCBSA demands that "comparable standards are adopted by providers and vendors at the same time they are required for impacted payers."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Need for a clear, stable, single set of technical standards to build to. Avoiding the cost and complexity of supporting multiple, conflicting standards.
specificPoints: Many recommend that ONC/CMS focus initial adoption and certification requirements on the payer side, arguing that payers are the source of the PA rules and must have capable APIs before providers can connect to them.
Develop robust, collaborative testing tools to ensure API consistency (as proposed by Epic).
Focus certification on payer interactions first (as proposed by Cerner and EHRA).
The Electronic Health Record Association (EHRA) states that the "ONC certification approach with CMS policies is critical" and that both should focus adoption on the "payer side."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The technical and procedural challenges of harmonizing standards, ensuring a clear process for standards advancement, and preventing industry confusion.
specificPoints: They provide the most detailed technical analysis of the conflict between HIPAA (X12) and FHIR standards, offering potential solutions like "standard agnostic" approaches or clear roadmaps for convergence.
HHS should establish a "single, consistent process for standards advancement" (as proposed by AHIMA).
Agencies should revisit and harmonize the HIPAA/non-HIPAA standards adoption processes (as proposed by Point-of-Care Partners).
CAQH CORE provides a stark warning about the risk of industry confusion from dueling standards.
WEDI urges the development of a "comprehensive regulatory roadmap."

noteworthyInsights.insight: The 'CMS First' sequencing argument: BJC HealthCare provides a highly logical and compelling argument that technology standards (ONC's role) cannot be effectively developed 'without a defined set of PA practices and minimum standards around which to build' (CMS's role). This highlights a critical dependency in the rulemaking process.

The need to revisit the National Health Plan ID (HPID): New York Urology Specialists makes an unexpected connection, arguing that the failure to implement a standardized HPID is a root cause of automation challenges and that a government-managed 'HUB' is the only viable solution, requiring coordinated federal action.

Federal rules may weaken stronger state laws: Allina Health raises a unique and critical point about unintended consequences, noting that new federal rules could preempt and weaken existing state-level patient protections if not carefully drafted, a concern not raised by others.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder-driven priorities: A clear pattern emerges where each stakeholder group's primary recommendation reflects its position in the ecosystem. Providers want process reform first ('CMS First'). Payers want reciprocal obligations ('Simultaneous Alignment'). Vendors want clear technical specifications ('Payer-Side First').

Convergence on a 'Regulatory Roadmap': While they may disagree on the details, a wide range of stakeholders from different camps (WEDI, AHIP, AHIMA, Cigna, Kaiser Permanente) have converged on the idea of a single, public, long-term HHS roadmap as the solution to uncertainty and lack of coordination.

Fear of Unintended Consequences: Multiple commenters independently identified two major potential negative outcomes: 1) creating a more efficient system for a flawed process (as noted by UCSF and AUA), and 2) creating dueling, incompatible standards that increase burden (as noted by CAQH CORE and Premier).

keyQuotations.quote: [H]aving different requirements for prior authorization and attachments under ONC authorities and HIPAA is likely to confuse the industry and thus stall adoption.
ONC and CMS should work together to address other contributors to the overall burden of prior authorization, to avoid merely sending more low-value prior authorizations electronically.

Although the payer is technically regulated by CMS and not ONC, both sides need to be compatible to achieve integrated care delivery. There is excellent value in seeking certification to ensure interoperability. There must be consistency and reciprocity across the EHR and payer sides...

Premier urges ONC to work more closely with its federal partners, especially CMS, and harmonize efforts... it is imperative to avoid setting different requirements, standards, and/or processes via different rules.

BJC recommends that CMS first establish 'industry-wide regulations that streamline, standardize, and make transparent when, where, and how PA is deployed by plans'. Once CMS regulations are in place, ONC should then 'consider following the technological standards...'

As ONC collaborates with agency partners to develop rules on electronic prior authorizations, we urge the consideration of existing states’ laws if they are more stringent than the regulations that may be developed by HHS.
CMS and ONC should ensure that comparable standards are adopted by providers and vendors at the same time they are required for impacted payers.
A great first step towards reducing prior authorization burden would be for ONC, CMS, private payers, and EHR vendors to accept the same clinical definitions for data elements and report formats.
WEDI urges ONC to work with federal partners to coordinate mandates and compliance dates and develop a comprehensive regulatory roadmap.

6.9.themeDescription: Debate on Modular vs. End-to-End Certification Scope

sections.executiveSummary: Public comment reveals a fundamental architectural disagreement on how to structure ePA certification. A strong majority, led by Health IT developers and technology companies, overwhelmingly favors a modular, granular certification to accommodate diverse multi-vendor systems, foster innovation from "best-of-breed" solutions, and ensure a feasible, staged implementation. Conversely, a significant and vocal contingent of physician organizations and patient advocates strongly recommends a single, end-to-end certification module to guarantee a seamless, integrated clinician workflow and prevent the creation of a fragmented system that could worsen administrative burden. The primary tension is between the developers' need for technical flexibility and the clinicians' non-negotiable requirement for a simple, unified user experience.

consensusPoints.text: Da Vinci IGs are the correct foundation, but require refinement for certification. A strong majority of commenters from all stakeholder groups (vendors, providers, payers, standards bodies) agree that the HL7 Da Vinci Implementation Guides (CRD, DTR, PAS) are the right starting point. However, most also state that the IGs as currently written are too coarse for direct use in certification and must be broken down into more granular, testable "building blocks" or interaction sets.

Flexibility for diverse Health IT ecosystems is essential. Nearly all commenters, including many who favor end-to-end certification, acknowledge the market reality that provider organizations often use different systems for clinical, administrative, and revenue cycle functions. Any certification scheme must account for this multi-vendor environment and the use of third-party applications.

A single standard is preferable to multiple competing standards. Commenters across the board agree on the need for a single, unified standard for ePA to avoid a "worst-case scenario where a provider must implement a different API for each individual payer". The debate is not about whether to standardize, but about the granularity of the certification for that standard.

areasOfDebate.description: The central debate is over how ONC should structure its certification criteria—as a single, monolithic workflow or as smaller, modular components. This represents a critical architectural decision for the certification program, impacting both vendors and providers.

positions.label: Modular Approach
positions.stance: Certify discrete functional components or "building blocks" of the ePA workflow separately.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority
keyArguments: Reflects Market Reality: Accommodates the common provider practice of using different vendors for clinical, administrative, and financial systems.
Fosters Innovation: Enables "best-of-breed" third-party apps to innovate and certify specific components without having to build a full end-to-end system.

Ensures Feasibility: Prevents making certification infeasible for developers whose products do not span the entire workflow (e.g., a clinical EHR vendor shouldn't be forced to build revenue cycle modules). A monolithic requirement would be technically and financially infeasible for many.

positions.label: End-to-End Approach
positions.stance: Certify the entire "soup-to-nuts" ePA process as a single, holistic module.
positions.supportLevel: A significant minority
keyArguments: Guarantees Seamless Workflow: Ensures providers get a fully integrated, automated process within their primary system, which is critical for adoption and burden reduction.
Prevents Fragmentation: Avoids a "piecemeal" approach where providers are forced to stitch together multiple certified modules, adding cost, complexity, and new points of failure.
Reduces Provider Burden: A single certified solution is seen as the only way to truly deliver on the promise of automation without creating new, confusing workflows.
Certification should include usability metrics to ensure ePA capabilities are deployed within established clinical workflows, not as separate, layered-on applications or portals.

positions.label: Hybrid Bundled Certification
positions.stance: Define and certify distinct clusters of functionality (e.g., 1. Discovery, 2. Data Gathering, 3. Submission/Tracking) as separate modules.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed by consultants and others as a middle ground
keyArguments: Offers a middle ground between the all-or-nothing modular vs. end-to-end approaches, providing flexibility while maintaining logical groupings of functionality.

positions.label: Dual Certification Paths
positions.stance: Create a certification for a "complete" ePA product as well as a separate certification path for specific ePA modules.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed by WEDI
keyArguments: Supports both large, integrated vendors capable of building a complete solution and smaller, specialized developers focusing on specific components.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Developers & Trade Associations (e.g., EHRA, Cerner, Epic, athenahealth)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Certification feasibility, high development burden/cost, being forced to develop products outside their core business, and stifling innovation.
specificPoints: They emphasize that the market reality of multi-vendor IT environments makes a monolithic certification requirement technically and commercially unworkable. They warn that a coarse, end-to-end approach is "premature".

Overwhelmingly advocate for modular, granular certification criteria based on functional "building blocks" rather than entire workflows or IGs, allowing them discretion to certify to the components they build.
Epic notes that ePA features span modules beyond the typical scope of certified EHRs (e.g., registration, revenue cycle), making a single criterion infeasible for many developers.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Physician & Provider Organizations (e.g., AMA, ACP, ACOG, ACC)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Increasing clinician burden, creating fragmented and confusing workflows, patient care delays, and the financial cost of integrating multiple disparate solutions.
specificPoints: Their focus is entirely on the end-user experience. Technology that is not seamlessly integrated into the clinical workflow is a failure, regardless of its technical elegance. They argue external apps "do little to reduce provider burdens".
Strongly recommend a single, "soup-to-nuts" certified module to guarantee a complete, automated ePA process without relying on ancillary components.
The AMA states it is "unacceptable to assume physicians will 'mix and match' modules for ePA functionality".

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Payers & Insurers (e.g., Anthem, UnitedHealth Group, Humana, Cigna, Kaiser)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Achieving true end-to-end automation while allowing for practical, incremental implementation across their diverse provider networks.
specificPoints: This group is divided. Some, like Anthem, emphasize that the workflow "needs to be considered as a single process" for certification to be meaningful.
Others, like UHG and Humana, advocate for modular certification of individual capabilities (CRD, DTR, PAS) to provide flexibility and support incremental deployment.

A mix of modular and end-to-end recommendations, reflecting their dual role as technology implementers and process owners. Kaiser Permanente, as an integrated system, uniquely favors a single system for implementation simplicity.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Technology Innovators & Consultants (e.g., Olive AI, Point-of-Care Partners, AEGIS.net)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring that certification does not stifle innovation from "best-of-breed" or specialized third-party solutions that are not part of a monolithic EHR.
specificPoints: They argue that much of the innovation in ePA automation comes from outside traditional EHRs. A modular approach is seen as essential to allow these specialized tools to participate in the certified ecosystem.
Advocate for modular certification that allows "best-of-breed options for deeper, purpose-built capabilities".
Point-of-Care Partners proposes a hybrid model of certifying three distinct functional "bundles" separately.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Integrated System View: Kaiser Permanente, as a large integrated delivery network that is both provider and payer, provides a powerful real-world perspective. They state, "it is simpler to implement one system for ePA versus multiple systems with different certified functionalities," offering strong practical support for the end-to-end argument from an implementation standpoint.

Certification Costs as an Innovation Barrier: Suncoast RHIO highlights that the high fees required for comprehensive certification (similar to 2015 Edition CEHRT) can be "prohibitive for all but the very large vendors, thereby stifling innovation." This provides a compelling financial argument for a more accessible, modular certification path.

Workflow vs. Functional Capability: MCG Health makes the critical distinction that ONC should "certify functional capabilities... as opposed to... a specified workflow." This would allow flexibility in how a capability is implemented (e.g., real-time in the workflow vs. a back-office process), supporting innovation within a modular framework.

Span Beyond Certified EHRs: Epic points out that the full ePA workflow spans systems often outside the scope of CEHRT, such as registration and revenue cycle systems. Mandating a single certification criterion could force EHR developers to "expand product/business lines subject to certification overhead" or exit the program, a significant unintended consequence.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Developer vs. Clinician Divide: The most prominent pattern is the stark philosophical split between technology developers (who prioritize technical flexibility and feasibility via a modular approach) and clinical end-users (who prioritize workflow simplicity and guaranteed usability via an end-to-end approach).

A Hybrid Middle Ground: A notable pattern emerged from consultants and some payers proposing a "best of both worlds" solution: certify discrete functional bundles (e.g., CRD, DTR, PAS) separately to provide flexibility, but with the ultimate goal that providers would implement all modules to achieve the end-to-end workflow. This is seen as a pragmatic path forward.

Experience Correlation: Commenters with direct responsibility for software development and multi-system integration (vendors, large health systems) overwhelmingly support modularity. Commenters representing frontline clinicians who bear the burden of inefficient workflows (physician groups) are the staunchest supporters of a single, unified module.

keyQuotations.quote: [The AMA] strongly recommends incorporating all PA functional capabilities into a single module. Physicians need assurance that EHRs support a seamless, “soup-to-nuts” automated PA process without relying on ancillary components.

Requiring certification to a full prior authorization workflow for provider-focused health IT is premature, considering both the variety of practical and valid configurations of health IT and the absence of clearly defined, more granular interaction sets within the respective guides.

Any required capabilities for Certified Health IT must be thoughtfully deployed into established clinical workflows, not layered on top of previously deployed certified technology, which can hamper workflow and damage existing systems.

Allowing the payer to choose from multiple different APIs or implementation options only creates a worst-case scenario where a provider must implement a different API for each individual payer they contract with.

ONC should ensure medical ePA functionality is available both native and adjunct to the core HIS/EHR system. Innovation should not be limited to existing EHR vendor systems but should consider the value of best-of-breed options.

A segmented or disjointed ePA system that incorrectly pulls data and results in the need for re-entry or correction of pulled data will create additional administration burden and limit the amount of time physicians can apply to providing care to patients.

We strongly encourage a whole solution rather than a piecemeal approach. This is too complex to move forward one piece at a time. Development would be completed and then require rework or be unusable, which is unreasonable for software developers.
As an integrated health care organization... we have found that it is simpler to implement one system for ePA versus multiple systems with different certified functionalities.

For frontline physicians who are already facing burnout and staff shortages, investing now in a single harmonious standard would be the best option for preventing the long-term challenges and inefficiencies of having to translate between and satisfy multiple sets of standards.
ONC should permit providers and clinicians to automate prior authorization processes using third-party applications of their choosing that can be integrated within clinical and EHR workflows.
Defining a single certification criterion for a full, HIPAA-compliant prior authorization workflow may be infeasible. ePA features span modules beyond our current certification scope.

7.1.themeDescription: Mandating Standardized Payer Timelines for Response

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus among healthcare providers, professional associations, and health IT vendors that current prior authorization response times are unacceptably long, variable, and harmful to patients. A strong majority advocates for federally mandated, standardized timelines, most commonly citing 24 hours for urgent and 72 hours for standard requests, with 24/7/365 payer operations. The primary tension is between this provider-driven demand for strict, immediate federal rules and the payer perspective, which cautions against superseding existing state laws and calls for more flexible, incremental standards that account for clinical complexity.

consensusPoints.text: Current Payer Response Timelines are Unacceptable and Must Be Standardized

exceptions.text: While payers like Cigna and Priority Health do not oppose the idea of timeliness, they raise significant caveats about how standards should be set, arguing against rigid federal mandates and for a more flexible, industry-agreed approach.

consensusPoints.text: Payers Must Process Prior Authorization Requests 24/7/365
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of provider organizations and their associations

consensusPoints.text: Shorter Timelines Are Feasible with Technology
consensusPoints.supportLevel: broad agreement, particularly among health IT vendors, interoperability groups, and tech-forward providers

areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to impose strict, uniform national deadlines for payer responses or to allow for more flexible standards that respect existing state laws and clinical complexity.

positions.label: Strict Federal Deadlines
positions.stance: Mandate specific, short national timelines (e.g., 24h urgent/72h standard) for all payers.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of healthcare providers, professional societies, and patient advocacy groups.
keyArguments: Ensures patient safety by preventing dangerous care delays
creates a predictable, level playing field for providers
reduces administrative burden by eliminating follow-ups

positions.label: Flexible State/Payer Standards
positions.stance: Federal rules should not supersede existing state-level regulations or impose a one-size-fits-all timeframe.
positions.supportLevel: Payers/Insurers.
keyArguments: Many states already have established timelines (e.g., 14 days) that should not be preempted
a single national standard doesn't account for clinical complexity requiring manual review
overly aggressive service levels are not technically feasible for all parties in the transaction

areasOfDebate.description: The debate focuses on whether to aim for immediate real-time decision-making or to adopt a more gradual, incremental rollout of faster response times.

positions.label: Real-Time Aspiration
positions.stance: The goal of any new regulation should be to enable real-time or near-real-time decisions at the point of care.
positions.supportLevel: Many providers, some tech vendors, and medical groups.
keyArguments: Technology already allows for this
decisions made after a patient leaves the office create rework and care gaps
a patient-centric system demands immediate answers

positions.label: Incremental Rollout
positions.stance: A phased approach is needed, starting with simpler real-time transactions and allowing more time for complex requests.
positions.supportLevel: Standards development organizations, some payers and tech vendors.
keyArguments: Not all requests can be automated immediately
ultra-fast service levels require mature technology and industry-wide agreement
it is more practical to pilot faster times for specific services first

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Direct patient harm from delayed care; operational chaos from unpredictable wait times; severe administrative burden and cost (e.g., UnityPoint Health's 60+ FTEs for PA); hospital discharge backlogs caused by weekend delays.
specificPoints: They provide the most visceral, ground-level examples of system failure, such as approvals arriving after a patient has been discharged or patients waiting in the hospital over a weekend for a Monday morning decision.
Mandate aggressive timelines (24h/72h), require 24/7 payer operations, and push for real-time processing at the point of care.
Memorial Sloan Kettering reports response times ranging from one to 14 days, with peer-to-peer reviews adding another ten business days.
Labcorp reports an average turnaround time of nine days.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Aggregating member concerns about administrative burden, physician burnout, and negative patient outcomes into a unified policy message. They often cite member survey data to substantiate the scale of the problem.
specificPoints: They translate on-the-ground problems into specific regulatory recommendations, often suggesting precise language for timelines (e.g., 3 calendar days) and proposing accountability mechanisms like "deemed approval" if deadlines are missed (APTA).
Strong federal preemption with uniform standards (24h/72h); requirements for payers to provide reasons for delays; accountability for missed deadlines.
AMRPA provides survey data showing average wait times of ~2.5 days for IRF decisions.
AAFP and NCAFP cite the L.A. Care case where a backlog of 9,000 requests led to patient harm and death.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Federal overreach into areas governed by state law; imposition of technically infeasible service levels (e.g., Cigna's concern over a 15-second response time); one-size-fits-all rules that don't account for requests needing complex clinical review.
specificPoints: They are the only group to defend existing state-level regulations (CVS Health citing 14-day standards) and to highlight that provider-submitted information is often incomplete, necessitating delays.

Defer to state-level standards; if federal rules are set, they must be flexible and developed with industry consensus; standardize turnaround times but account for review complexity (Priority Health).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of standardized payer APIs and response protocols prevents their technology from working effectively; manual follow-up via phone/fax undermines the entire point of electronic systems.
specificPoints: They focus on the technical "how," advocating for specific standards (HL7 FHIR Da Vinci PAS IG) and framing faster response times as a key incentive for provider adoption of new technology (WEDI).

Mandate payer adoption of specific FHIR APIs for automated decision responses; require payers to automate their internal processes (Allscripts); pursue an incremental rollout of real-time capabilities.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Discharge Bottleneck: The Connecticut Hospital Association provides a critical insight by explicitly linking payer PA delays—especially the lack of weekend staffing—to hospital discharge backlogs. This illustrates how PA inefficiency contributes directly to hospital overcrowding and increased inpatient costs.

The "Gotcha" Denial: UnityPoint Health points out a perilous unintended consequence: payers demanding a peer-to-peer review within an expedited (e.g., 24-hour) window. If the provider cannot schedule the review in that short timeframe, the request is denied. This turns a seemingly responsive process into a mechanism for denial.

The "Real-Time" vs. "State Law" Standoff: The stark contrast between providers demanding real-time decisions enabled by technology (Novant Health) and payers defending existing 14-day state-level statutes (CVS Health) perfectly encapsulates the core political and philosophical divide on this issue.

Data-Backed Evidence of Delays and Solutions: Several commenters provided hard numbers. AMRPA's survey finding a ~2.5 day average wait for IRF decisions and Labcorp's 9-day average quantify the problem, while the AHIP/RTI study cited by ACP and others showing a 69% time reduction with ePA quantifies the potential solution.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The "Weekend and After-Hours" Problem: A recurring pattern identified by hospital systems (CGH, Conn. Hospital Assoc.) and vendors (Allscripts) is that the 9-to-5, weekday-only nature of many payer PA departments is a primary driver of delays, especially for inpatient and emergency services.

Specialty Care Urgency: Comments from cancer centers (Moffitt, MSK, Dana-Farber, ADCC), mental health providers (APA, Clarity Child Guidance Center), and surgeons (AAHKS) highlight that for time-sensitive conditions, even a "standard" delay of several days can have severe clinical consequences.

Unintended Consequences of Timelines: Beyond UnityPoint's "gotcha" denial, Clarity Child Guidance Center notes that a payer's 24-hour adjudication clock can start upon an ADT feed, before providers have had time to complete the full clinical documentation, creating an impossible-to-meet deadline. This suggests timeline rules must be carefully designed around clinical workflows.

keyQuotations.quote: "[P]ilots flying in over the weekend are not asked to wait until Monday to land; similarly, patients and healthcare practitioners should not wait until Monday to start treatment."
"Longer periods of time between patient engagement and treatment intervention, result in greater likelihood of delayed, missed, or inappropriate care for patients."
"The goal must be real-time or near-real-time processing of requests to ensure decisions can be made at the point of care before patients walk out the door."
"Any ePA Certification Process Should Not Supersede State Standards for Response Times to ePA Requests. Most states have clearly established 14 days as an acceptable timeline..."
"Patient care occurs 24/7/365, but payers do not operate on the same schedule."

"[T]he fact that many prior-authorization notifications are received days and even weeks after the plan was notified of a patient’s admission, often prior approvals are received after the patient’s discharge."
"Psychiatrists attempting to address emergent issues with a patient require a faster turnaround from payers via ePA to address patients who are in acute crisis."
"While a ten-day turnaround time may work for PMDs [Power Mobility Devices], it will not work for other product categories [requiring immediate delivery]."
"Many payers give status updates that simply read, 'pending,' but with no indication of what further information might be needed for a final decision."
"[W]hen providers are unable to schedule and hold a review within the expedited timeframe, prior authorizations are subsequently denied."
"The average turnaround time for Labcorp regarding a prior authorization approval is nine days from the date of request."
"An automated and standardized electronic prior authorization system would expedite the turnaround time for decisions, giving patients much needed relief from waiting for coverage determinations..."

sections.executiveSummary: Public input reveals an overwhelming consensus among healthcare providers, professional societies, health IT vendors, and patient advocates demanding the standardization of medical criteria used for prior authorization (PA). Commenters describe the current system of varied, opaque, and proprietary payer rules as a primary driver of patient harm, administrative waste, and physician burnout. The central tension is between this near-unanimous call for federally mandated, evidence-based standards and the payer industry's stated need for autonomy to manage diverse health plans. The dominant recommendation is for regulatory action to compel payers to use transparent clinical criteria derived from national medical specialty society guidelines.

consensusPoints.text: Payer criteria are dangerously inconsistent and unpredictable. Nearly all commenters from provider, administrative, and technology sectors decry the lack of uniformity in PA rules. They report that each payer—and often, each plan within a single payer—maintains its own unique set of requirements for the same service, creating what one commenter called "50,000 different health plan formats for the same treatment". This variation forces providers to navigate a chaotic landscape, adding "billions of dollars" to healthcare costs and causing significant care delays. Ascension notes, "Providers today must navigate different standards of 'medically necessary' across different payers–often for the same procedure–and each payer can require the submission of different information to support coverage of a procedure." The Medical Group Management Association states, "It involves different medical necessity requirements across payers. These differing requirements, many of which change suddenly without adequate notice to practices... result in inefficient communication and delays in patient care." Mass General Brigham adds, "Right now each payor makes their own rules."

exceptions.text: The payer trade association AHIP explicitly opposes uniform standards, citing the need to accommodate "varying plan programs, sponsors, and panels".

consensusPoints.text: Payer criteria are opaque and function as a "black box." A strong majority of provider-side commenters state that payers' clinical guidelines are often proprietary and inaccessible. This lack of transparency makes it "nearly impossible for physicians to anticipate what each independent health plan may request," leading to a cycle of denials and appeals that wastes resources and delays care, as noted by the South Carolina Medical Association. Premier Inc. notes the "payer opacity about which evidence-based guidelines are used to determine medical necessity." The Physicians Advocacy Institute says, "Health plans commonly use proprietary medical necessity criteria and clinical guidelines, making it a 'black box' for physicians to anticipate what evidence is needed." UCSF CDHI adds, "PBMs can have opaque or infrequently updated guidelines, leading to non-adherence to accepted standards of care or acknowledgment of FDA-approved therapies." The Federation of American Hospitals points out that payer handbooks are "cumbersome and non-transparent... and simply do not present the information needed to achieve a timely and appropriate authorization."

Payer criteria frequently deviate from established, evidence-based national guidelines. An overwhelming majority of physicians and professional societies report that payer PA rules are often inconsistent with or directly contradict guidelines developed by medical specialty societies. This leads to denials for care that is considered the standard of care by clinical experts. The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons provided striking survey data, reporting that 46% of their members found payers "rarely" used evidence-based medicine or national guidelines, and less than 1% found they "always" did. The American Medical Association reports, "Nearly a third of physicians (30-33%) report health plans rarely or never use evidence-based criteria in prior authorization." The American Urological Association states, "Guidelines from insurers are not always consistent with specialty guidelines, and prior authorization requirements and coverage denials frequently supersede clinician judgement, creating the most significant obstacles to patient care." Moffitt Cancer Center observes, "Third parties that manage the authorization process, especially for radiology services, create their own requirements and do not always follow NCCN or FDA guidelines."

areasOfDebate.topic: Mandatory National Standardization of Clinical Criteria
areasOfDebate.description: The central point of contention is whether clinical criteria should be federally standardized or if payers should retain autonomy.

positions.label: Mandate Standards
positions.stance: The federal government should mandate that all payers use a single, transparent, and nationally standardized set of evidence-based clinical criteria.
positions.supportLevel: An overwhelming majority of commenters, including virtually all healthcare providers, professional associations, health IT vendors, and patient advocates.
keyArguments: Enables Automation: Without standard rules, automating PA is technically and financially infeasible. As NASL states, "Health IT companies... cannot program for the possibly unending combinations of payers and payer requirements".
Reduces Patient Harm: Standardization would ensure care decisions are based on clinical evidence, not arbitrary or cost-driven rules, reducing delays for necessary treatment.
Decreases Administrative Waste: A single set of rules would eliminate the billions of dollars and immense staff time currently spent navigating thousands of different payer requirements.

positions.label: Payer Autonomy
positions.stance: Payers must retain the flexibility to establish their own medical necessity standards and PA requirements for different plans and populations.
positions.supportLevel: A single, but highly significant, commenter: AHIP, the national trade association for health insurance providers. A health system with its own health plan, Priority Health, also supports payer autonomy but couples it with a strong call for transparency.
keyArguments: Plan Variation: Uniform standards are not practical "due to varying plan programs, sponsors, and panels".
Utilization Management: Implicitly, retaining autonomy is necessary to effectively manage costs and ensure appropriate utilization for specific patient populations and benefit designs.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient harm from care delays, immense administrative burden leading to staff burnout and high costs, erosion of clinical judgment by non-medical reviewers.
specificPoints: They provide the most visceral, ground-level examples of system failure, such as denials for FDA-approved drugs because they are not on a payer's list, or having to get PA for care that is ultimately approved 99% of the time, where the delay itself causes harm.
Mandate the use of national specialty society guidelines (e.g., NCCN, ACC), require full transparency of rules, and eliminate PA for services that consistently follow evidence-based pathways.

UCSF CDHI detailed denials for standard-of-care IBD treatments and robotic surgery despite years of positive data. UnityPoint Health noted that the current complexity "subverts medical necessity guidelines and prioritizes process over health and wellness decisions."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The systemic nature of the problem impacting all their members, the undermining of professional standards, and the lack of accountability from payers.
specificPoints: They aggregate individual provider complaints into powerful policy arguments and provide crucial quantitative data from member surveys (e.g., AAHKS, AMA). They position their own organization's guidelines as the logical, evidence-based standard to be adopted.

Require payers to base PA criteria on peer-reviewed, evidence-based guidelines from national medical specialty societies, mandate that denials be reviewed by a physician of the same specialty, and publish all PA logic as an open standard.

The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons cited survey data that less than 1% of members felt payers always used evidence-based criteria. The Physicians Advocacy Institute called for an end to "proprietary" (undisclosed) clinical standards.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The technical and financial infeasibility of developing and maintaining automated PA solutions that can interface with thousands of inconsistent, non-standard, and constantly changing payer rule sets.
specificPoints: They frame the issue as a foundational "business problem" rather than a technological one. Without standardized rules to encode, technology cannot solve the problem; it may even worsen it. They argue that standardization is a prerequisite for any meaningful ePA implementation.

Mandate that payers declare their PA requirements in a standardized, machine-readable format, simplify rules to be based on discrete, electronically available data, and standardize data requirements before building technology around them.

HL7 Da Vinci Project noted the primary challenge is for payers to "express the data needed for PA decision-making in CQL," calling it a "business transformation issue." NASL stated that programming for "unending combinations of payers and payer requirements" is why "standardization is urgently needed."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining the ability to manage utilization and costs for diverse plans, defending the role of PA, and (for UM vendors) positioning their products as a solution.
specificPoints: This group offers the only dissent to mandatory standardization. AHIP provides a direct rebuttal. Others, like MCG Health and HealthHelp, offer a market-based alternative: payers should adopt their independently developed, evidence-based guidelines as a de facto standard, promoting a collaborative rather than purely adversarial model.
Proposed solutions include payer autonomy (AHIP), adoption of third-party evidence-based guidelines (MCG Health, HealthHelp), and payer transparency (Priority Health).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Direct and irreversible patient harm caused by being forced to "fail first" on inappropriate treatments and by delays in accessing physician-recommended care.
specificPoints: They provide the most compelling arguments regarding patient safety and the human cost of the current system, focusing on outcomes like "irreversible disease progression".
Ensure PA requirements support clinically effective treatments and do not defy physician recommendations for appropriate care.

The Michael J. Fox Foundation for Parkinson's Research powerfully stated that PA "forces people with Parkinson’s to try ineffective treatments," leading to negative impacts and irreversible progression.

noteworthyInsights.insight: "A Business Transformation Issue" - The HL7 Da Vinci Project astutely frames the core challenge not as a technological one, but as a "business transformation issue" for payers, who must first do the difficult work of codifying and standardizing their opaque clinical rules before any technology can automate them.

"The Paradox of Cancer Care PA" - The Alliance of Dedicated Cancer Centers highlights a critical flaw in the PA process for oncology: "The payers approve the prior authorization requests because the care plans follow NCI guidelines, but the harm in care delay occurs." This suggests the process itself, even when it results in an approval, is a primary source of harm.

"Data-Backed Indictment of the Status Quo" - The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons provides powerful survey data: "Less than 1% of respondents... stated health payors always base prior authorization criteria on evidence-based medicine... while a significant 46% of respondents stated payors rarely used such data."

"The Pediatric Blind Spot" - The American Academy of Pediatrics brings a unique perspective, noting that "Adult rules for PA are often inappropriately applied to the pediatric population," calling for pediatric-specific expertise in policy development.

"The Infeasibility of Automation without Standardization" - Health IT vendors like NASL make a compelling technical argument that standardization is a prerequisite for progress, stating that vendors "cannot program for the possibly unending combinations of payers and payer requirements."

emergingPatterns.pattern: Technology as a Potential Threat: A recurring fear is that automating the current PA system without first fixing the underlying rules will only make things worse. The American Urological Association and others worry that ePA could lead to a "proliferation of clinically inappropriate prior authorization requirements" because it will be easier for payers to implement them.

An Almost Complete Provider-Payer Stalemate: The chasm between the provider community's demand for standardization and the payer industry's defense of autonomy is absolute. This suggests that voluntary or market-based solutions are unlikely to succeed, and regulatory intervention is the only path forward in the view of most commenters.

"Transparency" as an Insufficient Solution: While universally desired, many commenters imply that simple transparency is not enough. The Federation of American Hospitals notes that even when payers provide rule books, they are "cumbersome, non-transparent, confusing, [and] obfuscate the rules," suggesting that the rules themselves must be standardized, not just published.

keyQuotations.quote: [The] complexity subverts medical necessity guidelines and prioritizes process over health and wellness decisions.

Less than 1% of respondents to the 2022 AAHKS Survey stated health payors always base prior authorization criteria on evidence-based medicine and/or guidelines from national medical specialty societies, while a significant 46% of respondents stated payors rarely used such data in prior authorization criteria.

Prior authorization approvals for drugs are based on several factors, including previous 'failures' with other medications... Thus, people with Parkinson’s often do not get symptom relief and suffer negative impacts, including irreversible disease progression, because PA forces them to try ineffective treatments.
Health IT companies serving LTPAC and other providers... cannot program for the possibly unending combinations of payers and payer requirements, which is why standardization is urgently needed.
The payers approve the prior authorization requests because the care plans follow NCI guidelines, but the harm in care delay occurs.

A challenging aspect will be for payers to express the data needed for PA decision-making in CQL for automated documentation collection and evaluation. This is primarily a business transformation issue, not a technology issue.

We believe the real purpose of third-party prior authorizations should be to assure that patients are covered for a specific diagnosis or procedure, not to question the ordering physician on his/her medical knowledge and understanding.
Health plans are not clear when a prior authorization request fails to meet medical policy," frequently providing 'generic denial responses' such as 'The service did not meet medical policy'.

Guidelines from insurers are not always consistent with specialty guidelines, and prior authorization requirements and coverage denials frequently supersede clinician judgement, creating the most significant obstacles to patient care.
Unless and until payer PA criteria are standardized, e-PA efficiencies are likely to be ephemeral.
Despite 15 years of data from multiple centers and state registries showing improved outcomes and decreased 90-day costs, some insurers deny care as 'experimental.'
This survey clearly and alarmingly highlights the inappropriate admission placement tools and guidelines currently used by MA plans.
Payers are also inconsistent with items that require PA.

7.3.themeDescription: Requiring Public Reporting of Payer PA Metrics

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming, near-unanimous consensus across all stakeholder types for a federal mandate requiring payers to publicly report their prior authorization (PA) performance metrics. The primary tension is not if this should be done, but how—specifically, which metrics are essential, who is the primary audience for the data, and how to prevent misinterpretation. The dominant thrust of recommendations is for a comprehensive, standardized set of publicly reported data (including approval/denial rates, decision times, and appeal outcomes) to be used by regulators for oversight, by providers to gain leverage, and by patients to make informed choices, ultimately creating market pressure for payers to reduce administrative burdens.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters (20 of 21) across all stakeholder groups—including providers, trade associations, government agencies, and technology vendors—unequivocally support requiring payers to publicly report PA metrics. This is seen as a foundational step toward transparency and accountability. The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) calls for the public release of "payer prior authorization data regarding approvals, denials, appeals, wait times, and other important metrics". BJC HealthCare states that reporting "must be a mandatory function within all future e-PA modules and CEHRTs" and that plans should be required to issue "regular public reports on their use of PA limitations". Technology vendor Olive AI "strongly supports requiring payers to publicly report their PA metrics," believing it will foster healthy market competition.

exceptions.text: No commenter explicitly opposed public reporting. However, one commenter (AAHomecare) provided a significant caveat regarding the interpretation of denial data, urging caution against using it as a simple measure of provider compliance.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters agree on a core set of metrics that must be reported. While some propose more extensive lists, there is broad agreement on the fundamentals. The Physicians Advocacy Institute (PAI) specifies the need for "the list of services requiring prior authorization, percent approved, percent denied or denied and later approved, and time to final approval". PennMedicine recommends reporting on "approval, denial (including reason for denial), pending status by payer, and time to resolution". Epic Systems Corporation agrees that health plans should publish "metrics on median turnaround time for processing PA requests, as well as approval and denial rates for items and services".

exceptions.text: None. While some commenters advocate for more granular data, all support this core set.

areasOfDebate.topic: Primary Purpose and Audience for Reported Data
areasOfDebate.description: The central debate is whether the primary audience for reported data should be consumers, to drive market competition, or regulators and providers, for oversight and negotiation.

positions.label: Market Pressure
positions.stance: The data's primary purpose is to empower consumers and create market competition.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily supported by technology vendors and some provider groups.
keyArguments: If consumers can see metrics like average turnaround times, they can select more responsive health plans during open enrollment, forcing payers to compete on efficiency and service. This creates a market-based incentive for improvement.

positions.label: Oversight & Leverage
positions.stance: The data's primary purpose is for regulatory oversight and to provide leverage for healthcare providers.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by most healthcare providers, their associations, and government entities.
keyArguments: Regulators (federal and state) need this data to monitor payer behavior, identify bad actors, and enforce rules. Providers can use their own performance data to negotiate for "authorization relief" (gold-carding) from payers.

areasOfDebate.description: This debate centers on the necessary level of detail for reported metrics, contrasting a core, high-level set with a more granular, detailed dataset.

positions.label: Core Metrics
positions.stance: Reporting should focus on a standardized set of high-level, essential metrics.
positions.supportLevel: Broadly supported as a baseline by most commenters.
keyArguments: A core set (approvals, denials, decision times) is the most critical information needed for transparency and is easier to implement consistently across all payers.

positions.label: Deep Granularity
positions.stance: Reporting must include highly detailed and specific data points.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily advocated by technology vendors and physician advocacy groups seeking deeper insights.
keyArguments: High-level data can hide problems. Granular data, such as decision times in "seconds and minutes" or tracking post-claim denials that are later paid, is needed to push for true automation and expose administrative waste.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden, preventing care delays, and holding payers accountable for opaque and inconsistent processes.
specificPoints: They see performance data as a tool for direct leverage. Dana-Farber uniquely proposed using individual provider performance data to negotiate for "authorization relief" from payers.

They propose to mandate public reporting of a comprehensive list of metrics, including denial reasons and appeal overturn rates. They strongly support embedding these reporting requirements into certified health IT.
BJC HealthCare insists reporting "must be a mandatory function within all future e-PA modules".
The South Carolina Medical Association wants data collected on "interoperability or connectivity issues" that cause inadvertent denials.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring that regulations foster innovation and create a competitive market for their solutions.
specificPoints: They view transparency as a market catalyst. They are most likely to advocate for highly granular, machine-readable data that can be used to develop new tools and drive automation.
They propose to require reporting of metrics at a very detailed level (e.g., "seconds and minutes" for decisions) to incentivize real-time automation. Frame public data as a tool for consumer choice.
1upHealth proposes reporting decision times with high granularity to "incentivize exploring real-time PA automation".
Olive AI argues public metrics allow consumers to "select responsive and efficient health plans".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Gaining data for effective oversight of health plan quality and service trends.
specificPoints: This group views the data through the lens of a regulator and public program administrator.
They propose to ensure that new capabilities include reporting requirements that allow CMS and state agencies like CalHHS to review data for oversight purposes.
CalHHS states that capabilities "should include reporting requirements so CMS and plans can review data for oversight of quality and service trends".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preventing the misinterpretation of reported data, which could unfairly penalize suppliers.
specificPoints: AAHomecare is the only commenter to highlight that PA requests are sometimes submitted with the expectation of a denial. This is done to provide official documentation to a prescriber or patient that an item is not covered.

They propose to include a field on the PA request where the submitter can indicate if they expect a non-affirmation, so that denial rates can be interpreted correctly and used for provider education rather than punishment.
AAHomecare recommends tracking requests based on the expected decision, as a high non-affirmation rate "should not be a reflection of supplier activity or compliance".

noteworthyInsights.insight: The most unique insight comes from AAHomecare, which explains that suppliers "frequently submit PA requests anticipating a non-affirmation decision to demonstrate to the prescriber and/or beneficiary that an order did not meet coverage requirements". This reframes a "denial" from a purely negative outcome to a necessary administrative step in some workflows.

Dana-Farber Cancer Institute articulates a novel use for the data: providers should be able to see their own performance metrics compared to peers and "leverage this performance score for access to authorization relief". This moves beyond simple transparency to an actionable tool for reducing burden on high-performing clinicians.

1upHealth’s recommendation to report decision times in "seconds and minutes" is a forward-looking proposal designed to distinguish between slow manual processes and true electronic automation, thereby incentivizing payers to invest in more advanced, real-time systems.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Commenters are looking to state-level actions as models. Allina Health cites legislation in Minnesota as a positive example, and the SCHA sees federal data as a way to empower state insurance departments. This suggests a pattern where federal rules could either replicate or enable state-based oversight.

A clear pattern emerges of two complementary, but distinct, primary audiences for the data: 1) Consumers, to drive market competition during plan selection (emphasized by tech vendors), and 2) Regulators/Providers, for oversight and direct negotiation (emphasized by provider organizations). Effective policy would likely need to serve both purposes.

The most significant potential unintended consequence—penalizing providers for intentionally sought denials—was clearly identified by AAHomecare, indicating that a simplistic approach to metric reporting could have negative effects.

keyQuotations.quote: [We] reiterate our support for public reporting of prior authorization performance data...similar to the legislation enacted in Minnesota to address ongoing challenges with prior authorization.
Providers should understand how their performance measures against others and leverage this performance score for access to authorization relief.

The PA non-affirmation rate should not be a reflection of supplier activity or compliance, as suppliers frequently submit PA requests anticipating a non-affirmation decision to demonstrate to the prescriber and/or beneficiary that an order did not meet coverage requirements.

If consumers have visibility into a health plan’s ePA compliance and performance...they can use this insight to select responsive and efficient health plans during open enrollment, fostering healthy market competition.
Such reporting 'must be a mandatory function within all future e-PA modules and CEHRTs'.
...making more raw data available will create new opportunities for state insurance departments to create better oversight of insurance companies and consider quality measurement and standards.
Reporting at the 'granularity of seconds and minutes for approvals/denials' will incentivize exploring real-time PA automation.
Without mechanisms for transparency and accountability, patient care will continue to be negatively impacted.
Not having availability to payors' data on prior authorizations leaves physicians without information on how to meet payor criteria or whether problems exist.

7.4.themeDescription: Enabling Real-Time Transparency of PA Requirements

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming, near-unanimous consensus across all stakeholder groups—providers, payers, tech vendors, and patients—on the urgent need for real-time, point-of-care transparency of prior authorization (PA) requirements. The primary goal is to eliminate administrative "guesswork" and prevent unnecessary PA submissions by allowing providers to electronically query a payer’s rules directly from their workflow. The central tension is not if this should be done, but how: a debate exists between implementing a dynamic, real-time API query model (like HL7 Da Vinci CRD) versus a system of payers publishing machine-readable files of their rules. The dominant recommendation is for regulators to mandate that payers expose their PA requirements via a standardized, workflow-integrated API, but this is accompanied by a critical warning: the system will fail if the data provided by payers is not accurate, complete, and trustworthy.

consensusPoints.text: A universal demand for a real-time, queryable system for PA requirements. This is seen as a foundational requirement for any meaningful PA reform.

exceptions.text: No commenters opposed the principle of transparency.

consensusPoints.text: The primary goal is to avoid unnecessary PA submissions, not just automate existing ones. A strong majority of commenters, particularly providers and standards bodies, emphasized that the greatest value of real-time transparency lies in preventing the submission of PAs that are not required. This avoids wasted effort for both providers and payers.

Transparency must be integrated directly into the clinical workflow. To be effective, the ability to check PA requirements cannot live in an external web portal. It must be seamlessly integrated into the Electronic Health Record (EHR) at the moment a provider is ordering a service or medication.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A vast majority of providers and health IT vendors

areasOfDebate.topic: Technical Mechanism for Exposing Payer Rules

positions.label: Live API Query
positions.stance: A system where the provider's EHR sends a real-time, automated query to the payer's API for a specific patient and service at the point of ordering.
positions.supportLevel: The most widely supported approach, favored by most providers, standards bodies, and many tech vendors.
keyArguments: Provides the most current, patient-specific information
prevents EHRs from storing outdated rules
enables dynamic, complex rule-checking

positions.label: Published Files
positions.stance: An alternative model where payers publish a comprehensive, machine-readable file of their PA requirements (e.g., quarterly), which EHRs would then consume and use to make determinations locally.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed by a few major health IT vendors.
keyArguments: Reduces the volume of API calls ('chattiness') between systems
could be simpler for payers to implement than a complex real-time API

positions.label: Computable Rules
positions.stance: Payer requirements should be returned in a processable, computable format (e.g., Clinical Quality Language - CQL) that can be directly executed by a Clinical Decision Support (CDS) engine in the EHR.
positions.supportLevel: A minority of highly technical commenters, including standards consultants and tech developers.
keyArguments: Enables true automation and intelligent workflows
brings the "rules to the data" for immediate evaluation without human interpretation
prevents ambiguity

positions.label: Standardized Text
positions.stance: The immediate priority is getting clear, standardized, electronically accessible text that lists documentation requirements, even if it's not fully computable.
positions.supportLevel: Implicitly supported by the majority of provider and association commenters who focus on ending the current chaos of phone calls and portal hunting.
keyArguments: A more pragmatic and achievable first step than requiring all payers to immediately codify complex rules into a computable language
still a massive improvement over the status quo

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The daily frustration of inconsistent, opaque, and constantly changing payer rules; care delays and negative patient outcomes from 'guesswork'; extreme administrative burden from navigating portals and making phone calls.
specificPoints: They provide the most visceral descriptions of the current system's failures, describing it as a "maze of opacity and complexity" and a "game of ‘guess what I’m thinking’".

They propose solutions to mandate that payers provide real-time, actionable information within the EHR workflow that is returned in seconds and includes specific documentation needs, coverage details, and even formulary alternatives.
Moffitt Cancer Center details how rules are inconsistent even within the same insurance company, and requirements for a single test are often hidden until a claim is denied.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Aggregating member complaints about administrative burden, provider burnout, and patient harm; ensuring any new standard is robust and universally adopted.
specificPoints: They often cite data to quantify the problem, such as the WEDI survey finding only 18% of providers could check PA requirements without initiating a request. They also uniquely focus on ensuring the system accounts for exemptions like "gold carding".

They propose solutions with strong advocacy for federal mandates requiring payers to adopt standards-based APIs (like Da Vinci CRD/DTR) and make all clinical criteria and requirements electronically accessible at the point of care.
The American College of Cardiology calls this a "bare minimum requirement" for modern healthcare.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Technical feasibility, the need for clear and stable standards, and the implementation challenges of connecting to thousands of different payers.
specificPoints: They focus on the architectural 'how,' raising critical issues like the need for a national payer endpoint directory, the debate between live APIs and published files, and the necessity of receiving rules in a truly computable format.

They propose solutions like a phased approach, potentially starting with a "yes/no" answer on PA need; development of a national endpoint directory; and clear mandates for payers to publish rules in a machine-readable format.

Massachusetts Health Data Consortium provides a stark warning: "We strongly suggest you not attempt to replicate, store or update the applicability of PA rules on the CEHRT side" as they change too frequently.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Improving efficiency, reducing provider abrasion, and maintaining the integrity of utilization management.
specificPoints: They frame their support in terms of mutual benefit, noting that transparency can "decrease PA requests" and reduce call center volume, saving them resources. They often highlight their existing, proprietary ePA platforms as evidence of their commitment.
They show general support for a "standardized electronic approach" and making requirements electronically accessible in EHRs at the point of care.
AHIP notes that inquiries on PA requirements are "high volume, and increased automation (CRD) would provide great efficiencies".

noteworthyInsights.insight: Paradigm Shift: "Bring the Rules to the Data" - Elsevier powerfully articulated the ideal state by referencing a HITAC member's comment that ePA should "bring the rules to the data rather than the data to the rules." This captures the shift from providers manually gathering data and pushing it to an opaque payer system, to an automated process where computable payer rules are brought into the EHR and evaluated against patient data in real time.

Hidden Failure Mode: Authorizing Non-Covered Benefits - Dana-Farber Cancer Institute pointed out a subtle but devastating problem where health plans "will authorize treatments for patients who do not have benefits for the service." This means transparency must go beyond just the PA requirement to include confirmation of the patient's actual benefit coverage to prevent situations where a patient gets an approved service they are later billed for in full.

Payer Accountability: Use Your Own Data - MCG Health proposed that payers should be required to satisfy certain documentation requirements (e.g., proof of antecedent treatments like physical therapy) from their own data repositories (claims, prior PAs) before asking the provider to resubmit that information. This would shift the burden of demonstrating history from the provider to the payer who already holds the data.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Experience Correlations: Commenters with deep technical or standards-development expertise (e.g., Lantana, Point-of-Care Partners, HL7) focus heavily on the need for computable rules and harmonization of standards, seeing this as the key to true automation. In contrast, commenters from clinical practice (e.g., UCSF, Peter Basch) focus more on the user experience: speed, workflow integration, and the quality of the conversation it enables with the patient.

Facility-type Patterns: Large, integrated delivery networks that also operate a health plan (e.g., Kaiser, Providence, Priority Health) have a uniquely balanced perspective. They strongly support transparency, seeing the benefits for both their provider and payer arms, but are also acutely aware of the technical challenges of implementation.

Unintended Consequences: Multiple commenters, led by the AMA, identified a key potential unintended consequence: if a mandated transparency system provides unreliable data, it will not only fail but will actively destroy provider trust and set back the cause of automation by reinforcing the belief that manual verification is the only safe path.

keyQuotations.quote: [The current system is like a] metaphorical contestant in an electronic game of “guess what I’m thinking.”
The Electronic Prior Authorization process should immediately and transparently identify prior authorization requirements, so physicians can provide the correct information on the first submission.
A major adoption barrier for providers is that they do not trust payer information about PA because of historical challenges in accuracy or completeness of data in existing HIT systems.
We strongly suggest you not attempt to replicate, store or update the applicability of PA rules on the CEHRT side.
