It should be a bare minimum requirement that healthcare providers and staff can electronically determine if a PA is required and the specific requirements for each item/service without initiating a request.
Patients are harmed most by prior authorization when clinicians do not have easy access to prior authorization or step therapy limitations.

We suggest making rules available in a computable format, such as CQL, so that a CDS service (such as CDSHooks) could consume those rules directly and alert clinicians in real time when prior authorization is applicable.
For electronic prior authorization to be meaningfully useful to the clinician, decrease burden, and improve patient care, the response from the payer must contain actionable information.
Many payers give status updates that simply read, “pending,” but with no indication of what further information might be needed for a final decision.
Providers are blind to what device is covered by a patient’s payer, if there are specific supplier requirements, or if authorization is needed.
Automating the capability to identify when prior authorization is required provides a 'quick win' as 10-50% of authorization requests are submitted when not actually required.

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment overwhelmingly supports implementing "gold carding" programs to exempt high-performing providers from prior authorization, with near-unanimous agreement from providers, professional associations, and advocacy groups who see it as a key strategy to reduce administrative waste and care delays. The central tension pits this widespread call for burden reduction against a lone but notable concern from one vendor that such programs increase healthcare costs. Key recommendations converge on adopting an established legislative model, such as the one in Texas, and mandating that these exemptions be automated and integrated directly into certified electronic prior authorization workflows.

consensusPoints.text: There is near-unanimous consensus across all stakeholder types—including healthcare providers, professional organizations, trade associations, and even some payers and technology vendors—that a "gold carding" or provider exemption program is a necessary and beneficial reform. Of the 28 commenters, 27 expressed clear support for the concept. The core agreement is that requiring prior authorization from providers who consistently demonstrate adherence to evidence-based medicine and have high approval rates is an unnecessary, wasteful administrative burden that delays care.

exceptions.text: One technology vendor, Olive AI, explicitly argued against gold carding programs, citing concerns about increased healthcare spending.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters, particularly those with technical expertise, agreed that for an exemption program to be effective, it must be automated and integrated into the electronic prior authorization (ePA) workflow. The exemption status of a provider must be communicated in real-time at the point of care to realize the burden reduction benefits.

areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether gold carding programs effectively reduce administrative burden and care delays, or if they primarily lead to increased healthcare spending.

positions.label: Reduce Burden
positions.stance: Implement performance-based exemptions to reduce administrative waste and care delays.
positions.supportLevel: The overwhelming majority
keyArguments: Frees up clinical and administrative resources to focus on patient care rather than low-value paperwork.
Allows payers to focus review efforts on actual outliers instead of compliant providers.
Reduces dangerous delays in care for services that are almost always approved, improving patient outcomes.

positions.label: Increase Costs
positions.stance: Do not implement these programs as they drive up spending.
positions.supportLevel: One technology vendor
keyArguments: Gold carding and direct elimination of PAs have "repeatedly shown to increase healthcare spending without noticeable improvement in quality or value."

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Severe administrative burden, wasted staff time, provider burnout, and harmful patient care delays caused by PAs for routinely approved services.
specificPoints: They provide specific, real-world examples of the absurdity of the current system, such as Dana-Farber's note that despite an "incredibly low rate of clinical prior authorization denials for radiation oncology services," health plans continue to manage this service tightly.
They strongly advocate for automated, performance-based systems that grant exemptions based on metrics like a <1% denial rate or a 90% approval rate, integrated directly into the EHR.
UCSF's analogy of gold carding being like "TSA pre-check" for physicians practicing evidence-based medicine powerfully frames the issue.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Translating their members' widespread pain into concrete policy action. They focus on standardization, regulatory mandates, and creating viable models for implementation.
specificPoints: They often cite specific legislative models, like Texas HB 3459, as a proven template for national policy (TMA, PAI, AdvaMed, SCMA).
They also connect the technical standards (e.g., EHR certification) to the policy goals.

Advocate for ONC to require support for gold carding in certified health IT, link program adoption to payer quality ratings (ACOG, FAH), and adopt specific metrics (e.g., 90% approval over 6 months) as a national standard.
The Texas Medical Association promotes its state's successful legislation as a model for federal action.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The business and technical implications of implementation.
specificPoints: This group contains the only dissenting voice (Olive AI), which fears increased costs. Conversely, it also contains a crucial assenting voice from a major payer (Anthem), which confirms that ePA standards should be able to support provider performance-based rules.
Technology vendors like Cohere Health see it as a positive incentive, rebranding it as "greenlighting".
Most business stakeholders want clear, standardized rules and technical specifications so they can build compliant and effective products.

Anthem's comment that systems should be able to handle variable requirements "prevents providers with consistent past performance from being forced to meet higher documentation standards required of others" is a key insight from a payer perspective.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient access to care, reducing system-wide burden, and aligning regulations with clinical reality.
specificPoints: These groups are adept at framing the issue in terms of established policy language, adopting terms like "trust and verify framework" from task force recommendations to build consensus (ASCO, RRC).
They also cite precedent from existing CMS programs to argue for broader adoption (ADCC, MSK).
Push for HHS to require or strongly incentivize gold carding, and for ONC to build support for it into the core functionality of certified health IT.
The Physicians Advocacy Institute (PAI) specifically recommends ONC consider adopting the Texas model.

noteworthyInsights.insight: UCSF's framing of gold carding as "analogous to TSA Pre-Check" for "physicians practicing evidence-based medicine" is a powerful, easily understood metaphor that crystallizes the concept for policymakers.

Anthem, a major national payer, stated that any new ePA standard "should be able to match the prior authorization request with these variables, allowing for different requirements based on consistent provider performance." This is a crucial piece of evidence from a payer that accommodating these programs is technically feasible and expected.

AAHomecare proposed a logical and impactful extension of the "trust" principle: claims that receive prior authorization should be exempt from routine post-payment audits, barring suspicion of fraud. This would address another major source of provider burden.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Convergence on the "Texas Model": The Texas law (HB 3459), with its clear 90% approval threshold over six months, was repeatedly cited by multiple state and national associations (PAI, TMA, AdvaMed, SCMA) as a concrete, replicable model for federal regulation.

Adoption of "Trust and Verify" Language: The phrase "trust and verify framework," likely originating from an ONC task force, has been widely adopted by major advocacy groups (AMA, ASCO, RRC) and businesses (Elsevier), indicating a shared understanding and a common language for this policy goal.

Automation is Non-Negotiable: A consistent pattern across provider, association, and technical stakeholders is the insistence that policy must be married to technology. A gold carding program that isn't automated and integrated into the clinical workflow at the point of ordering is seen as an incomplete and likely ineffective solution.

keyQuotations.quote: [Electronic Prior Authorization should help implement 'gold-carding' (where physicians practicing evidence-based medicine receive bypass status, like TSA pre-check).]
Continuing to require prior authorization from providers despite such a proven track record is an unnecessary administrative burden with little clinical benefit for patients.

High approval rates after inordinate delays drive the perception among clinicians that the goal of prior authorization is to discourage appropriate consumption of healthcare resources rather than to ensure high-quality care.

Olive explicitly argues against implementing 'gold carding' or direct elimination of PAs. They believe such programs have repeatedly shown to increase healthcare spending without noticeable improvement in quality or value.

Any ePA standard... should be able to match the prior authorization request with these variables, allowing for different requirements based on consistent provider performance. This approach prevents providers with consistent past performance from being forced to meet higher documentation standards required of others.

The expansion of PA should exempt any claims for items that receive PA from subsequent routine audits, unless probable fraud and abuse is suspected. This would dramatically reduce the burden placed on the Medicare audit and appeals system...
WEDI urges ONC, CMS, and partners to convene a cross-industry group to develop guidelines for a model Gold Carding program.
The ONC’s e-PA certification requirements should be designed to include functionalities that enable providers to take advantage of: Trust and verify frameworks (i.e., gold carding).

7.6.themeDescription: Ensuring Accountability for Third-Party Payer Vendors

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment is overwhelmingly critical of the current role third-party vendors (UMOs, RBMs, PBMs) play in the prior authorization ecosystem. Providers and their advocates universally describe a fragmented, opaque, and burdensome system where these delegated entities operate with little accountability, causing significant care delays and administrative waste. The key tension lies between providers demanding that vendors be forced to adopt modern, unified API standards, and payers noting the complexity and legacy systems involved. The dominant recommendation is that regulatory standards must apply equally to these third-party vendors, and that health plans must be held responsible for providing a single, seamless electronic gateway for providers, regardless of their own internal delegation arrangements.

consensusPoints.text: Third-party vendors must be held to the same standards as payers. Commenters who addressed the topic asserted that any entity performing utilization management on behalf of a payer must be subject to the same requirements for timeliness, transparency, and technical compliance. This was the central, unifying principle across provider, association, and technology vendor comments.

exceptions.text: While payers like Cigna and AHIP acknowledge the need to incorporate their vendors, they frame it as a complex integration challenge rather than a simple mandate.

consensusPoints.text: The delegated PA model creates fragmentation and confusion for providers. Providers and their associations detailed profound operational confusion caused by payers outsourcing PA. Identifying the correct entity to contact, navigating disparate requirements for a single episode of care, and reconciling conflicting information are common, burdensome experiences.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of providers and their associations

consensusPoints.text: Health plans should be ultimately responsible for their vendors' performance. A clear consensus among providers is that the burden of managing third-party relationships should fall on the payer, not the provider. The desired state is a seamless, single point of contact with the health plan, which would then be responsible for coordinating with its own vendors.

areasOfDebate.description: The primary debate centers on the best path forward for technical integration of third-party vendors, pitting the mandate for new, modern API standards against the preservation of established X12 standards to avoid disruption.

positions.label: Mandate Modern APIs
positions.stance: Mandate that all entities in the PA process, including third-party vendors, adopt modern, certified, API-based standards (e.g., FHIR Da Vinci Implementation Guides). This position is advanced by providers, their associations like the American Medical Association, and technology vendors like RadNet and Amazon Web Services.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, including nearly all providers, their associations, and technology vendors.
keyArguments: Failure to include vendors creates a 'critical gap' that renders interoperability efforts moot.
A unified standard is the only way to reduce provider burden and create a scalable, seamless system.
It prevents vendors from becoming a technical bottleneck.

positions.label: Preserve Existing Standards
positions.stance: Caution against forcing new API requirements onto the established X12 standards already used for communications with many third-party Utilization Management administrators. This position is articulated by CVS Health.
positions.supportLevel: A distinct minority position, articulated by a major payer/PBM.
keyArguments: Many insurers already follow existing HIPAA technical standards (X12) for third-party UM.
Simply layering new certification processes onto these existing functions will not necessarily reduce workload and may add complexity.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Healthcare Providers (Health Systems, Specialty Centers, and Practices)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Extreme administrative burden from fragmented systems; patient care delays caused by confusion and opaque vendor processes; having to integrate with multiple proprietary vendor portals.
specificPoints: They provide the most granular, visceral examples of system failure, such as the need for two separate PAs for one PET scan (Moffitt) or the staggering cost and time to integrate with a single RBM (RadNet).
Mandate a single API gateway through the health plan.
Require vendors to use the same technology as payers.
Eliminate forced use of third-party vendor portals.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Systemic regulatory gaps that allow vendors to operate without accountability; unqualified peer reviewers at vendor organizations; potential for vendors to monetize new regulatory requirements through added fees.
specificPoints: They aggregate member complaints into national policy issues, highlighting how individual frustrations point to a lack of clear regulations for intermediaries (AMA) and the need for national standardization of vendor operations (SCHA).
Close the regulatory loophole for intermediaries.
Mandate same-specialty peer review (ASTRO; APMA).
Require vendors to adhere to national clinical guidelines.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The complexity of integrating multiple proprietary vendor systems into a coherent ePA process; avoiding disruptive mandates that ignore existing infrastructure.
specificPoints: They offer an insider's view, noting that they themselves can be constrained by their vendors' technology (ACHP) and defending the use of established (non-API) standards for third-party communication (CVS Health).
A focus on testing and integration of systems rather than blanket mandates (Cigna).

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring new standards are applied universally to create a functional, scalable market for automation and interoperability solutions.
specificPoints: They speak to the technical prerequisites for success, advocating for certification criteria to apply to any entity performing PA (Point-of-Care Partners) and outlining how standardized data can enable automation via AI/NLP (Olive AI).
One vendor also provides a defense of the UM model (HealthHelp).
Extend certification criteria to all delegated entities.
Use standards to create a single front-end for providers.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The 'Many-to-Many' Technical Hurdle: Mass General Brigham offers a cogent explanation for vendor dysfunction: a single RBM may service many payers, each with many plans, making it technically prohibitive to 'maintain a robust online system' due to the need for 'constant updates to their computer programming.' This points to a structural market failure, not just bad actors.

The Concrete Cost of Integration: RadNet provides a rare and powerful data point on the direct financial burden of vendor fragmentation, stating that integrating their system with just one major RBM cost 'nearly $100,000 and required over 700 staff hours.'

The Payer's Own Constraint: The Alliance of Community Health Plans (ACHP) adds an important nuance, stating that 'Payers generally obtain administrative platforms from vendors, which means the payer is restricted to what the vendor provides.' This suggests payers may not have full control over the technology used by their delegates, complicating accountability.

The Vendor Counter-Narrative: In a landscape of complaints, HealthHelp, LLC, a UM vendor, proactively argues that its model is educational and collaborative, and that its 'financial incentives and quotas do not influence appropriate use recommendations.'

emergingPatterns.pattern: Specialty Care as the Epicenter: The most acute frustrations with third-party vendors are concentrated in specialty fields like oncology (Moffitt, Dana-Farber, ASTRO), radiology (RadNet, Mass General Brigham), and physical therapy (APTA), where complex care decisions are frequently delegated to non-specialist reviewers at RBMs and other UMOs.

Delegation Itself is the Flaw: A clear pattern shows that the very act of a payer delegating PA to a third party introduces new points of failure. The hand-off creates confusion over responsibility (Dana-Farber), fragmented submission requirements (Moffitt), and technology gaps (AMA), suggesting the problem is structural to the delegation model as currently practiced.

Provider Demand for a 'Single Pane of Glass': A consistent solution proposed by providers is that the payer must serve as a single, unified front-end or 'gateway.' Providers want to interact with one entity—the payer—via one API, and let the payer manage its own complex web of vendors behind the scenes (NY Urology, Olive AI).

keyQuotations.quote: [It] should be the health plan's responsibility to 'build and maintain real-time API connections to all its 'business associates' and return all information through ONE back connection to the healthcare provider.' Healthcare providers 'should NOT be in the position of running the health plan's IT operations.'

We feel that a potential reason for not converting third parties to a faster and more efficient modes of operation is not related to the care of a patient, but simply a means of creating the potential for claims denials and to slow the delivery of patient claims and reimbursement.
Some services require authorization for one CPT code through the insurance company and an additional authorization from a third-party vendor for a different CPT code that is part of the same test.
It is impossible for a reviewer to determine if a treatment is efficient and appropriate unless the review is performed by a radiation oncologist.
Providers are 'left to navigate these complex relationships' when trying to secure an authorization, which 'often leads to delays'.

RadNet is currently integrating/automating its prior-authorization process with one of the largest RBMs in the country, which has an estimated cost of 'nearly $100,000 and required over 700 staff hours for this integration.'
Commercial payors frequently use Radiology Benefits Management (RBM) companies... [which] service many different payors and plans, [making] a robust online system difficult to maintain.
It is 'not always clear when a TPA is involved or how to interact with them'.
[New API requirements should not be forced onto existing X12 functions as] simply porting the certification process onto other functions will not reduce the workload for each functionality.
Sporadic use of IGs across the PA process due to lack of obligation for intermediaries/clearinghouses could increase administrative burden and patient harm.

8.1.themeDescription: Concerns about Health IT Costs and Vendor Fees

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on the costs of Health IT for electronic prior authorization (ePA) is overwhelmingly one of concern, bordering on alarm, from the provider community. While there is broad consensus on the need for electronic solutions, there is near-unanimous agreement that the financial burden of acquiring, implementing, and maintaining the necessary technology should not fall on providers, especially smaller practices. The central tension is not if there will be costs, but who should pay for them, with a dominant recommendation that payers, who mandate prior authorization, should bear the costs, and that federal agencies must prevent EHR vendors from profiting off regulatory requirements through excessive fees.

consensusPoints.text: New ePA technology will impose significant, multi-faceted costs on providers. Nearly all commenters, particularly healthcare providers and their associations, agree that implementing ePA is not a simple software purchase. It involves substantial direct and indirect costs, including technology acquisition, system updates, staff training, workflow redesign, and ongoing maintenance. Support for this point is virtually unanimous across all provider and association comments. The American Association of Medical Colleges (AAMC) states that "The costs and time invested to adopt new certified EHR technology standards... are not nominal" and require "significant staffing hours to health IT deployment and clinician training". RadNet details costs for technology, personnel, and workflow modifications. The Connecticut Hospital Association highlights that new technology is "extremely resource-intensive".

EHR vendors are expected to pass development costs on to providers. A strong majority of commenters, especially physician advocacy groups, express deep-seated concern that EHR vendors will leverage new certification requirements to charge providers for expensive upgrades, new modules, or API access fees. This is seen as a primary mechanism through which costs will be unfairly shifted. The Texas Medical Association (TMA) worries "that EHR vendors will pass on development costs through expensive upgrades, fees for API access, or new module charges". The American Society for Radiation Oncology (ASTRO) is concerned that "third-party vendors will use regulatorily-required updates as an opportunity to generate additional charges and fees". Premier Inc. highlights concerns about "additional fees that EHR vendors may assess to providers" to meet regulatory requirements.

Smaller and under-resourced practices are disproportionately vulnerable to these costs. Most stakeholders, including provider groups, payers, and government entities, acknowledge that smaller, independent, or rural practices lack the capital and IT expertise of larger systems. They fear these practices will be priced out of compliance, exacerbating health disparities. The American College of Physicians (ACP) notes, "Under-resourced health systems and practices... are also the least likely to be able to afford the implementation expenses". A New York urology practice warns the proposal "must consider its effect on smaller healthcare practices" who "will not find it financially viable to comply". Anthem, a payer, concurs that for smaller practices, "migration costs may be prohibitive".

Providers will not invest without a clear and demonstrable Return on Investment (ROI). Many commenters, led by hospital associations and implementation-focused groups like WEDI, stress that providers, facing severe financial strain, will not adopt new, expensive technologies without evidence of tangible benefits that outweigh the costs. WEDI states, "Absent a clear Return on Investment (ROI)... providers will be very unlikely to invest resources in untried and untested technologies". The American Hospital Association (AHA) believes investment may be limited "only where there are recognizable, tangible, and substantial benefits".

areasOfDebate.topic: Who Should Bear the Financial Burden of ePA Technology?
areasOfDebate.description: Who Should Bear the Financial Burden of ePA Technology?

positions.label: Payers Pay
positions.stance: The costs of ePA technology should be borne entirely by the payers who require prior authorization in the first place.
positions.supportLevel: The dominant position, held by a strong majority of physician specialty and state medical associations.
keyArguments: Since payers created the prior authorization system and its associated costs, they should pay for the technology to automate it.
Providers should be held harmless from the costs of complying with payer mandates.
This is a matter of basic fairness.

positions.label: Shared Cost
positions.stance: The investment in new ePA capabilities should be shared among all stakeholders, including providers, payers, clearinghouses, and developers.
positions.supportLevel: A minority view, articulated by at least one major healthcare provider.
keyArguments: ePA is a system-wide improvement that benefits all parties through increased efficiency.
Therefore, the investment should be a shared responsibility rather than placed on a single stakeholder group.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Physician & Provider Advocacy Groups (e.g., AMA, AAFP, ACOG, ACS, ASTRO, specialty societies)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unfunded mandates forcing them to purchase costly technology; vendors profiting from new regulations; the existential threat to small/independent practices.
specificPoints: They frame the cost issue as a moral one—that providers are being forced to pay to fix a problem created by payers, which ultimately harms patient care and drives burnout. They provide stark data, such as ACOG’s claim that providers spent $339 million on ePA in 2020 while plans spent only $11 million.
Explicitly mandate that payers bear the full cost of ePA technology; regulate or prohibit vendor fees for regulatory updates; provide direct financial incentives to practices.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Hospital Associations & Large Health Systems (e.g., AHA, FAH, Ascension, Trinity Health, Allina)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The immense resource intensity (staff hours, IT development, training) required for enterprise-wide implementation; lack of a clear ROI; costs of integrating disparate systems and using intermediaries.
specificPoints: They highlight the staggering scale of implementation in complex organizations. The Federation of American Hospitals estimates that for a large health system, piloting and adoption "could take 35,000+ hours".
Require rigorous cost-benefit analysis before mandates; ensure standards are stable and don't require costly rework; promote common, modular requirements to reduce custom build costs.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Vendors & Consultants (e.g., Cerner, Epic, Lantana, Mettle, Voluware)
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The significant development burden to build and certify new functionality; the risk that high costs will suppress provider adoption, making their investment worthless; prohibitive certification fees that stifle innovation.
specificPoints: They offer a ground-level view of the technical and market barriers. Mettle Solutions provides a critical insight that even if their app is free, EHR vendor fees to install the app are a major barrier to adoption.

Create open-source reference implementations to lower costs for all developers; restrict EHRs from charging fees for installing standards-compliant apps; use modular certification to reduce costs and encourage competition.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Non-standard, one-off solutions increasing costs for the entire industry; prohibitive migration costs for smaller practices hindering universal adoption.
specificPoints: While acknowledging provider cost burdens, their focus is on the systemic benefit of standardization as the primary cost-containment strategy.

Use industry-supported standards to lower barriers for entry and reduce costs for both providers and payers. Humana explicitly states that "the cost of EMR adoption should not be passed on to the provider".

noteworthyInsights.insight: The "App Store" Barrier: Mettle Solutions points out a critical market failure: even when a burden-reducing app is offered for free by its developer, fees charged by the major EHR vendor to simply install it from their app marketplace act as a prohibitive barrier for most physicians. This suggests that without regulation, the EHR vendor can effectively tax any innovation in its ecosystem.

Stifling Innovation Through Certification Costs: Suncoast RHIO provides a compelling argument that high, monolithic certification fees are "prohibitive except for the very large vendors," which "shuts out innovation" and limits provider choice, creating a de facto oligopoly.

The Cost Disparity Data Point: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists cites a 2020 statistic that plans spent $11 million on ePA while providers spent $339 million. This single data point powerfully crystallizes the provider community's argument about the inequitable distribution of costs.

Counterintuitive Standard Costs: Voluware, Inc., a developer with a decade of experience in the field, makes the surprising claim that using established standards like HL7 and X12 278 has been the "most expensive" way to exchange data, challenging the assumption that any mandated standard is inherently more efficient.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Small vs. Large Practice Divide: A consistent pattern is the differentiation between large health systems with IT departments and small/independent practices with limited capital and expertise. Policies are viewed through the lens of how they will impact the most vulnerable practices.

The Unfunded Mandate Framework: Providers universally frame new ePA technology requirements as an unfunded mandate. They see it as payers shifting their own administrative costs onto the clinical workforce, with EHR vendors acting as the collection agency.

A Three-Way Tension: Comments reveal a clear tension between providers (who bear the cost), payers (who drive the requirement), and vendors (who control the technology). An effective policy must navigate the competing financial interests of all three groups.

Adoption is Contingent on Affordability: A clear pattern emerges that the success or failure of any ePA standard hinges entirely on its affordability. As Gainwell Technologies noted, if it's too costly, providers simply "won't do it," rendering the entire effort futile.

keyQuotations.quote: [T]he costs of removing patient barriers should not be born solely by providers who would need to upgrade their EHR systems to implement new ONC standards that help increase efficiency for payors to continue building upon the already broken system of payor prior authorization standards.
keyQuotations.sourceType: The American Association of Hip and Knee Surgeons (AAHKS)

keyQuotations.quote: Under-resourced health systems and practices currently suffer the most in terms of prior authorization burden. They are also the least likely to be able to afford the implementation expenses of electronic prior authorization.
The financial impact and complexity of software integration would be crippling.
If the PAS API experience is too difficult or costly, providers won’t do it and the IT companies just built and went through costly certifications for no reason.
Absent that ROI, especially in times of economic uncertainty, providers will be very unlikely to invest resources in untried and untested technologies.
Mettle believes that the fees imposed by the DEVELOP vendors [EHRs] are a barrier to most physicians.

In adopting and implementing electronic prior-authorization, there will be costs related to: (1) technology and (2) personnel/human resources... RadNet is currently integrating/automating its prior-authorization process with one of the largest RBMs in the country with an estimated cost of nearly $100,000 and over 700 staff hours spent.

Beware of the tendency of some vendors... to band together creating certifications of their own at high priced consulting based fees... Providers should be able to pick and choose their own methods, products, or approaches without being held captive to certification on top of a certification with a price tag that drives them to not comply.

[HHS should] Remember that overall key goals include reducing burden on providers, reducing costs to providers, and addressing the highest per capita GDP cost of healthcare in the world, not making it worse through high priced technology requirements.

8.2.themeDescription: Call for Provider Compensation for PA Work

sections.executiveSummary: There is universal agreement among commenters that the administrative work of prior authorization is an uncompensated mandate that shifts payer costs to providers. The central tension in public input is not if providers should be paid, but how—through direct payment via new CPT codes or through softer incentive models. The dominant thrust of recommendations is for federal regulators to create a formal, mandatory mechanism to compensate providers for this labor, thereby correcting a key economic imbalance in the healthcare system.

consensusPoints.text: PA is an uncompensated mandate requiring provider compensation. All 9 commenters on this theme supported this position. They argue that this work is performed on behalf of the payer and for the payer's financial benefit, yet the provider bears the full cost in time and resources.

exceptions.text: While the agreement on the need for compensation is universal, commenters propose slightly different mechanisms, ranging from direct payment via new CPT codes to shared savings models and MIPS incentives.

areasOfDebate.description: The debate is not whether to compensate providers, but how to implement a compensation mechanism. The primary disagreement centers on whether to mandate direct payment or use a more indirect incentive-based model.

positions.label: Direct Payment
positions.stance: Mandate direct financial reimbursement for PA work through new, dedicated billing codes or other formal payment structures.
positions.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters, particularly medical practices and professional organizations.
keyArguments: Direct payment is the only way to "internalize" the cost of PA to the payers who mandate it, correcting the current "asymmetric financial incentive."
Creates a clear, standardized process for billing for administrative labor that is distinct from clinical care reimbursement.
Ensures financial viability for practices, especially smaller ones, to comply with PA requirements.

positions.label: Incentive Models
positions.stance: Use incentive programs or shared savings to encourage provider adoption and offset costs, rather than creating a direct payment requirement.
positions.supportLevel: A minority view proposed by an advocacy group and an IT vendor.
keyArguments: Incentives (like MIPS) can encourage adoption of new electronic PA (ePA) technologies without creating a complex new billing system.
Sharing the cost savings generated by ePA aligns payer and provider interests towards efficiency.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lost revenue from unreimbursed care, the direct financial cost of hiring staff for PAs, and the "uncompensated time on the phone" for highly trained clinical staff.
specificPoints: They articulate the ground-level economic reality, describing PA work as a "money-losing activity" that directly impacts their practice's bottom line and ability to invest in patient care. They also highlight the absurdity of having to expend "unreimbursed time and resources to educate" external providers hired by payers.
Strong preference for direct compensation, such as new CPT codes with statutory reimbursement rates.
NY Urology Specialists states that "if fairly compensated with a small profit margin, providers would hire staff to handle unlimited prior authorizations," framing it as a solvable business problem.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Systemic cost-shifting from payers to providers, the scale of the administrative burden across the entire profession, and the lack of incentive for providers to adopt new ePA technology without compensation.
specificPoints: They provide data to quantify the national scope of the problem (e.g., the AMA's survey finding that PA consumes "almost two business days" of practice time weekly). They frame the issue as an "unfunded mandate" and connect the financial burden directly to the quality of patient care.
A mix of direct compensation calls (TMA) and shared savings models (AAP), with a strong emphasis on measuring and reporting the time burden (AMA).

The Texas Medical Association notes that existing billing codes for professional services do not account for the "excessive time spent on prior authorizations," highlighting a gap in the current reimbursement structure.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of provider incentive to adopt and use new ePA technologies.
specificPoints: They view the issue through a technology adoption and workflow lens. They uniquely articulate that ePA proposals "essentially ask providers to perform payment-related work as part of the clinical treatment workflows," highlighting a fundamental conflict in the proposed process.
Suggests a softer, incentive-based approach, such as an "extra MIPS incentive," to encourage provider participation.
Their observation that providers "want to treat patients, not be intermediaries" succinctly captures the role conflict imposed by uncompensated PA work.

noteworthyInsights.insight: NY Urology Specialists offers a sophisticated economic analysis, arguing the "asymmetric financial incentive" for payers to overuse PA will only worsen with automation unless the costs are "internalized" to the health plans through provider compensation.

Allscripts points out that new ePA workflows are "essentially asking providers to perform payment-related work as part of the clinical treatment workflows," a critical insight into how these mandates fundamentally alter the nature of a provider's job.

The AMA provides striking data from its 2021 physician survey, noting the weekly PA workload consumes "almost two business days" of physician and staff time, grounding the abstract problem in concrete, quantifiable terms.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A significant number of comments come from cancer centers and oncology associations (Moffitt, ASTRO, ADCC, Dana-Farber). This suggests the burden of PA is particularly acute in specialties that rely on complex, expensive, and time-sensitive treatments where delays and administrative hurdles have severe consequences.

The perspective from a smaller specialty practice (NY Urology) highlights that the financial burden of uncompensated PA work is a critical threat to the viability of non-hospital-based providers, potentially driving further consolidation in healthcare.

keyQuotations.quote: [PA work is] an uncompensated financial burden on healthcare practices, as CPT codes do not account for its cost.
To be adopted, prior authorization cannot be a money-losing activity for healthcare providers. It cannot be a 'jackpot' to health plans.
The concepts embedded in this RFI are essentially asking providers to perform payment-related work as part of the clinical treatment workflows.
We believe that the time and money spent by clinics absorbing PA costs into their overhead could be used to assist patients.
...providers developing a treatment plan should not be requested to expend unreimbursed time and resources to educate external providers that payers mandate for care delivery.
If PA savings are not shared with providers, there will be no incentive for them to perform the additional work associated with ePA.
Our members often spend significant amounts of uncompensated time on the phone trying to ensure their patient receives needed care.
It becomes difficult to justify additional clinical resources when services were clinically reviewed and authorized pre-service.

8.3.themeDescription: Need for Seamless Workflow Integration and Usability

sections.executiveSummary: There is an overwhelming consensus among all stakeholders that for electronic prior authorization (ePA) to succeed, it must be seamlessly integrated into provider EHR workflows, automating data entry and eliminating the need for separate portals and manual processes. Commenters universally warn that any solution that adds clicks, fragments workflows, or increases administrative burden will be abandoned, rendering the entire effort a failure. The central debate is not if integration should happen, but how: through native EHR functionality versus a more open ecosystem of third-party apps, and how to design systems that support the reality of staff-delegated workflows, not just physician-centric tasks.

consensusPoints.text: Seamless EHR integration is non-negotiable. Solutions requiring users to leave the EHR are considered a primary cause of current inefficiencies and a barrier to adoption.

Proprietary payer portals are a major source of burden and must be eliminated. A strong majority of providers, their associations, and even some payers identified the proliferation of separate, non-standardized payer portals as a key driver of inefficiency, data re-entry, and workflow fragmentation.

Poorly designed systems will be abandoned. An overwhelming number of commenters from every group warned that usability is the single most critical factor for adoption. If a new ePA system is not demonstrably faster, easier, and less burdensome than current methods (including fax and phone), providers will not use it.

Automation of data collection is essential. A vast majority of commenters insisted that ePA systems must automatically pull necessary data from the EHR. Manual data re-entry, transcribing information, or answering questions for which the payer already has the data were cited as unacceptable burdens.

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Best Achieve Workflow Integration
areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on whether to achieve integration through native EHR functionality developed by vendors or via an open app ecosystem allowing for third-party solutions.

positions.label: Native EHR Functionality
positions.stance: Integrate ePA capabilities as a core, seamless part of the certified EHR system itself.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by large health systems and some EHR vendors who favor a unified, "soup-to-nuts" approach.
keyArguments: Ensures a consistent user experience, avoids fragmentation, leverages existing EHR privacy/security infrastructure, and is more likely to be fully integrated into existing workflows without disruption.

positions.label: Open App Ecosystem
positions.stance: Certify EHRs to support open APIs that allow providers to choose and plug in "best-of-breed" third-party ePA applications.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by specialized tech vendors, some provider groups wanting choice, and consultants.
keyArguments: Fosters innovation, allows for more specialized and nimble solutions than what large EHR vendors might develop, and gives providers flexibility to choose tools that best fit their specific workflows.

areasOfDebate.topic: Who is the Primary User: Clinician or Staff?
areasOfDebate.description: The debate focuses on whether ePA systems should be designed primarily for real-time use by clinicians at the point of care, or for the more common delegated workflow where administrative staff manage the process.

positions.label: Clinician-Centric Design
positions.stance: Standards and workflows should assume the ordering clinician is the primary user, enabling real-time decisions at the point of care.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in some standards development (Da Vinci IGs); supported by some payers and tech visionaries.
keyArguments: Allows for meaningful conversations with patients about care options and costs in real-time; can prevent downstream work by resolving PA needs immediately.

positions.label: Staff-Delegated Workflow
positions.stance: Systems must be designed to support a common reality where administrative staff handle most of the PA process on behalf of clinicians.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of providers, professional associations, and some tech vendors.
keyArguments: Forcing clinicians to manage PA adds unacceptable burden and is an inefficient use of their time; reality of practice is that these are delegated tasks requiring features like work queues, status dashboards, and the ability to save/pass off tasks.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden ("clicks," data re-entry, phone calls); avoiding fragmented workflows (multiple portals); preventing solutions from adding new burdens that worsen burnout.
specificPoints: They provide vivid, ground-level details of workflow chaos, such as scanning faxes to Docusign for telehealth as mentioned by UCSF, staff spending half their time just checking status updates as noted by MCG Health, and the need for an "ON/OFF switch" for poorly designed features as raised by Dr. Shteynshlyuger.
A single, standardized, automated process inside the EHR; robust work-queue and delegation functionality for staff; auto-population of all known data.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The technical complexity and cost of implementing vague or conflicting standards; the risk of building certified features that providers refuse to use; the challenge of accommodating highly variable provider workflows and IT ecosystems.
specificPoints: They focus on the practical challenges of development, warning that some proposed standards could be "disruptive to patient care" (Epic) or represent an "enormous shift in workflow" that is unrealistic (Allscripts). They highlight that compiling data automatically might still require a manual review/signature step, which negates the benefit (EHRA).
Clear, non-disruptive, and well-tested standards; flexibility in certification to allow for different provider environments; phased approaches to implementation.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring they receive accurate and complete documentation; driving provider adoption of electronic methods to reduce their own internal administrative costs.
specificPoints: They see EHR integration not just as a benefit for providers, but as a critical tool to get providers to use electronic channels. They strongly advocate for two-way electronic communication to eliminate paper and phone follow-up.
Incentivize EHR vendors to create user-friendly solutions as suggested by AHIP; mandate robust, EHR-integrated workflows to ensure adoption as suggested by Anthem.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Aggregating the voice of their members (overwhelmingly providers) about burden, care delays, and burnout; ensuring policy reflects real-world practice needs.
specificPoints: They bring a systemic, human-factors view, emphasizing the need for user-centered design (AMIA) and real-world testing before mandates (AHIMA). Many cite survey data on the scale of the problem.
Certification criteria must include usability metrics; solutions must be "soup-to-nuts" and not partial; all payers must be required to participate to avoid parallel, fragmented workflows.

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Suncoast RHIO, from its experience in a CMS pilot, points out that the 'human factor' is the most overlooked and critical component of interoperability, as organizations are focused on meeting requirements, not optimizing technology.

Point-of-Care Partners notes that a major adoption barrier is that providers simply do not trust the accuracy of payer-provided data due to historical failures, making them hesitant to rely on automated systems.

UCSF CDHI provides a compelling, modern example: paper-based workflows are 'untenable in telehealth, where providers are not in physical clinics' near a fax machine, making truly electronic solutions a necessity.

Mass General Brigham offers a direct comparison, praising the 'Tufts program' as effective and meeting the 'spirit and intent' of PA, while condemning the Harvard Pilgrim Health Care process as 'far too complicated, inflexible, and requires too much manual intervention.' This suggests workable models exist.

Aneesh Chopra & Brendan Keeler suggest a pragmatic path forward by illustrating how one could 'MacGyver' an effective ePA solution by repurposing existing, deployed functionalities rather than building an entirely new, complex system from scratch.

emergingPatterns.pattern: A clear pattern emerges where frontline clinicians and their associations focus on the messy reality of daily work (delegation, interruptions, multiple systems), while technology-focused commenters (SDOs, some vendors) focus on the elegant potential of specific standards (like CDS Hooks), sometimes understating the immense challenge of fitting that ideal into the real-world chaos.

Multiple commenters, including AMIA, MEDITECH, and Voluware, identify the danger of partial implementation. A system that works for only some payers, some services, or some steps in the process is not a partial victory; it is a net-negative that increases overall workflow complexity by forcing providers to manage multiple parallel systems.

Several commenters, including UCSF CDHI and Troy Kaji, point to the slow but ultimately successful integration of e-prescribing as a model for how ePA should be implemented: deeply integrated, increasingly usable over time, and becoming the default standard.

keyQuotations.quote: [E]Prior auth, 'as currently written, is only marginally better than existing prior authorization approaches,' and 'unless payer rules are made transparent and actionable earlier in the process, it merely enables an electronic version of 'guess what I’m thinking.'
Providers are often asked to create logins for payer or DME vendor web portals, which is untenable across dozens or hundreds of payers.

External applications and portals that interface with existing Health IT systems may technically meet e-PA capability requirements but will do little to reduce provider burdens and do not meet the intent of needed reforms.
Currently, the manual process creates lag time, so having ePA readily available within the EHR would streamline user workflow.
If capabilities... are not seamless and cause additional burden due to the need for re-entry or correction of inputted data, then they will likely be abandoned.
Functionality offered by EHRs must be better than, and not equal to, the currently utilized systems for prior authorization, such as online portals and 1-800 lines.
If the PAS API experience is too difficult or costly, providers won’t do it and the IT companies just built and went through costly certifications for no reason.
Poorly usable solutions are burdensome to train and burdensome to use, while great usability promotes adoption and decreases burden.

The prior authorization process is going to have to take into account that current workflows and timing may not directly translate into a fully digital (no manual intervention) process; not a complete replacement of process.
ONC must ensure technology allows 'pass-offs' between physicians and staff (e.g., saving/delegating PAs) to avoid inadvertently increasing physician administrative tasks.

[We] express concern that the Da Vinci IGs assume the clinician themselves will be at the center of the prior authorization process, whereas in most practice settings, it is the provider’s staff that collect and prepare information and submit authorization requests.

A problematic situation would arise if not all payers participate in an integrated system... An estimated 75% of providers who use electronic prior authorization also use other prior authorization methods. These two different systems require different workflows, which create additional time constraints and inefficiencies when used concurrently.

Some practices have multiple employees to perform just this function – for example, to print each authorization submission, keep them in a stack, log into each provider portal every day, and manually check the status of each.
Electronic health records (EHRs) can and should be optimized using as few “clicks” as possible...

We strongly recommend EHRs adopt a referral/PA module approach with functionality to support intake, update, sending, and historical record keeping... this is essential workflow, even if outside DaVinci IG transaction specs.
Creating additional forms or templates to complete within an electronic PA process simply shifts the burden from one portal to another without making any real difference.
A system that allows a provider to submit information should also allow the payer to request additional information or documentation through the same system.
Actionable information transparency embedded in the ordering process is respectful of clinicians and patients – and is in keeping with all other aspects of modern online life.

Only in this way will patients also see the maximum benefit of a PA process embedded in a provider’s workflow, fully automated – the ability to have a meaningful conversation with their provider at the point of care about their options...

8.4.themeDescription: Opposition to Shifting Administrative Burden to Clinicians

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on this theme is unified and emphatic: any electronic prior authorization solution must be designed to protect clinicians' time and not shift administrative burdens into the clinical workflow. Commenters from nearly every stakeholder group view PA as a payment-related function that must be firewalled from the clinical encounter to prevent exacerbating a full-blown physician burnout crisis. The central tension exists between proposals for deep, in-workflow automation (e.g., the Da Vinci DTR model), which providers fear will be disruptive and burdensome, and the dominant recommendation to mandate flexible, delegable systems that allow PA tasks to be handled separately by administrative staff.

consensusPoints.text: An overwhelming majority of commenters, particularly provider organizations and clinicians, fundamentally believe that prior authorization (PA) is a payment-related administrative task that should be firewalled from the clinical decision-making process and direct patient care. RadNet states unequivocally, "Prior-authorization is an administrative, not clinical, function and we advise against changes that affect clinical operations." New York Urology Specialists argues, "Prior authorization is a payment-related function that should not interfere with direct patient care or clinical decision-making during the patient encounter." The Texas Medical Association concurs, stating "PA is a payment-related function that should not interfere with direct patient care."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: This view is held by nearly all provider-side commenters, including individual clinicians, health systems, and professional societies.

consensusPoints.text: There is universal agreement across all stakeholder types—including providers, vendors, and patient advocates—that any new electronic prior authorization (ePA) system must not, under any circumstances, shift administrative tasks to clinicians or increase their workload. This is framed as a critical backstop against exacerbating the current physician burnout crisis. The American Medical Association (AMA) insists, "ePA should not shift administrative burden from office staff to physicians or other clinicians." Epic Systems Corporation warns, "ONC must ensure that adopted standards for PA in certification do not shift additional documentation burden to clinicians." Allina Health voices concern that poorly executed ePA could lead to the "unintentional outcome, adding more burden to patients and care teams."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: This point is made explicitly or implicitly by virtually every commenter (at least 30 of the 35 provided extracts).

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters, led by professional organizations and IT vendors, stress that ePA systems must be designed to allow clinicians to delegate PA-related tasks to administrative or support staff. This is seen as the primary mechanism for protecting clinician time. The AMA states ONC must ensure technology allows "pass-offs' between physicians and staff (e.g., saving/delegating PAs)." AHIMA recommends that capabilities "consider the need for delegation of prior authorization related activities by a provider to back-office staff." Epic notes that many components of PA are already "handled by support staff" and that centralized teams "minimize clinician burden."
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Supported by major professional organizations (AMA, AHIMA, ACP) and health IT vendors (Epic, Cerner).

positions.label: Separate Workflows
positions.stance: Prioritize protecting the clinical encounter by keeping PA tasks separate and handled by administrative staff.
positions.supportLevel: The vast majority of healthcare providers, professional organizations, and several major IT vendors (Epic, Allscripts, Cerner).
keyArguments: In-workflow interruptions disrupt patient care, undermine clinical judgment, and inappropriately shift payment functions to clinicians. Many practices already have efficient, centralized, non-clinical teams for PA, and a clinician-centric model would be a step backward.

positions.label: Integrated Workflows
positions.stance: Use in-workflow tools like CDS hooks to prompt clinicians at the point of care to streamline data collection and potentially avoid denials.
positions.supportLevel: A minority view, primarily held by some utilization management and IT solution providers.
keyArguments: Prompting the clinician at the "order-sign" moment can ensure correct data is captured upfront, prevent downstream rework, and provide educational content, ultimately preserving provider productivity.

positions.label: Ordering Physician
positions.stance: The ordering physician, who has the clinical relationship and rationale, should be the primary driver of the PA process.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily articulated by ancillary service providers.
keyArguments: The clinical justification and necessary records originate with the ordering prescriber. Placing the burden elsewhere is an inefficient and unfair shift of responsibility.

positions.label: Rendering Provider
positions.stance: The rendering provider is often unfairly tasked with chasing down documentation that should have been handled by the ordering clinician.
positions.supportLevel: Articulated by rendering providers.
keyArguments: It is unfair for rendering providers (e.g., imaging centers) to bear the onus of obtaining medical necessity documentation that the ordering clinician should have secured before the referral was ever made.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Physician burnout, protecting the sanctity of the patient-physician relationship, preventing uncompensated administrative work.
specificPoints: They provide the most detailed critique of how specific ePA models (like Da Vinci DTR) could disrupt clinical workflows and directly contradict the goal of reducing burden. The ACP uniquely highlights that while ePA might help a practice overall, it may not reduce the physician's specific burden, which is the most critical factor.

Proposed solutions include mandating delegable workflows, separating administrative and clinical functions, minimizing data requirements ('clicks'), and establishing guardrails against payer interference in medical decision-making.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workforce burnout, operational inefficiency, diverting staff resources from patient care to administrative tasks.
specificPoints: They quantify the burden in terms of staff time and resources, emphasizing how PA "pull[s] our providers away from the bedsides". They connect administrative inefficiencies directly to the quality and availability of patient care.
Proposed solutions include streamlining and automating processes to allow providers to "return time saved to focusing on patient care needs".

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: The technical feasibility of proposals, avoiding the creation of disruptive or rigid workflows, ensuring standards are implementable.
specificPoints: They offer a crucial reality check, with several (notably Epic and Allscripts) pointing out that most PA is already handled by support staff, making a new 'clinician-centric' ePA model a dangerous step backward. Cerner provides a clear framework for separating clinical and administrative functions in system design.

Proposed solutions include designing systems with clear separation of clinical and administrative tasks, allowing for asynchronous and delegated workflows, and avoiding models like Da Vinci DTR that interrupt clinicians.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Feasibility of distributing logic (CQL), achieving efficiency through automation.
specificPoints: They are more likely to see in-workflow prompts as a tool for efficiency. Cigna envisions workflows "that do not include the provider at all." HealthHelp sees the 'order-sign' hook as a way to preserve provider productivity, a view in direct tension with providers.
Proposed solutions include focusing on end-to-end automation and the use of CDS at the point of care to guide choices and capture data.

noteworthyInsights.insight: New York Urology Specialists provides a uniquely powerful and practical recommendation: "Healthcare providers must have the ability to 'DISABLE' ePA functionality in their EHR to prevent administrative burdens from interfering with clinical workflows." This highlights the depth of mistrust and the desire for ultimate control to protect patient care.

Allscripts and Epic deliver a critical insight: since current PA is largely handled by support staff, designing a new ePA system centered on the clinician is not an improvement, but a regression that would create new burden for doctors.

Power to the Patients, a patient advocacy group, astutely notes a potential downside: making PA information readily available to patients could lead to more questions and confusion, "entail[ing] more laptop time for care providers" and compromising direct care time.

1upHealth provides a crucial reality check on idealized workflows, pointing out that clinicians are often not "sitting real-time with the patient" when PA issues arise and may be responding to messages after hours, making in-workflow prompts impractical and burdensome.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The burden of PA is not uniform. The American College of Physicians points out that in small practices lacking dedicated administrative staff, the PA burden falls directly on physicians, making the need to protect their time even more acute in these settings.

A clear pattern emerges of healthcare providers, professional societies, and major EHR vendors (like Epic and Allscripts) aligning on the need for separate, delegable, back-office workflows. This stands in contrast to a vision articulated by some payer-aligned and UM vendors that favors more deeply integrated, in-workflow automation at the point of care.

A subtle tension is visible between ordering clinicians and rendering/ancillary providers (RadNet, AAHomecare). Both agree the burden is misplaced, but they highlight how it can be unfairly shifted between them, indicating that a successful ePA solution must clarify roles and responsibilities along the entire care and referral chain.

keyQuotations.quote: [P]hysician burden has spiraled beyond crisis levels. Reducing physician burden is among the College’s highest priorities.
Prior-authorization is an administrative, not clinical, function and we advise against changes that affect clinical operations.
The DTR IG’s approach...would also shift documentation requirements for prior authorization that previously could have been handled by support staff to clinicians.
Current PA activities are managed by support staff, not providers, so the premise of the question is incorrect. Providers want to treat patients, not be intermediaries.
These requirements pull our providers away from the bedsides and from the lifesaving care they have been trained to give.

ONC's vision for ePA embedded into EHR workflow 'forcing doctors to complete PA forms during ordering'...will require physicians to spend additional uncompensated time, increasing administrative burden, lowering productivity, increasing wait times, and limiting care access.

This integration would be useful to clinicians only to the extent that the standards do not require: Additional input from clinicians. Additional “clicks” to navigate. Additional documentation beyond what is already captured in the EHR.

if electronic prior authorization creates efficiencies but then causes time constraints (e.g., requiring providers to address many in-basket messages about denials/approvals), efficiency may be lost, and patient care could suffer.
The potential volume of computer-generated and DTR-transmitted PA questions sent to providers may lead to significant provider overload, similar to coding and quality measure documentation burdens.
Shifting the center of care back to the patient-provider relationship and away from payer-imposed administrative burdens should be a paramount consideration in any policy ONC ultimately adopts.
By allowing payers to automate query into CEHRT or to insert information at the point of care (e.g., CDSHooks card), they are able to influence physicians’ medical decision-making process.
Receiving documentation and requests automatically would be huge for provider burnout.

8.5.themeDescription: Disproportionate Impact on Small and Rural Practices

sections.executiveSummary: There is overwhelming consensus from all stakeholder groups—including providers, payers, and IT vendors—that any electronic prior authorization (ePA) mandate will disproportionately burden small, solo, and rural practices. These entities lack the financial resources, dedicated IT staff, and technical infrastructure to adopt and implement new ePA technologies, threatening their viability and potentially reducing access to care in vulnerable communities. The central tension is between the goal of universal ePA adoption and the risk of forcing under-resourced practices to close. Consequently, the most dominant recommendation is for the federal government to provide significant financial incentives and technical assistance, modeled on past programs, to ensure an equitable transition.

consensusPoints.text: Small, rural, and under-resourced practices face a prohibitive financial and operational burden. This is the single most consistent point of agreement, with nearly all commenters stating that small, solo, rural, or otherwise under-resourced practices will be disproportionately harmed by the costs and complexity of ePA implementation. The American College of Physicians (ACP) states they are the least likely to afford implementation expenses, a point echoed by payer organization AHIP, which notes migration costs may be prohibitive. The South Carolina Medical Association (SCMA) warns that onerous policies could push physicians out of rural areas and small practices.

exceptions.text: While large health systems like Cleveland Clinic and UnityPoint Health acknowledge this burden on smaller providers, they note their own greater capacity to adopt the changes, highlighting the growing resource gap.

consensusPoints.text: Financial and technical support from the federal government is essential for equitable adoption. This is the most common recommendation, with a strong majority of commenters explicitly calling for federal incentives, funding, and/or technical assistance to help smaller practices. For instance, the Association for Clinical Oncology (ASCO) strongly supports incentives, Anthem recommends that ONC and CMS develop provider incentives mirroring past programs, and the American Association of Nurse Practitioners (AANP) calls for resources to assist smaller practices without undue cost.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (at least 15)

consensusPoints.text: Real-world testing of new standards must include a diverse range of practice settings. A significant number of commenters, particularly professional associations, insist that testing must move beyond large, well-resourced health systems to be considered valid. The American Medical Association (AMA) states more testing is needed in practices of all sizes and specialties, the Regulatory Relief Coalition (RRC) recommends testing practicability in small practice settings, and the Medical Group Management Association (MGMA) adds that testing should include the cost of implementation in different practice settings.

areasOfDebate.description: The debate centers on the best method to ensure small practices can access ePA technology: mandating comprehensive features in all certified EHRs versus providing flexibility in enforcement and timelines.

positions.label: Strict Certification
positions.stance: Mandate high-level ePA capabilities in all base Certified EHRs to ensure even off-the-shelf products are functional for small practices without costly add-ons or custom development.
positions.supportLevel: Primarily physician advocacy groups.
keyArguments: This approach prevents vendors from selling fragmented, expensive modules
it ensures that a 'certified' product is a 'complete' product
it levels the playing field for small practices that lack IT staff for implementation.

positions.label: Flexible Enforcement
positions.stance: Implement phased-in enforcement, penalties, exemptions, or measure re-weighting to give smaller practices more time and flexibility to comply.
positions.supportLevel: Large health systems, specialty societies.
keyArguments: Acknowledges the reality that smaller entities cannot adopt technology at the same pace as large systems
prevents mandates from becoming punitive and forcing practice closures.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial viability of small/solo/rural practices; increased clinician burnout when administrative work falls to physicians; exacerbation of health disparities; patient access to care being threatened.
specificPoints: They frequently connect the IT burden to the historical context of prior incentive programs (like Meaningful Use) that excluded many non-physician or specialty providers, leaving them further behind technologically, a point made by APTA. They also provide a sharp critique of standards-development bodies being dominated by large hospital systems, as noted by the RRC.

The American College of Physicians highlights that in small practices, PA work falls directly on the physician, fueling burnout and provider shortages in underserved areas. The South Carolina Medical Association warns of a physician exodus from rural areas if mandates are too onerous.
There is a nearly universal call for federal financial incentives and technical support; mandating inclusive real-world testing; and requiring robust capabilities in base EHR certification.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: For small practices, the immediate financial viability and ability to function, as highlighted by NY Urology. For large systems, the impact on their referral networks and the ecosystem-wide disruption when community partners are unable to keep up, as noted by UCSF.
specificPoints: Large systems like Cleveland Clinic and Providence acknowledge their own resource advantages while advocating for solutions for their smaller counterparts, recognizing the interconnectedness of the healthcare system.

UCSF provides a stark example of community gastroenterology practices being unable to manage complex patients due to administrative burden, forcing those patients to travel long distances for care at academic centers.
Phased-in enforcement is a proposed solution from groups like UnityPoint Health.

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Recognizing that prohibitive migration costs for small practices are a barrier to the wide-scale adoption that would benefit the entire system.
specificPoints: While acknowledging the problem, payers uniformly position the solution as a federal responsibility. They advocate for government-funded provider incentives rather than direct payer subsidies or reduced technology costs.
Anthem recommends federal provider incentives, potentially modeled after the Meaningful Use program, to encourage adoption. AHIP echoes that migration costs may be prohibitive for small practices.

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Health IT Vendors, Consultants & Standards Bodies
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring standards are adoptable across the entire market, not just by large, sophisticated players; avoiding the 'disenfranchisement' of providers with less capable HIT; overcoming low adoption in specific sectors like Medicaid.
specificPoints: These commenters provide a market and technical reality check. Cerner focuses on varying product abilities, while OCHIN provides a crucial view from the safety-net sector, emphasizing that these communities suffer from chronic underinvestment. WEDI recalls past successful models of federal support like Regional Extension Centers.

Proposed solutions include federal funding for safety-net provider engagement in testing (OCHIN), incentives and education, especially for Medicaid users (Point-of-Care Partners), and the revival of technical assistance programs (WEDI).

noteworthyInsights.insight: UCSF's comment that "many community gastroenterology practices can't care for complex IBD patients due to the administrative burden, forcing patients to travel long distances to university centers" is a powerful, specific example of how small practice burdens directly shift care, increase patient travel, and create access bottlenecks at academic centers.

The Regulatory Relief Coalition (RRC) points out that the provider members of the HL7 Da Vinci Project—a key group developing these standards—are all "large hospitals and health care systems that might be expected to have substantial resources." This insight challenges the assumption that the current standards development process adequately represents the needs of small, independent practices.

The American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) provides a crucial historical lesson, noting that PTs were excluded from Meaningful Use incentives. This has had lasting repercussions, leaving them with less capable EHRs and creating referral barriers, demonstrating how policy decisions about technology funding can have long-term, negative consequences on care coordination.

WEDI's reminder of the Regional Extension Centers (RECs) program provides a concrete, previously successful federal model for delivering the kind of technical assistance that nearly all commenters agree is necessary for small and rural practices.

emergingPatterns.pattern: The Urban/Rural & Community/Academic Divide: There is a clear pattern where administrative burdens on smaller community and rural practices (UCSF, SCMA) risk creating a 'brain drain' of both patients and physicians towards better-resourced urban and academic centers, straining those centers and abandoning local communities.

Payer-Provider Consensus on the Federal Role: A striking pattern is the alignment between payers (Anthem, AHIP) and provider groups (AMA, AAP, etc.) on two key points: 1) the problem of disproportionate burden on small practices is real, and 2) the solution lies in federal incentives for providers. This shared position suggests a viable policy path with broad support.

The Clinician vs. Staffing Chasm: Multiple commenters (ACP, ACC) draw a sharp distinction between large practices with dedicated IT and administrative staff who handle PA, and small practices where this work falls directly onto the physician or nurse practitioner. This pattern directly links the technology and resource issue to the national crisis of clinician burnout.

Legacy Systems as an Anchor: The National Association for the Support of Long Term Care (NASL) highlights that for many providers (especially in LTPAC), the issue is not just adopting new technology but the difficulty and cost of integrating with or replacing legacy systems that were never designed for this level of interoperability, a problem exacerbated by a lack of previous incentive funding.

keyQuotations.quote: [U]nder-resourced health systems and practices currently suffer the most in terms of prior authorization burden. They are also the least likely to be able to afford the implementation expenses of electronic prior authorization.
Many community gastroenterology practices can't care for complex IBD patients due to the administrative burden, forcing patients to travel long distances to university centers.

In small practices that are less likely to have support staff, prior authorization work falls directly on the physician and overburdens them. This leads to shortages of physicians in rural and economically depressed areas.
With fewer caregivers per capita and greater distances to care in rural areas, a system that traps those caregivers in endless stretches of administrative busywork is costly to patients.

Onerous policies for electronic prior authorization could push physicians out of rural areas and small practices where a lack of broadband and the need for low overhead precludes electronic record keeping.

ONC should take care not to “disenfranchise” smaller providers including physician practices, freestanding clinics and even post-acute care settings or home health agencies that may have less capable HIT portfolios...

For e-PA to be effective and equitable in all clinical settings, ONC must require the highest levels of functional capabilities during the certification process and should not rely on staff-level implementation.
